question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain. She reports that the pain is primarily in her fingers and wrists and is often accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She has also noticed some swelling in these joints and difficulty in performing daily tasks such as opening jars. Her medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. She is currently taking over-the-counter ibuprofen with minimal relief. On physical examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic due to worsening skin lesions and joint pain. She describes the lesions as red, scaly patches that started on her elbows and knees, and have slowly spread to other parts of her body over the past 5 years. She also reports stiffness and swelling in several fingers on her right hand that is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. On examination, there are well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales on her elbows, knees, and scalp. Her right index and middle fingers are swollen with tenderness over the distal interphalangeal joints. A complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty walking for the last 3 weeks. She reports tripping over her left foot and has noticed weakness in her left leg, particularly when lifting the front part of her foot while walking. She denies numbness or tingling in the legs but mentions occasional knee pain and morning stiffness that lasts for about 30 minutes. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, she has a foot drop on the left side and weakness of the left ankle dorsiflexors. There is no swelling or tenderness in the joints. Her deep tendon reflexes are 2+ bilaterally and symmetric. Sensory examination is intact. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her current symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty walking. She reports frequent tripping and has noticed weakness in her right foot. There is no pain associated with the weakness, but she occasionally experiences pins and needles sensations in her right leg. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot. The patellar and Achilles reflexes are normal. There is no joint swelling, redness, or warmth. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 25-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her knees and ankles that started a week ago and seem to move from one joint to another. She mentions having a sore throat about two weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. She also reports fatigue and mild fever. Physical examination reveals erythema and tenderness in the affected joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated antistreptolysin O titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 1-week history of a purplish rash on her legs and blood in her urine. She also reports a low-grade fever and joint pain in her knees and ankles. She denies any recent upper respiratory infections or new medications. Her medical history is significant for occasional migraines, for which she takes sumatriptan as needed. Her vital signs include a temperature of 100.2°F (37.9°C), blood pressure of 125/80 mmHg, pulse of 76/min, and respirations of 16/min. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower extremities and mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory studies show hematuria on urinalysis and normal platelet count. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with right shoulder pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. She states that the pain is most severe when she tries to lift her arm above her head and describes difficulty reaching for objects on high shelves. She denies any trauma or injury to the shoulder. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, well controlled on levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is limited active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is mild tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder, but no swelling or warmth. Neurological examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a normal white blood cell count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An X-ray of the shoulder reveals mild subacromial space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping for the past three months. She describes the pain as a deep aching sensation, primarily in her shoulders, neck, and back. She reports waking up feeling unrefreshed despite a full night's sleep and experiencing increased sensitivity to touch. Her medical history is significant for irritable bowel syndrome, but she denies any recent infections or new medications. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals multiple tender points in the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and medial knee. There is no swelling, erythema, or synovitis of the joints. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibody tests are also negative. What is the most likely diagnosis given her clinical presentation?

A 50-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of skin tightening and discoloration of her fingers. She reports that the tips of her fingers turn white and blue intermittently, especially in cold temperatures, which is followed by redness upon warming. She also notes that the skin on her fingers feels tight and shiny. On further questioning, she mentions occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, her fingers show taut skin with slight puffiness, and there are areas of hypopigmentation and hyperpigmentation. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test with a centromere pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent joint pain and skin changes. She reports the joint pain started about 6 months ago and has been progressively worsening. The pain is predominantly in her fingers and toes, with occasional swelling and a feeling of stiffness in the morning that improves throughout the day. She also notes red, scaly skin lesions on her elbows and knees that have appeared over the past year. She denies fever, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension, controlled with medication. Family history reveals that her father has psoriasis. On examination, there are multiple erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knees. Her fingers show a 'sausage-like' swelling and nail pitting is observed. Elevated inflammatory markers are noted on laboratory workup. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, headaches, and a new onset of elevated blood pressure over the last 3 months. She reports swelling in her lower extremities and occasional joint pain. She has a history of mild depression and uses sertraline. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 160/100 mm Hg and 1+ pitting edema in the lower extremities. Her laboratory tests show a serum creatinine of 2.0 mg/dL and urinalysis reveals 1+ proteinuria and hematuria. Her ESR is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her renal findings?

options.A: Chronic kidney disease due to hypertension

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain over the past two weeks. She reports fatigue and mild fever but denies any recent infections, weight loss, or night sweats. Her medical history is notable for a resolved case of strep throat three months ago. She takes no regular medications and has no known allergies. On examination, she has tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior aspect of her legs and mild swelling in her knees but no synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 48 mm/hr, and negative rheumatoid factor. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful oral and genital ulcers that have been recurring for the past 6 months. She describes the oral ulcers as shallow and present on the inner lips and tongue, while the genital ulcers are deeper and located on the vulva. She reports joint pain in her knees and ankles without significant swelling, and states that she feels fatigued most days. She denies gastrointestinal symptoms, ocular issues, or recent travel. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals multiple aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity and a few well-defined ulcers on the vulva. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and slightly elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, weight gain, and feeling cold all the time. She mentions experiencing joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings, which lasts about 30 minutes. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals dry skin and coarse hair. A thyroid panel is ordered, revealing elevated TSH levels and decreased free T4 levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty using her hands for daily activities. She has no significant past medical history and takes occasional ibuprofen for pain relief. Her family history is notable for lupus in her mother. On examination, she has swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally, with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 58 mm/h and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 12 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 32-year-old female, who is 28 weeks pregnant, presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back and pelvic pain that has worsened over the past month. She describes the pain as a deep ache that is more pronounced with prolonged standing or walking and is relieved by rest and a warm bath. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her medical history is unremarkable, and this is her first pregnancy. Physical examination shows a well-nourished woman with a normal gait and tenderness to palpation over the sacroiliac joints. Her vital signs are within normal limits. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 66-year-old woman comes to the physician because of persistent bone pain and discomfort in her legs and hips for the past 3 months. She noticed that the pain worsens with activity and slightly improves with rest. She has been postmenopausal for 15 years and has a history of smoking one pack of cigarettes daily for 40 years. Her medications include calcium supplements and vitamin D. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the tibiae and pelvis. Laboratory studies show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, while other liver function tests and calcium levels are within normal limits. X-rays of the affected areas show cortical thickening and enlargement of the bone. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.G: CT scan of the affected limbs

question: A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past 2 months. She describes the sensation as “pins and needles” and notes that it worsens at night. She has difficulty buttoning her clothes and occasionally drops objects. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, which is poorly controlled, and hypertension. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin A1c of 9.2%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive proximal muscle weakness over the past three months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She also experiences generalized fatigue and mild joint pain, but denies any morning stiffness. There is no history of recent trauma or new medications. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled with lisinopril.

On physical examination, she has symmetrical muscle weakness in her shoulders and hips, but normal strength in her distal muscles. She has a mild, non-specific rash on her knuckles. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and normal inflammatory markers. An electromyography study shows a myopathic pattern.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, generalized weakness, and joint pain over the past three months. She notes that her fatigue worsens as the day progresses, and she requires frequent rest periods. She also reports occasional episodes of dizziness and shortness of breath, particularly during exertion. On further questioning, she mentions frequent nosebleeds and easy bruising, which she attributes to clumsiness. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies alcohol or drug use. The physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae on her lower extremities, and mild tenderness in her wrists and knees. Laboratory investigations show hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, leukocytes of 3,200/µL, and platelets of 75,000/µL. Peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia, and her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purpura on her lower extremities and noticeable blood in her urine. She describes feeling generally unwell with mild fatigue over the past month. She denies any recent infections or travel history. On further questioning, she reports mild joint pain in her knees and ankles, which she attributes to her recent increase in physical activity. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medication. A urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Laboratory tests reveal normal blood counts and renal function, but elevated inflammatory markers. She is afebrile with stable vital signs. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, unintentional weight loss of 15 pounds over the past 3 months, and pallor. She also notes occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees but characterizes it as mild. She denies any recent infections, travel, or changes in diet. Her menstrual history is unremarkable. On physical exam, she appears pale and her conjunctivae are pale. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) 70 fL, serum iron 20 µg/dL, ferritin 10 ng/mL, and total iron binding capacity (TIBC) 450 µg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past six months. She also reports feeling cold more often than usual, dry skin, and occasional joint pain in her hands and knees. Her medical history is significant for a distant episode of postpartum thyroiditis. On physical examination, you note a slightly puffy face and dry, coarse skin, but no synovitis or visible joint deformities. She is afebrile, her blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg, and her heart rate is 62 beats per minute. Laboratory tests show decreased free T4, elevated TSH, and positive antithyroid peroxidase antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of joint pain and swelling affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles. She recently returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States, where she spent a week camping. She reports no fever or skin rashes but mentions feeling fatigued. Her medical history is notable for seasonal allergies, and she has no known drug allergies. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.6°C (99.7°F), blood pressure of 120/80 mm Hg, pulse of 78/min, and respirations of 16/min. Physical examination reveals erythema and tenderness over the affected joints, but no deformities or nodules. Laboratory results show a leukocyte count of 9,000/mm3, ESR of 50 mm/hr, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral hip pain. The pain is particularly severe in the morning and gradually improves with movement as the day progresses. She reports intermittent episodes of low-grade fevers and significant fatigue over the past few months. She has a history of psoriasis but denies any gastrointestinal symptoms. Her medications include topical corticosteroids for her skin condition. On examination, there is limited range of motion in both hips, and tenderness is noted over the sacroiliac joints. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory results reveal: ESR 45 mm/hr, CRP 18 mg/L, and ANA is negative. An x-ray of the pelvis shows sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds. She also reports occasional joint discomfort, particularly in her wrists and knees, but has not experienced morning stiffness. Physical examination reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 11,000/mm^3 with an elevated absolute lymphocyte count. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated, and a chest X-ray is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.A: CT scan of the chest and abdomen

question:
A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with concerns about unexplained weight gain over the past 3 months, accompanied by persistent fatigue and stiffness in her hands and knees, particularly in the mornings. She also notes mild periorbital edema that seems to fluctuate throughout the day. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent changes in diet or physical activity level. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her family history includes a mother with hypothyroidism. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, heart rate is 78/min, and BMI is 28 kg/m2. There is noticeable puffiness around her eyes, and her joints are tender to palpation without significant swelling. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 6,800/mm3
TSH 8.2 µIU/mL (normal range: 0.4-4.0 µIU/mL)
Free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal range: 0.8-2.3 ng/dL)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of right shoulder pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She states that the pain is dull and constant, with occasional sharp exacerbations when reaching overhead or behind her back. She has difficulty combing her hair and fastening her bra. She denies any history of trauma. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, both well-controlled with medication. She takes no other medications and has no known drug allergies. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a restricted range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or erythema, and the neurologic examination is unremarkable. An X-ray of the right shoulder shows no acute bony abnormalities. What is the next best step in management for this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers. She also reports occasional joint pain in her knees and ankles and a recent episode of uveitis. She denies any significant weight loss, but mentions feeling more fatigued than usual. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and similar lesions in the genital area. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm her underlying condition?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of recurrent episodes of painful swelling in her left great toe. She reports these episodes occur about twice a year and tend to resolve spontaneously within a week. The current episode began two days ago after she enjoyed a seafood dinner and a few glasses of wine. She describes the pain as severe and throbbing, and notes that the toe is red and warm to the touch. She has a history of hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril, and she occasionally takes ibuprofen for the pain. Physical examination reveals an erythematous, swollen left big toe with palpable nodules along the joint. Her vital signs are stable, and there are no other joint abnormalities. Laboratory studies show an elevated serum uric acid level. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient's condition?

A 28-year-old female comes to the clinic due to a rash on her legs and joint pain for the past two weeks. She describes purplish spots on her lower legs that have not faded with time. Additionally, she has noticed blood in her urine over the last few days. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she has non-blanching purpuric lesions on her lower extremities and mild tenderness in her knees, but no swelling or warmth. Laboratory tests show normal renal function, but urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of persistent, non-painful swelling in her neck and axillary regions for the past 3 months. She also reports low-grade fevers, unintentional weight loss of 15 pounds, and night sweats over the same period. She has occasional joint stiffness in her hands that is worse in the morning but improves throughout the day. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, multiple firm, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. There are no skin rashes or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, WBC count of 8,200/mm³, and ESR of 45 mm/hr. A lymph node biopsy is performed. What is the most likely underlying condition associated with this patient's presentation?

A 27-year-old woman presents with painful, red nodules over her shins that have developed over the past two weeks. She has been experiencing joint pain, particularly in her knees and ankles, and mild fatigue, but denies any significant morning stiffness. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She recently recovered from a self-limited upper respiratory infection. On examination, the nodules are raised, tender, and erythematous. Her vital signs are stable, and there is mild tenderness in the affected joints without swelling. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers with an ESR of 65 mm/hr and CRP of 12 mg/L. Complete blood count shows mild leukocytosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of recurrent fevers and night sweats. She has noticed significant weight loss and fatigue over the same period. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent travel or infections. On examination, she has multiple non-tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory results show mild anemia, elevated ESR, and CRP levels. A chest X-ray reveals mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and a recent history of recurrent infections. She reports experiencing aching pain in her wrists and knees, along with significant morning stiffness for the past 3 months. She denies any rash or oral ulcers. On examination, she appears pale, and there is tenderness in her wrists and knees without swelling or erythema. Laboratory investigations reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, white blood cell count of 3,200/mm3, and platelet count of 95,000/mm3. Additional tests show elevated inflammatory markers and a negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, unintentional weight loss of 8 kg (17.6 lbs) over the past 3 months, and intermittent episodes of dizziness. She also reports feeling cold most of the time and experiencing generalized weakness. The patient mentions a family history of autoimmune disorders. On examination, she appears pale, and her conjunctivae are notably anemic. Laboratory results reveal a hemoglobin level of 8.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 74 fL, and low serum iron. Her peripheral blood smear shows microcytic, hypochromic red blood cells. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purplish spots on her lower legs and blood in her urine. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Medical history is significant for mild asthma for which she uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, palpable purpura are noted on the lower extremities. There is no joint swelling or tenderness. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count, but urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Serum creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of lower back pain that worsens with rest and improves with activity. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and occasional swelling of her knees and ankles. Her medical history is significant for uveitis one year ago that resolved with treatment. She denies recent trauma. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/75 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally, but no peripheral joint swelling. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and ESR. HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman is brought to the clinic by her partner due to increasing fatigue, joint pain, and recurrent bruising over the past 4 weeks. She reports significant fatigue that has worsened over this period and mentions the presence of bruises on her arms and legs without any trauma. She also experiences pain in her wrists and knees that is more pronounced in the morning. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, she appears pale and has palpable purpura on her lower extremities. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 88/min, blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal the following:

Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 28%
Leukocyte count: 2,500/mm³
Platelet count: 80,000/mm³

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with worsening bone pain over the past 6 months. She indicates that the pain is located primarily in her pelvis and lower spine and is aggravated by prolonged standing or walking. Her medical history includes hypertension controlled with medication. She is postmenopausal and takes calcium and vitamin D supplements regularly. Physical examination reveals localized tenderness over the lower spine and pelvis without joint swelling or deformity. Laboratory studies show: elevated alkaline phosphatase, normal calcium, and normal phosphate levels. A radiographic study shows thickening of the pelvic bone. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman visits the clinic due to increasing joint pain and stiffness in her hands over the past year. She reports difficulty gripping objects and notes that the shape of her fingers has changed. She has morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, and her symptoms improve with activity. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. Examination reveals swelling and deformity of the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show the following:
Rheumatoid factor: positive
Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies: positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 48 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurrent episodes of pain and swelling in her right big toe over the past year. She describes the episodes as sudden in onset, with the toe becoming red, swollen, and extremely tender, resolving over several days. She has noticed small, firm nodules on her ear and elbows. She denies fever, fatigue, or any other joint involvement. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, and she is on lisinopril. Laboratory findings reveal elevated serum uric acid levels. Joint aspiration shows negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. Which of the following is most likely related to this patient's underlying condition?

A 35-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to persistent fatigue and muscle weakness for the past 4 months. She reports weight gain despite a reduced appetite, occasional joint pain, and sensitivity to cold. Her medical history is significant for mild depression treated with sertraline. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg, pulse of 68/min, and temperature of 36.5°C (97.7°F). She has dry skin and mild periorbital edema. Laboratory tests show a TSH level of 8.7 µU/mL (reference range: 0.4–4.0 µU/mL), free T4 of 0.8 ng/dL (reference range: 0.8–1.8 ng/dL), and ESR of 22 mm/h (reference range: 0–20 mm/h). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Start sertraline at a higher dose
options.B: Perform an MRI of the pituitary gland
options.C: Check for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.E: Perform an ultrasound of the thyroid gland

question: A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of back pain and stiffness. She notes that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also reports occasional pain in her hips and shoulders. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent infections or trauma. Her family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has reduced range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show an ESR of 45 mm/hr. HLA-B27 testing is positive. Pelvic X-ray shows sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because of a 3-week history of joint pain and swelling. Her symptoms began with a sore throat that resolved on its own a few weeks ago, but she now reports pain that seems to move between her knees, wrists, and ankles. She describes the joint pain as severe and it is accompanied by redness and warmth. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medication. She is active in school sports and has no known allergies. On examination, her joints are tender to touch, and there is slight swelling in the knees and wrists. Cardiac examination reveals a faint systolic murmur at the apex. Laboratory studies show elevated anti-streptolysin O titers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that is most severe in the morning and improves with physical activity. She reports stiffness lasting over an hour after waking. She does not have significant joint pain, but occasionally experiences mild discomfort in her knees and hips. Her family history includes rheumatoid arthritis in her mother and psoriasis in her father. Physical examination reveals limited lumbar spine mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of diffuse bone pain, predominantly in her hips and lower back. She has a history of menopause at age 50 and has not taken hormone replacement therapy. She reports that the pain worsens with weight-bearing activity and has no significant morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin respectively. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the lumbar spine and pelvic girdle, with no synovitis or joint swelling noted. Laboratory studies show elevated alkaline phosphatase levels, normal calcium and phosphate levels, and normal inflammatory markers. A radiograph of the pelvis shows thickened trabeculae and cortical bone in the iliac wings. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of multiple subcutaneous nodules on her elbows and wrists over the past 3 months. She reports intermittent fever, fatigue, and unexplained weight loss of 10 pounds over the same period. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies any history of joint pain or swelling. On physical examination, numerous firm, non-tender nodules are observed on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and elbows. Laboratory evaluation reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with a normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition contributing to her presentation?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of nasal congestion, sinus pressure, and a persistent cough. She reports episodes of hemoptysis and fatigue. Recently, she has noticed swelling in her legs and decreased urine output. Her medical history includes hypertension and allergic rhinitis. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse 88/min, and respirations 18/min. Lung auscultation reveals crackles at the bases. Laboratory studies show elevated creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. A chest X-ray shows multiple bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic concerned about a 12-pound weight gain over the past 3 months, despite maintaining her usual diet and exercise routine. She also reports puffiness around her eyes, particularly noticeable in the morning, and stiffness in her knees and wrists that improves throughout the day. She denies fever, night sweats, or recent changes in medication. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine, and osteoarthritis. She denies smoking or alcohol use. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and slight joint tenderness without swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show normal renal function, and urinalysis is negative for proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in her lower extremities. She reports that the weakness has been worsening over the past 3 months, and she occasionally experiences mild joint pain and fatigue. Her medical history includes mild hypertension, treated with amlodipine. Physical examination reveals decreased strength in both legs, with normal sensation and reflexes. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, with an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. Electromyography reveals evidence of a demyelinating neuropathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.D: Lumbar puncture for cerebrospinal fluid analysis
options.E: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the brain and spine

question: A 40-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and feeling cold all the time over the past six months. She reports increasing difficulty in concentrating and episodes of depression. She denies any joint pain or swelling, dry eyes, or oral dryness. She has a history of hypertension controlled with lisinopril, and her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. Her vital signs are stable, but she appears slightly lethargic. Laboratory tests show the following: TSH 8.5 mIU/L (normal: 0.4-4.0), free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal: 0.8-1.8), ESR 20 mm/hr (normal: 0-20). What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers. She has also experienced intermittent joint pain and swelling in her knees and ankles over the past few months. She denies any significant weight loss, fever, or skin rashes. On examination, she has multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and a few erythematous nodules on her legs. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 115/75 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is most likely to be associated with this patient's symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of pain and swelling in her knees and elbows for the past 2 weeks. She describes the pain as aching and notes it is worse in the morning. She also reports a rash that appeared shortly after a recent hiking trip in an area known for ticks. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent illness. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any regular medications. Her temperature is 98.1°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination reveals erythema migrans on her right thigh and warm, swollen joints in her knees and elbows. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of fever and generalized lymphadenopathy over the past three months. She describes intermittent joint pain and significant fatigue that affects her daily activities. Physical examination reveals bilateral cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, with mild tenderness in the wrists and knees, but no visible joint swelling. Laboratory tests show anemia with hemoglobin of 10 g/dL, leukopenia with a white blood cell count of 3,000/mm3, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An ultrasound of the abdomen reveals mild splenomegaly. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.C: Order serologic testing for Epstein-Barr virus
options.H: Schedule a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis

question: A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, post-nasal drip, and recurrent sinus infections. Over the past 3 months, she has developed a dry cough and occasional hemoptysis. She also reports fatigue and has noticed blood in her urine on two occasions. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her temperature is 37.6°C (99.7°F), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, heart rate is 80/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Lung auscultation reveals crackles in the lower lobes bilaterally. Urinalysis shows hematuria and proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and skin changes. She reports that her joint pain is asymmetric, affecting her knees and fingers most prominently, and is associated with morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes. She also mentions the appearance of red, scaly patches on her elbows and knees, which have been persistent and occasionally itchy. The patient denies any significant weight loss or fever. On physical examination, dactylitis is noted in several fingers, and there are erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of her elbows. A nail examination reveals pitting. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain over the last week. She describes the joint pain as migratory and affecting her knees and ankles. She has a history of seasonal allergies but is otherwise healthy. She reports a recent sore throat but denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals multiple tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior surfaces of her legs. Her throat examination is normal. Laboratory results show a mild elevation in erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of painful, mottled discoloration of her skin, primarily affecting her legs. She also reports several episodes of spontaneous nosebleeds and occasional easy bruising. Her past medical history includes recurrent miscarriages and deep vein thrombosis treated with anticoagulants. Current medications include warfarin, with which she is compliant. Her family history is significant for early-onset cerebrovascular disease in her father. On examination, she is alert and oriented, with normal vital signs. Examination of the lower extremities reveals livedo reticularis and swelling without skin breakdown. Laboratory findings show a prolonged prothrombin time and partial thromboplastin time. A recent Doppler ultrasound of the legs shows new thromboses. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the past 3 months. She reports feeling more tired than usual, and her family has noticed she appears pale. She denies any heavy menstrual bleeding or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, well-controlled with levothyroxine. On physical exam, she appears pale, and a systolic ejection murmur is noted. Laboratory results reveal hemoglobin of 9.0 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 70 fL, and low ferritin levels. Further testing shows elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step for this patient's management?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty lifting her right foot and a noticeable change in her gait. She reports tripping frequently and has also noticed some numbness on the top of her foot. The symptoms began gradually and have progressively worsened. She has a past medical history significant for hypertension and obesity. She has no known allergies and takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is weakness of dorsiflexion and eversion of the right foot, and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the foot. Reflexes are normal, and there is no muscle wasting noted. An MRI of the lumbar spine is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient’s symptoms?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and a rash that developed over the past two weeks. She reports being bitten by a tick while hiking in a wooded area about a month ago. The rash initially appeared as a small red spot near the site of the tick bite and has since expanded into a larger, circular rash with central clearing. She also experiences fatigue and mild fever. Physical examination reveals a faint erythematous rash on her right thigh and tenderness in her knees and wrists. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic pain in both hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notices a decrease in grip strength. She also reports seeing small, firm nodules under her skin, particularly over her elbows. She has a family history of autoimmune conditions. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 72/min, respirations are 15/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty walking over the last 3 months. She describes feeling weakness in both legs, particularly when climbing stairs or standing from a seated position. She has a history of hypothyroidism, controlled with levothyroxine, and occasional mild joint pain in her hands and knees. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. On examination, her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 122/80 mmHg, and pulse is 72/min. Neurological examination shows 4/5 strength in hip flexors and knee extensors bilaterally, with intact sensation and normal deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory studies reveal normal creatine kinase levels but elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with severe back pain that started suddenly while she was bending to lift a lightweight object. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region. She has a history of osteoporosis, and her medications include alendronate and calcium supplements. She denies any recent falls or trauma. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T8 vertebra and a decreased range of motion in the thoracic spine. An X-ray of the thoracic spine shows a compression fracture at T8. Laboratory studies reveal normal calcium and phosphate levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.F: Increase calcium and vitamin D intake

question: A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lbs) over the past 2 months. She also reports having generalized weakness, occasional dizziness, and difficulty concentrating. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Her family history includes a father with coronary artery disease and a mother with hypothyroidism. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. On physical examination, her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg. She appears pale and has slightly dry skin. Cardiopulmonary examination is normal. Abdominal examination shows no hepatosplenomegaly. A complete blood count reveals a hemoglobin concentration of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 78 fL, and a ferritin level of 10 ng/mL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Colonoscopy to evaluate for gastrointestinal bleeding
options.B: Serum iron studies to assess for iron deficiency
options.C: Bone marrow biopsy to evaluate for marrow infiltration
options.D: Serum vitamin B12 levels to assess for deficiency
options.F: Thyroid function tests to rule out hypothyroidism
options.G: Erythropoietin levels to assess for renal anemia

question:
A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-week history of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. She also reports progressive swelling and tenderness in her neck and axillary regions. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or use of new medications. Her past medical history is significant only for occasional migraines. On physical examination, she appears pale and has palpable, non-tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal:
Hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 4,200/mm3
Platelet count 180,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 60 mm/h
Serum
LDH 450 U/L
Total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL
A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute onset of joint pain and swelling in her fingers, wrists, and knees. She recently returned from a two-week trip to the Caribbean. She reports feeling feverish and has experienced fatigue and a generalized rash over the past few days. She denies recent infections or similar symptoms in the past. On examination, there is bilateral swelling and tenderness of the wrists and knees. Skin examination reveals a maculopapular rash on her trunk and extremities. Laboratory tests show mild leukopenia, and elevated C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman is brought to the clinic by her family due to persistent fatigue, joint pain, and recurrent infections over the past month. She reports that the joint pain affects her wrists and knees and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She also mentions easy bruising and frequent nosebleeds. No significant medical history is noted except for mild seasonal allergies. Her family history is significant for hypothyroidism in her mother. Physical examination reveals pale conjunctiva, petechiae on her lower extremities, and tenderness in several joints. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory findings show:
Hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 2,500/mm3
Platelet count 90,000/mm3
ESR 45 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor Positive
ANA Positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of numbness and tingling in her feet and hands for the past three months. She reports that these symptoms are more pronounced in the evenings and occasionally wakes her at night. She also notes experiencing occasional burning pain in the same areas. The patient denies any notable joint pain or swelling, but mentions increasing fatigue and mild weight loss over the past few months. Her past medical history includes a long-standing diagnosis of type 1 diabetes, for which she takes insulin. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On examination, decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick is noted in a stocking-glove distribution. Deep tendon reflexes are decreased bilaterally. Laboratory results reveal a normal CBC, normal renal function, and elevated HbA1c. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-week history of joint pain and a rash on her face that worsens after sun exposure. She reports that the joint pain is mostly in her wrists and knees, and she has been experiencing significant fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, the patient appears well, but there is a prominent erythematous rash across her cheeks and nose. There is no swelling, but tenderness is noted in the wrists and knees. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of intense pain and swelling in her right big toe over the past 6 months. Each episode lasts a few days and resolves spontaneously. She notes increasing difficulty walking due to pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning. She denies any significant trauma or overuse. She has a history of hypertension, managed with hydrochlorothiazide, and hyperlipidemia, managed with atorvastatin. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, swollen, and tender with palpable subcutaneous nodules over the joint. Laboratory tests reveal serum uric acid levels of 9.5 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists that has persisted for the past 4 months. She describes the stiffness as being most pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving throughout the day. Her medical history is unremarkable except for episodic tension headaches. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness at the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild ulnar deviation noted. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is most likely associated with her condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, headaches, and new-onset hypertension. She reports increased swelling in her legs and has noticed foamy urine. Her past medical history is significant for obesity and osteoarthritis. She denies any fever, joint pain, or skin rashes. On examination, her blood pressure is 162/98 mm Hg, heart rate is 88/min, and she has bilateral pitting edema up to her knees. The rest of the examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal elevated serum creatinine of 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 0.9 mg/dL), and urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria with no red blood cells or casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 31-year-old pregnant woman, currently at 28 weeks of gestation, presents to her obstetrician with complaints of lower back pain and pelvic discomfort that have been worsening over the past 4 weeks. She describes the pain as dull and aching, with occasional sharp twinges that radiate to her buttocks and thighs. The discomfort is most pronounced when she walks or stands for extended periods. She denies any fever, urinary symptoms, or recent trauma. Her prenatal course has been uncomplicated thus far. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and a slight waddling gait. A mild lordosis is noted. The patient is concerned about the impact of the pain on her pregnancy and daily life. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain and decreased range of motion. She reports that the pain began insidiously and has progressively worsened, especially when trying to raise her arm above her head or reach behind her back. She denies any recent trauma or injury to the shoulder. She has no morning stiffness in her other joints and denies fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. Her medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder and limitation in both active and passive range of motion. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no acute fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling that started after a basketball game. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the knee joint. She denies any previous knee injuries or chronic joint problems. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is otherwise healthy. Physical examination reveals a swollen, tender right knee with limited range of motion. The patient exhibits an antalgic gait. Her vital signs are stable. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. Lab tests reveal a white blood cell count of 9,500/mm³, ESR of 25 mm/hr, and CRP of 3 mg/L. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue. She mentions that the pain is mainly in her hips and lower back and has been progressively worsening over the last 3 months. She denies any recent trauma, weight loss, or fever. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension managed with hydrochlorothiazide. Her mother was diagnosed with osteoporosis at an advanced age. On examination, her vital signs are: temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 138/85 mm Hg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the lumbar spine and hip joints. Initial laboratory tests show elevated serum calcium at 11.5 mg/dL (reference range: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and decreased serum phosphorus. The physician decides to order additional tests to further evaluate the findings. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Testing for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question:
A 45-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and tingling in her arms. She reports the symptoms are worse with neck movements and occasionally wakes her from sleep. She has no history of trauma. She has experienced intermittent fatigue and stiffness in her fingers, which improve throughout the day. She has a history of asthma managed with an albuterol inhaler. Her father has rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion of the cervical spine and diminished sensation in the C6 dermatome. Spurling's maneuver is positive. Laboratory studies show:
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 25 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic for recurrent oral ulcers over the past 6 months. She reports painful sores inside her mouth that occur every few weeks and heal spontaneously. Additionally, she has had episodes of painful genital ulcers and reports recent episodes of redness and pain in her eyes. She denies any joint swelling but mentions occasional joint pain and stiffness, especially in the morning. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and a few healing ulcers in the genital area. Ophthalmologic examination indicates anterior uveitis. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP levels. Based on this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of persistent fever and fatigue for the past 3 weeks. She has also experienced unintentional weight loss, night sweats, and occasional left knee pain, but denies any recent travel, cough, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism for which she is taking levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 38.3°C (100.9°F), mild tenderness of the left knee without swelling, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 14,000/mm³
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 78 mm/h
C-reactive protein (CRP) 15 mg/L
Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the next best step in the management of this patient?

options.D: Test for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.E: Obtain a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
options.F: Referral to an infectious disease specialist
options.G: Conduct a biopsy of the cervical lymph nodes

question: A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain. She describes the pain as migrating, initially starting in her knees and now affecting her ankles and wrists. She recalls having a sore throat about a month ago that resolved without treatment. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, she has mild swelling and tenderness over multiple joints, but no joint deformities are noted. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, heart rate is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. The physician orders laboratory tests, which show elevated inflammatory markers. The ASO titer is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purplish spots on her lower legs and intermittent blood in her urine. She also reports experiencing joint pain and fatigue over the past month. She denies any previous history of similar symptoms, recent travel, or new medications. Past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, the patient has palpable purpura on both lower extremities and mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory results show elevated inflammatory markers. Urinalysis confirms hematuria and proteinuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of swelling and pain in her hands and feet. She describes the pain as worse in the mornings, improving with activity throughout the day. She also reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals warm, swollen, and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both positive. Hand X-rays demonstrate soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and intermittent tingling sensations in her arms and fingers. She states that the symptoms are worse after prolonged periods of working at her desk. She also notes occasional morning stiffness in her neck. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any prior trauma or recent infections. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in the cervical spine, and Spurling's test reproduces her symptoms. An MRI of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes and a herniated disc at the C6-C7 level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to painful oral and genital ulcers that have recurred over the past 6 months. She reports that the ulcers typically resolve on their own but are painful and affect her ability to eat and have intercourse. She also notes episodes of joint pain and swelling, particularly in her knees and ankles, which occur intermittently. She denies fever, weight loss, or ocular symptoms. Physical examination reveals multiple shallow ulcers in her oral cavity and similar lesions in the genital area. There is mild swelling and tenderness in both knees. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent swelling in her neck and armpits over the last three months. She reports associated night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and fatigue. She does not have any significant joint pain, but describes intermittent joint stiffness in the morning. She has no prior history of autoimmune diseases, but her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has firm, non-tender lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 120/76 mmHg, pulse of 78/min, and respirations of 16/min. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and mild anemia. A lymph node biopsy is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her feet, which has gradually progressed to her fingertips. She describes a burning sensation that worsens at night. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, managed with metformin and lisinopril. She works as a secretary and reports difficulty typing due to the loss of sensation in her fingers. She denies recent trauma or changes in medication. Her family history is significant for celiac disease. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in the distal extremities. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 126/82 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin A1c of 8.2%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of joint pain and a red rash on her arms and legs. She reports that the rash appeared a few days after a hiking trip in the woods, during which she recalls removing a tick from her right thigh. She describes the joint pain as migratory and associated with swelling and stiffness in the knees and wrists. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals a maculopapular rash and mild synovitis in the affected joints. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. What is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past few months. She reports a recent history of increased thirst and frequent urination. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, she appears fatigued, and there is mild tenderness to palpation over the sternum and lumbar spine. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 12.1 mg/dL (reference range: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and a parathyroid hormone (PTH) level that is elevated. X-rays show diffuse bone demineralization. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition contributing to her symptoms?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute knee pain and swelling after participating in a weekend hiking trip. She describes twisting her knee during a fall while descending a steep trail. The pain aggravated with weight-bearing and movement, but she denies any clicking or locking. Her past medical history is unremarkable, with no history of inflammatory joint disease. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the lateral aspect of the right knee, with a reduced range of motion due to pain. McMurray test is positive on the lateral side. Laboratory tests reveal a normal white blood cell count, normal ESR, and CRP. An x-ray of the knee shows no fractures. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 2-month history of livedo reticularis and recurrent thromboses in her lower extremities. She reports intermittent episodes of blurred vision and severe headaches. Her past medical history is significant for two miscarriages during the first trimester. Her mother has systemic lupus erythematosus. The patient is currently taking oral contraceptives and ibuprofen as needed for joint pain. Physical examination shows a reticular, lace-like purple discoloration on her thighs and lower legs. Neurologic examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal:

Hemoglobin 12.1 g/dL
Leukocyte count 6,500/mm3
Platelet count 240,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 30 mm/h
Serum
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 0.3 mg/dL

Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her condition?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, sinus pressure, and a chronic productive cough. She reports increasing fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg during this period. Recently, she has also experienced hematuria and mild lower extremity swelling. Her medical history is significant only for recurrent episodes of sinusitis, and she denies any tobacco or alcohol use. Her family history is unremarkable. On examination, she appears pale with bilateral swelling of the ankles. Vital signs reveal a blood pressure of 145/95 mm Hg, pulse 88/min, and respiratory rate 18/min. Lung auscultation reveals scattered crackles in both lung bases. Laboratory studies show an elevated creatinine of 2.3 mg/dL and a urinalysis reveals red blood cell casts and mild proteinuria. A chest x-ray demonstrates bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a two-day history of painful, swollen joints in her hands and feet. She also reports a low-grade fever, fatigue, and a rash on her legs. She recently returned from a trip to Brazil, where she spent several weeks hiking and exploring. She denies any tick bites or recent upper respiratory infections. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals tender, swollen metacarpophalangeal and metatarsophalangeal joints. A faint, maculopapular rash is noted on her shins. Laboratory studies show a mild leukopenia and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 14-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to a 2-week history of purpura on her lower extremities, particularly noticeable after soccer practice. She also reports dark-colored urine and mild abdominal pain over the past few days. There is no fever, weight loss, or significant joint pain. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies, and she takes no regular medications. Family history is unremarkable for autoimmune conditions. On physical examination, she exhibits palpable purpura over her lower legs and mild tenderness in the abdomen without rebound. Laboratory results show hematuria and mild proteinuria. Blood tests reveal normal white blood cell count, hemoglobin at 12.5 g/dL, platelets at 250,000/mm3, and an ESR of 40 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and recent onset of painful genital ulcers. She also reports fatigue and non-specific joint pain in her knees and wrists that has been intermittent and non-deforming. She denies any history of similar symptoms in the family. On physical examination, multiple shallow ulcers are noted on her oral mucosa and genital area. There is no significant synovitis or swelling in her joints, and her skin appears normal without any rash. Laboratory tests reveal mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Test results for rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

options.I: Consider high levels of rheumatoid factor

question: A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance over the past several months. She also mentions occasional joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, which tends to improve throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, she appears pale and her thyroid gland is slightly enlarged. Her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 68/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, TSH of 8.5 mIU/L, and free T4 of 0.7 ng/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of persistent lower back pain that has been worsening over the past year. She describes the pain as dull and aching, primarily in the morning and improving with physical activity throughout the day. She has also noticed stiffness in her lower back and hips, which is particularly pronounced upon waking. Over-the-counter pain medication provides minimal relief. Her menstrual cycles are regular, and she denies any weight loss or fever. She has no significant medical history. Her father has a history of ankylosing spondylitis. Physical examination reveals reduced mobility in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a six-month history of progressive proximal muscle weakness, difficulty climbing stairs, and rising from a seated position. She also reports experiencing mild joint pain and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes. She denies any rash or skin changes. Her past medical history includes hypertension controlled with lisinopril. On examination, she has 4/5 strength in the proximal muscles of the arms and legs, with normal distal strength. There is no muscle tenderness, and her deep tendon reflexes are intact. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine kinase and normal ESR. An electromyography demonstrates myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive skin thickening on her fingers and hands over the past year. She reports experiencing episodes of finger color changes from white to blue to red, especially in cold weather. She also complains of fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and hypertension treated with lisinopril. Her vital signs are stable. On physical examination, the skin on her fingers appears shiny and thickened. Nailfold capillary examination shows dilated capillaries. Laboratory tests reveal a positive ANA and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis for this patient?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a severe headache and swelling in her legs over the past week. She also reports feeling unusually tired and having occasional episodes of blurred vision. She has no significant past medical history and is not currently on any medications. On examination, her blood pressure is 176/102 mmHg, heart rate is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. The physical examination is remarkable for bilateral pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal a serum creatinine of 2.0 mg/dL, which is elevated from her baseline of 0.8 mg/dL recorded six months ago. Urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria but no dysmorphic red blood cells or casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.F: Chronic kidney disease secondary to diabetes

question: A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening bilateral hip pain. She describes the pain as a persistent ache that is most severe when she rises from a seated position or after prolonged walking. She denies any morning stiffness but occasionally experiences fatigue and mild stiffness in her fingers. Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and mild osteoarthritis. She has been taking over-the-counter acetaminophen with minimal relief. Her temperature is 98.0°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 132/84 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, the hips are tender to palpation, with reduced range of motion, but no swelling or erythema is noted. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 28 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 1.2 mg/dL (reference range: 0.0-1.0 mg/dL). An X-ray of the pelvis shows mild joint space narrowing in both hips without erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and a facial rash that becomes more pronounced after sun exposure. She notes that the joint pain is mostly in her hands and wrists and tends to be worse in the morning. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, she has an erythematous rash over her cheeks and nasal bridge sparing the nasolabial folds. Vital signs are normal. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 4,500/mm^3, and platelet count of 150,000/mm^3. Additional serologic tests are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of acute pain and swelling in her right big toe over the past year. She describes the pain as severe and notes that it often occurs at night, waking her from sleep. The episodes last several days and are sometimes accompanied by low-grade fever.
