She has a history of hypertension and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Her medications include lisinopril and calcium carbonate. On examination, her right first metatarsophalangeal joint is swollen, erythematous, and tender to touch. There are several hard nodules present on the elbows and ears. Laboratory studies reveal a serum uric acid level of 9.5 mg/dL, with normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.F: Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystal deposition disease

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and unrefreshing sleep. She reports difficulty concentrating and frequent headaches, which have been affecting her job performance. She denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for depression, for which she takes sertraline. She has no family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals tenderness at multiple soft tissue sites but no synovitis or joint deformities. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. Thyroid function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and occasional fevers over the past three months. She describes the joint pain as diffuse and involving both her small and large joints, which is worse in the morning. She has no significant past medical history. On physical examination, she appears pale and has mild tenderness in her wrists and knees with no visible swelling. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, white blood cell count of 3,000/mm³, and platelet count of 110,000/mm³. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is 48 mm/hr. Liver and renal function tests are within normal limits. A peripheral blood smear shows normocytic, normochromic anemia. Rheumatoid factor is negative, and ANA is positive at 1:160 titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and swelling. She reports the pain started in her fingers and wrists and has now spread to her knees and ankles. She recently returned from a trip to Southeast Asia and denies any preceding trauma or significant illness. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. Examination reveals swelling and tenderness in multiple peripheral joints, with some limitation in range of motion due to pain. She has a faint maculopapular rash on her trunk and upper arms. Laboratory studies show:

WBC count: 7,800/mm3
ESR: 45 mm/hr
CRP: 6.2 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: negative
Anti-CCP: negative

Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 1-year history of progressive pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. She notes that the stiffness is worse in the morning, usually lasting over an hour, and improves as the day progresses. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with firm subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. She has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Considering this clinical presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 41-year-old woman comes to her primary care provider with complaints of bilateral joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting for over two hours for the past month. She reports pain in her wrists, hands, and knees that improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for a family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with reduced grip strength. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. Serology is positive for anti-CCP antibodies. Radiographs of both hands demonstrate periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. She reports stiffness in her hands and knees, particularly in the morning, that lasts over an hour. She has also noticed swelling and tenderness in her wrists and fingers bilaterally. There is no history of fever, weight loss, or recent travel. On physical examination, her hands show swelling of the proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, a normal white blood cell count, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 48 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past 3 months, along with periorbital edema and joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings. She reports feeling fatigued and has had difficulty concentrating at work. She denies any changes in her diet or exercise routine. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, she appears overweight with noticeable periorbital puffiness. Her blood pressure is 124/82 mmHg, pulse is 74/min, respirations are 16/min, and temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C). She exhibits mild tenderness in the small joints of her hands but no visible deformities. Lab tests reveal normocytic anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with worsening bone pain over the past six months, primarily affecting her pelvis and lower back. She also reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has occasional headaches. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with amlodipine, and hyperlipidemia. She is postmenopausal and has not taken hormone replacement therapy. On examination, there is tenderness over the pelvic region, but no deformities or synovitis in the joints. Laboratory tests reveal a normal calcium level, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and normal serum phosphorus. Radiographs of the pelvis show a mixed lytic and sclerotic appearance of the bone. Her vitamin D level is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 51-year-old woman presents to her family physician with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. She also notes a gradual swelling of her cheeks, especially around the parotid gland area, and experiences occasional fatigue and joint stiffness in the mornings. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent weight loss, fever, or night sweats. On physical examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands and decreased salivary flow. Schirmer's test shows decreased tear production. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with concerns about progressive weight gain over the past 6 months. She also reports periorbital puffiness, stiffness in her knees and fingers, especially in the morning, and general fatigue. She denies any significant changes in diet or physical activity. Her menstrual cycles are regular, and she has no significant stressors. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and non-pitting edema in both lower extremities. Her vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 122/78 mmHg, heart rate 72/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Laboratory results show normal renal function, slightly elevated TSH, and low free T4. Which of the following is the next step in management?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with 3 months of increasing bone pain in her hips and lower back. She has also noticed fatigue and a decreased appetite. She denies any recent trauma or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypercholesterolemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She has been postmenopausal for 18 years. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the pelvic girdle and lumbar spine. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory studies reveal: calcium 9.2 mg/dL, phosphate 3.4 mg/dL, alkaline phosphatase 280 U/L (elevated), and normal liver function tests. X-rays of the pelvis show cortical thickening and areas of radiolucency. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy. She also reports night sweats and a 3-kg unintentional weight loss over the past month. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. She denies any recent travel, animal exposures, or known sick contacts. Physical examination reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A chest x-ray is performed and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without pulmonary infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers over the past six months. She reports episodes of joint pain, particularly in her knees and ankles, which are more pronounced in the mornings. She has noticed increased fatigue and occasional fevers. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable. On examination, she has two small, well-defined ulcers on the buccal mucosa and one on the inner aspect of her labia. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A pathergy test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with pain and swelling in her left great toe joint that worsened over the past two days. She reports similar episodes occurring three times in the last year, typically resolving within a week. The patient notices nodules forming on her toes and ears over the past few months. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any fever, chills, or recent trauma. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous left first metatarsophalangeal joint with tenderness upon palpation, and the presence of tophi along the helix of the ears. Laboratory results show an elevated serum uric acid level, and joint aspiration yields needle-shaped crystals. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, fever, and night sweats. She also reports experiencing unintentional weight loss and generalized joint pain. Physical examination reveals multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary regions. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 12,000/mm3 with a lymphocytic predominance, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/h. A chest X-ray reveals mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in establishing the diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic because she has been feeling increasingly tired over the past 6 months. She reports that even after a full night's sleep, she feels exhausted throughout the day. She has also noticed that her hair has been thinning and her skin feels dry. She denies any weight change, but does report that her menstrual cycles have become irregular. She works as an accountant and describes no significant stressors in her life. She denies any joint pain or stiffness. On physical examination, her pulse is 60/min, and her skin is dry with slight non-pitting edema. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) level and decreased free thyroxine (T4) level. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past six months. The pain is dull and aching in nature, worse with activity, and alleviates with rest. She also reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes and occasional swelling in her hands. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her family history is significant for her mother having osteoarthritis. On examination, she has a normal gait but expresses discomfort on internal and external rotation of the hips. Routine lab work shows normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel, but increased inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty with tasks such as opening jars and typing. She has a history of smoking and her father had rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most consistent finding that supports the diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with swelling and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. She has also experienced general fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including her mother with lupus. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals bilateral swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. A hand X-ray indicates juxta-articular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 70-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending forward to pick up a newspaper. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the lower thoracic region. She denies any trauma, but notes that she has been feeling more tired than usual. Her medical history includes hypertension for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, there is localized tenderness over the T12 vertebra but no neurological deficits. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 30 mm/hr. X-ray of the spine reveals a compression fracture at T12. Which of the following is most likely related to this patient's underlying condition?

A 29-year-old woman is evaluated for progressive difficulty walking over the past month and lower extremity weakness. She describes the weakness as symmetric, and it is more pronounced in her thighs. She also reports numbness and tingling in her feet. Her past medical history includes asthma and seasonal allergies, and she denies any recent infections. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her family history is notable for an aunt with multiple sclerosis. Her temperature is 97.8°F (36.6°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Neurological exam shows decreased strength in her hip flexors and knee extensors, diminished deep tendon reflexes in the lower limbs, and decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in her feet. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. An MRI of the spine is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers. She describes these as deep and located on the inner aspects of her lips and cheeks. She also notes occasional painful genital ulcers that recur every few weeks. She reports intermittent joint pain affecting her knees and wrists, but denies any morning stiffness. She has not noticed any significant weight loss or fever, but mentions feeling generally fatigued. On examination, there are multiple healing ulcers in the oral cavity and one active ulcer on the labia. There is no significant lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent dryness of her eyes and mouth for the past 6 months. She reports having to frequently use artificial tears and sipping water throughout the day to alleviate the symptoms. Additionally, she notes swelling and tenderness near her jaw and occasional joint stiffness, particularly in the morning. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 115/73 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, she has bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands and dry oral mucosa. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of tingling and burning sensations in her hands and feet. She describes the symptoms as being worse at night and occasionally waking her from sleep. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in her daily activities. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is managed with metformin. Her vital signs are stable, with a blood pressure of 128/76 mmHg, heart rate of 75/min, and temperature of 36.7°C (98.1°F). On examination, she has decreased pinprick sensation in a stocking-glove distribution, with some mild weakness in her grip strength. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin A1c level of 8.5%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of persistent lymphadenopathy, night sweats, and weight loss. She reports swelling in her neck and axillary regions, which has not resolved and occasionally becomes tender. She also notes fatigue and occasional joint discomfort, particularly in her hands, but denies any significant joint swelling or morning stiffness. She recalls having similar symptoms ten years ago, which resolved spontaneously. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. On examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes that are firm and non-tender. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal complete blood count. A chest X-ray shows no mediastinal mass. A biopsy of one of the enlarged lymph nodes is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening shoulder pain and stiffness. She reports difficulty with overhead activities and states that the pain is worse at night, often waking her from sleep. She denies any recent trauma to the shoulder but mentions that she feels a general sense of fatigue. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is limited active and passive range of motion in the left shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no warmth or swelling of the shoulder joint, and the remainder of the musculoskeletal examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 25 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein (CRP) of 18 mg/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurrent painful oral and genital ulcers for the past 6 months. She also reports occasional joint pain in her knees and wrists, which is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies any recent travel or new sexual partners. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes ibuprofen for pain relief. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals several shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and similar lesions on the vulva. There is no evidence of synovitis in the joints examined. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 40 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr) and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 10 mg/L (reference range: 0-5 mg/L). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.F: Testing for celiac disease with tissue transglutaminase antibodies

question: A 56-year-old woman comes to the neurology clinic because of progressive difficulty walking over the past 8 months. She reports feeling weak in her legs, which makes it hard to climb stairs or get up from a chair. She also mentions occasional numbness and tingling in her feet. She denies any pain in her joints but describes mild fatigue. Her medical history includes hypertension and a remote history of breast cancer treated with surgery and chemotherapy 10 years ago. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 14/min. Neurological examination shows decreased strength in the hip flexors and quadriceps, as well as diminished deep tendon reflexes in the lower extremities. Sensory examination reveals a mild decrease in vibratory sensation at the toes. Electromyography of the lower limbs is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with episodes of unexplained bruising and a lacy, purple rash on her legs that worsens with cold exposure. She mentions having several episodes of leg swelling and pain over the past year, leading to a deep venous thrombosis diagnosis two months ago, for which she was started on anticoagulants. She also reports occasional joint pain and feeling unusually fatigued. Her medical history is significant for two miscarriages in the second trimester. Laboratory tests reveal a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) and a positive lupus anticoagulant. What is the most likely additional finding in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity. She denies any history of trauma or injury to her back. She reports that the pain occasionally radiates to her buttocks and is associated with fatigue and disturbed sleep. Ibuprofen provides some relief. Her family history is significant for her father who had psoriasis. On physical examination, there is limited range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A recent X-ray of the spine shows evidence of vertebral body squaring and sclerosis of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 65-year-old postmenopausal woman comes to the clinic because of progressively worsening bone pain in her pelvis and lower back over the past six months. She also reports fatigue and occasional headaches. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, treated with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. Her mother had osteoporosis and her father had rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is tenderness on palpation over the pelvis and lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show normal calcium and phosphate levels, but markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase. Bone scans reveal increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar vertebrae. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of elevated blood pressure and mild fatigue. Recently, she has noticed swelling in her ankles and occasional headaches. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or changes in urination frequency. Her medical history includes mild osteoarthritis treated with over-the-counter NSAIDs. On examination, her blood pressure is 165/95 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable, but there is mild peripheral edema. Laboratory studies reveal serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen of 30 mg/dL, and mild proteinuria. Urinalysis shows no casts or hematuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with headaches, fatigue, and swelling around her eyes and ankles over the past month. She mentions that her blood pressure readings at home have been elevated, and she has felt unusually tired. Her medical history is unremarkable, aside from occasional joint stiffness in the mornings. Family history reveals a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg, heart rate is 80/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal, but laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and proteinuria. Further examination of her joints reveals mild tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints without significant synovitis. Which of the following is the next best step in managing this patient's condition?

A 63-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of worsening bilateral hip pain. She reports that the pain is dull and aching, occasionally radiating down the thighs but is mostly localized to the hip joints. The discomfort worsens with prolonged standing or walking, and she feels stiff in the morning for about 30 minutes. She has hypertension and hyperlipidemia managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She drinks wine socially and does not smoke. The patient is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 80 kg (176 lb); BMI is 28.4 kg/m2. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in both hips, especially with internal rotation, and mild tenderness over the greater trochanteric area. Laboratory tests show mild anemia with a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL. An X-ray of the pelvis shows joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis in the hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a rash and dark-colored urine for the past week. She reports that the rash started on her lower legs and has spread to her thighs, and the urine discoloration has been persistent. She denies any recent infections or new medications. She has also experienced joint pain mainly in her knees and ankles, along with mild abdominal discomfort. Vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure of 118/75 mmHg, pulse of 78/min, and respirations of 14/min. Physical examination shows palpable purpura on the lower extremities with no significant systemic involvement. Laboratory studies reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-month history of difficulty walking and weakness in her right foot. She reports tripping frequently and has noticed that her foot seems to drop when she tries to walk. She has no significant past medical history and denies any recent trauma. She mentions experiencing occasional joint pain in her hands and wrists, which she attributes to her job as a typist. On examination, there is weakness in right foot dorsiflexion, and the right foot appears to slap the ground when she walks. There is no tenderness along the spine, and sensation to light touch is intact throughout. Laboratory tests show normal electrolytes, renal function, and inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's presentation?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent oral ulcers that have been occurring over the past year. She also reports experiencing painful genital ulcers that occur less frequently. The patient describes associated joint pain in her knees and ankles, along with episodes of fatigue. She denies any recent travel or new sexual partners. Her medical history is notable for intermittent rashes on her arms that improve with topical corticosteroids. On examination, there are several shallow ulcerations in the oral mucosa and tenderness over the bilateral knees without swelling. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a normal complete blood count. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman presents with persistent lower back pain and stiffness for the past year. The pain is more pronounced in the morning and improves with physical activity. She reports intermittent pain in her buttocks and hips, sometimes radiating down the posterior thighs, and occasional fatigue. She has no history of trauma or significant weight loss. Her family history is notable for an uncle with ankylosing spondylitis. Physical examination reveals limited lumbar flexion and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, but no peripheral joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP levels. An X-ray of her pelvis reveals sclerosis and narrowing of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is most likely to be found in further evaluation?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurrent episodes of nasal congestion, cough, and shortness of breath over the past 6 months. She reports having occasional joint pain and fatigue but denies any fever or weight loss. Her past medical history includes hypertension and seasonal allergies. She has been taking over-the-counter decongestants with minimal relief. On physical examination, she has bilateral crackles on lung auscultation and mild tenderness in the joints of her hands. A complete blood count reveals mild anemia, and urinalysis shows hematuria and proteinuria. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Order an antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) test
options.D: Refer to a pulmonologist for bronchoscopy
options.F: Prescribe antibiotics for suspected bacterial sinusitis

question:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic nasal congestion, runny nose, and occasional nosebleeds over the past 6 months. She also reports a persistent cough and increasing shortness of breath. Recently, she has noticed swelling in her ankles and frothy urine. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension, which is controlled with an ACE inhibitor. She denies any joint pain or stiffness. On examination, she has tenderness over the paranasal sinuses and diffuse crackles are heard on lung auscultation bilaterally. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. A renal biopsy is performed, and the results show necrotizing glomerulonephritis with crescent formation.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Chronic kidney disease due to diabetes

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and intermittent numbness and tingling in her arms and hands. She describes the pain as aching and worse with movement. There is no history of trauma. She also reports occasional headaches and difficulty sleeping due to discomfort. She does not smoke, and her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with an ACE inhibitor. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals reduced range of motion in the neck and diminished sensation in the fingertips bilaterally. Reflexes are brisk in the upper extremities, and there is no muscle wasting. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and metabolic profile. An MRI of the cervical spine is performed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Physical therapy focusing on cervical stabilization

question:
A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and color changes in her fingers and toes for the past 6 months. She describes episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon warming. She also notes some joint stiffness, particularly in the morning, and occasional fatigue. She has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. Family history is unremarkable. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, there is thickening of the skin over the fingers with some shiny appearance and mild swelling. Nailfold capillaroscopy reveals dilated capillaries. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 13.5 g/dL
White blood cell count: 7,000/mm^3
Platelet count: 245,000/mm^3
ESR: 25 mm/hr

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening skin lesions and joint pain. She describes the lesions as red, scaly patches predominantly on her elbows and knees. The joint pain is asymmetric, affecting her right index finger and left knee, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies significant systemic symptoms but reports occasional fatigue. Her medical history is significant for mild hypertension, controlled with medication. On examination, the skin lesions are erythematous with silvery scales, and the right index finger appears swollen with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory tests show an elevated C-reactive protein and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Raynaud's phenomenon is absent. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She describes pain in her wrists, hands, and knees, which improves with movement throughout the day. She has difficulty opening jars and notes her rings have become tighter. Her medical history is unremarkable, but her mother was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis in her 50s. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender bilaterally, with no deformities or subcutaneous nodules observed. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are both elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She also reports a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure and has been experiencing significant fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals a malar rash across the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, as well as tenderness and swelling in the wrist joints. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer and low complement levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the physician with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also describes occasional aching in her hips. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. The patient has been taking ibuprofen with partial relief of her symptoms. Physical examination reveals limited lumbar flexion and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and elevated C-reactive protein. An X-ray of the pelvis is ordered and shows bilateral sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and hands for the past few months. She also reports experiencing color changes in her fingers when exposed to cold, transitioning from white to blue to red, accompanied by pain and tingling. She mentions mild joint pain and fatigue but denies any significant morning stiffness. On examination, there is notable thickening of the skin over the fingers (sclerodactyly) and some digital ulcerations. Her blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, heart rate is 78/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). Nailfold capillary microscopy reveals dilated loops. Laboratory tests show a positive ANA. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins, accompanied by joint pain and fatigue. She recalls having a sore throat a few weeks prior, but denies recent fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Her medical history is significant for asthma managed with albuterol as needed. On examination, there are several tender, erythematous nodules on both lower legs, and mild swelling of the knees is noted. There is no rash, and her throat appears normal. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A rapid strep test is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Refer to rheumatology for suspected rheumatoid arthritis

question: A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressively worsening weakness in her shoulders and hips over the past 3 months. She finds it difficult to lift objects above her head and has trouble climbing stairs. She also reports occasional joint pain and morning stiffness in her wrists. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any skin rash, fever, or recent infections. She works as a librarian and does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals 4/5 muscle strength in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower extremities, with preserved distal muscle strength. There is no tenderness or swelling of the joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated creatine kinase (CK) level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of recurrent fevers and swollen lymph nodes. She reports feeling fatigued and has noticed swelling in her neck and armpits, which becomes more pronounced during febrile episodes. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or significant weight loss. She experiences occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, which she attributes to her active lifestyle. Her medical history is unremarkable except for eczema managed with topical creams. On examination, she has tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal leukocytosis, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA). A lymph node biopsy is pending. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized fatigue. She reports these symptoms are accompanied by night sweats and a 5 kg (11 lbs) weight loss in the last few months. She also notes occasional joint pain but denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. On examination, her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg. There is cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. The spleen is palpable 2 cm below the left costal margin. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL
Leukocyte count 12,300/mm3
Platelet count 210,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 45 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-day history of joint pain affecting her knees, wrists, and ankles. She reports that the pain began after returning from a trip to South America. The joints are swollen and tender, and she has noticed a slight rash across her cheeks. She denies any previous episodes of similar joint pain. She has no significant past medical history and takes no daily medications. She denies fever, chills, or recent infections. On examination, she has symmetrical polyarthritis and a faint maculopapular rash on her face. Laboratory findings reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of swollen and painful joints. She reports experiencing fatigue and mild fever. She recently returned from a trip to Southeast Asia where she engaged in outdoor activities. She denies any rash, photosensitivity, or recent infections. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness in the wrists, knees, and ankles. There are no skin changes or oral ulcers. Laboratory tests reveal a normal white blood cell count, mild thrombocytopenia, and an elevated C-reactive protein level. A recent viral serology test is pending. What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports stiffness lasting more than an hour in the morning and describes the pain as symmetrical and primarily affecting her small joints. She denies any recent trauma or injury. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and obesity. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 132/84 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past 8 months. She also mentions that her skin has become dry and her hair is thinning. She denies any recent illnesses, but reports feeling cold more often than usual. Her past medical history includes mild anxiety for which she takes escitalopram. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, she has a slightly puffy face and dry, coarse skin. Her vital signs include a temperature of 97.0°F (36.1°C), blood pressure of 124/78 mmHg, pulse of 58/min, and respirations of 16/min. Laboratory results show a TSH level of 9.8 mIU/L (reference range 0.5-4.5 mIU/L) and a free T4 level of 0.6 ng/dL (reference range 0.8-1.8 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 39-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and a persistent cough that has lasted for several months. She also reports occasional hemoptysis and recent swelling in her ankles. She has experienced fatigue, unintended weight loss, and arthralgia in her knees and wrists. She denies any fever or night sweats. Her medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 135/88 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, there is tenderness over the sinuses, and diffuse crackles are heard on lung auscultation. Mild pitting edema is present in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine, positive c-ANCA, negative ANA, and an elevated ESR. Urinalysis demonstrates hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past year. She also notes occasional swelling of her face and neck, which she attributes to enlarged glands. Over the past six months, she has experienced increased fatigue and difficulty swallowing dry foods. She denies any joint pain or stiffness. Her medical history is significant for a resolved bout of infectious mononucleosis five years ago. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Schirmer's test shows reduced tear production, and laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A lip biopsy is performed and shows focal lymphocytic sialadenitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a red, scaly rash on her face and arms that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports joint pain mainly affecting her hands and knees, as well as oral ulcers that have been painful. She denies any fevers, weight loss, or night sweats. She has a family history significant for autoimmune diseases, with her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has a well-demarcated erythematous rash over her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, with similar patches on her forearms. The oral examination reveals several small ulcers on the buccal mucosa. Laboratory results show a positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent sinus congestion, cough, and increasing shortness of breath. She reports hematuria for the past month and has been feeling increasingly tired. On physical examination, she appears pale and has a blood pressure of 145/90 mmHg, pulse rate of 92/min, and respirations of 20/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. Chest X-ray shows pulmonary infiltrates. An ANCA panel is sent to support the diagnosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis or next step in management for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, intermittent low-grade fever, and red-brown subcutaneous nodules on her forearms and elbows. She reports occasional joint stiffness, particularly in the morning, but denies any significant joint pain or swelling. She also mentions a 5-pound weight loss over the past two months without changes in diet or exercise.

Her family history is notable for autoimmune disease, including lupus in her sister. On examination, she has multiple firm nodules over the extensor surfaces of her arms, which are nontender and vary in size. Vital signs are normal, and she is afebrile. Laboratory results reveal mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is positive in a low titer, and rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.D: Test for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies
options.G: Order a CT scan of the abdomen

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a deep ache that initially started as mild discomfort but has become more intense, particularly after she has been sitting for prolonged periods. She denies any morning stiffness or joint swelling. She notes increased fatigue and difficulty climbing stairs, which she attributes to her hip discomfort. She has no significant past medical history, and family history is unremarkable for autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, there is a decreased range of motion in both hips with internal rotation eliciting pain. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Prescribe physical therapy focused on hip strengthening exercises
options.B: Refer for hip radiographs to assess for degenerative changes
options.D: Start a trial of oral corticosteroids
options.E: Measure serum levels of rheumatoid factor
options.G: Order a bone scan to evaluate for avascular necrosis

question: A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in her right arm. She describes the pain as a dull ache that occasionally radiates to her shoulder. The tingling is more pronounced in her thumb and index finger. She denies recent trauma but reports occasional morning stiffness in her fingers that lasts less than 30 minutes. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, her blood pressure is 135/85 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Neurological examination reveals reduced sensation to pinprick in the right C6 dermatome. Muscle strength in the upper extremities is 5/5 bilaterally, although she complains of fatigue. An MRI of the cervical spine shows a posterior disc herniation at C5-C6 without significant spinal cord compression. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty walking, noticing that her right foot tends to drag. She denies any trauma to the area but reports some mild pain in her upper arm for several months that she attributed to overuse. She also describes feeling more fatigued than usual and has experienced occasional tingling in her right hand. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot and lateral aspect of the right leg. Babinski sign is absent bilaterally. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers, with an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 32-year-old woman presents to your clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, dry skin, and unexplained weight gain over the past four months. She also reports feeling more sensitive to cold temperatures lately. The patient denies any significant changes in diet or exercise habits. Her medical history includes postpartum depression after the birth of her second child three years ago. She is currently taking a daily multivitamin and an over-the-counter iron supplement. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, her pulse is 60/min, blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg, and she has slight periorbital puffiness. A neck examination reveals mild enlargement of the thyroid gland. Laboratory results show TSH elevated at 7.5 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) and free T4 at the lower end of the normal range. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of lower back pain and stiffness. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity as the day progresses. She reports fatigue and occasional pain in her hips. She denies any significant joint swelling or redness but does mention discomfort in her neck. Her medical history is notable for irritable bowel syndrome. The patient denies any recent infections or fevers. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited movement of the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show elevated C-reactive protein. The patient is HLA-B27 positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of lower back pain. She describes the pain as dull and achy, predominantly in the buttock region, and notes that it improves with physical activity but worsens with rest, causing her to wake up in the early morning hours. She reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour. There is no history of recent trauma, and she denies any bowel or bladder dysfunction. Her family history is significant for a father who has ankylosing spondylitis. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following findings would most likely support an alternative diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis in this patient?

options.G: Absence of spinal fusion on imaging

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and fatigue. She reports that her fevers are accompanied by night sweats and generalized lymphadenopathy, particularly in the cervical and inguinal regions. She also mentions experiencing occasional joint pain in her hands and wrists that seems worse in the morning but subsides throughout the day. Her past medical history is non-contributory, and she does not take any medications. She denies recent travel, sick contacts, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes in the cervical and inguinal areas, as well as mild swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level, with normal white blood cell count and no anemia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty walking and tripping over her left foot. She reports a gradual onset of foot drop, which has made it challenging to walk without stumbling. The patient denies any recent fever, weight loss, or trauma. She has a history of intermittent joint pain in her hands and knees and reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Her family history includes a sister with lupus. On examination, her left foot shows weakness in dorsiflexion, and there is reduced sensation over the lateral aspect of the left leg. Reflexes are 2+ bilaterally, except for an absent left ankle jerk. Serum electrolyte levels, including calcium and potassium, are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, easy bruising, and intermittent joint pain. She reports being unusually tired and having difficulty completing her daily activities. She denies any recent infections or significant weight changes. Her menstrual periods have become heavier over the past few months. On physical examination, she has pallor of the conjunctiva and several petechiae are noted on her lower extremities. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 112/70 mmHg, and pulse is 88/min. The spleen is palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin. Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 2,300/mm3
Platelet count 90,000/mm3
Reticulocyte count 1%
The patient’s symptoms and lab findings are most suggestive of which of the following conditions?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent bone pain that has been worsening over the last 6 months, particularly in her lower back and hips. She has also experienced fatigue and occasional nausea. She reports that her symptoms are more pronounced after a day of physical activity. Her past medical history includes hypertension and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Her current medications include lisinopril and calcium supplements. On physical examination, she appears in mild distress due to pain but has no joint swelling or redness. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum calcium level of 12.2 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and a parathyroid hormone (PTH) level of 85 pg/mL (normal: 10-65 pg/mL). X-rays of the lumbar spine show generalized osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and episodes of discoloration in her hands triggered by cold exposure. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red, accompanied by a tingling sensation. These symptoms began about 6 months ago and have gradually worsened. She also notes some joint pain in her hands and wrists, which is most noticeable in the morning. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraines. She denies any recent weight loss, fever, or night sweats. Physical examination reveals taut skin over the fingers and mild synovitis of the wrist joints. Laboratory tests show a negative rheumatoid factor and elevated antinuclear antibody (ANA) titers. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 31-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to a 3-month history of joint pain in her wrists and ankles, as well as an intermittent rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She also mentions having painful sores in her mouth and feeling more fatigued than usual. She has no noteworthy medical history but recounts a family history of autoimmune disorders. Physical examination reveals a butterfly-shaped rash across her cheeks and nose, without any joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis given this presentation?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of numbness and tingling in her feet for the past 6 months. She describes the sensation as a burning pain that often worsens at night. She has a history of type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. She reports occasional joint stiffness and fatigue. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution in the lower extremities. Reflexes are decreased at the ankles. Laboratory test results show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Her hemoglobin A1c is 7.8% (reference range: 4.0-6.0%). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman who is 28 weeks pregnant comes to her obstetrician with complaints of lower back and pelvic pain that has progressively worsened over the past month. She describes the pain as a dull ache that becomes sharp with certain movements, especially when walking or getting up from a seated position. She denies any trauma or falls. This is her first pregnancy, and she reports normal fetal movements. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She does not take any other medications. Physical examination reveals a positive Trendelenburg sign and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory workup shows normal complete blood count and thyroid function tests. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Initiate physical therapy focused on pelvic stabilization
options.D: Advise on the use of a maternity belt
options.E: Perform a pelvic MRI to rule out sacroiliitis
options.F: Refer to an orthopedic specialist for further evaluation

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic complaining of gradual weight gain and swelling around her eyes over the last 3 months. She also reports morning joint stiffness lasting about 30 minutes, fatigue, and dry skin. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, which is well-managed with medication. On examination, her blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). There is periorbital edema and non-pitting edema of the lower extremities. Her thyroid gland is diffusely enlarged but non-tender. Laboratory tests reveal low serum sodium, high cholesterol levels, and elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-month history of skin discoloration on her lower extremities and a recent history of two miscarriages. She describes the skin changes as a lace-like purplish discoloration that worsens in cold weather. Additionally, she has experienced recurrent episodes of leg pain and swelling over the past year. Her medical history is significant for migraines and iron deficiency anemia, for which she takes ferrous sulfate. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Notable findings include livedo reticularis on her legs and mild tenderness along her left calf. Laboratory results show an elevated ESR and positive anticardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Doppler ultrasound of the lower extremities
options.B: Testing for Factor V Leiden mutation

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, weight loss, and nodules on her elbows. She also reports occasional fevers and a general feeling of malaise. The nodules are non-tender, firm, and located on the extensor surfaces of her arms. She denies any significant joint pain but mentions that her knees sometimes feel stiff in the morning for about 30 minutes. She has a history of smoking and her father had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. The nodules are palpable and range from 0.5 cm to 2 cm in size. Her hands show no swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with sudden onset of joint pain affecting her knees, ankles, and wrists over the past two weeks. She reports that the pain is severe upon waking but improves slightly throughout the day. She denies any significant past medical history but mentions that she recently returned from a hiking trip in the Appalachian Mountains. She also describes mild fatigue and a low-grade fever. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), blood pressure is 118/75 mmHg, and pulse is 78/min. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the affected joints with mild erythema. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). The patient’s ANA and rheumatoid factor are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful red nodules on her legs and joint pain in her knees and ankles for the past two weeks. She denies fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. She reports a mild sore throat a week before the onset of her current symptoms. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. She works as a kindergarten teacher and does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, she has tender, erythematous nodules on her shins and bilateral knee swelling without effusion. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg. Laboratory studies reveal a leukocyte count of 10,000/mm³ with a normal differential, C-reactive protein of 25 mg/L, and normal renal function. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman visits her primary care clinic complaining of bilateral hip pain that has gradually worsened over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is present most days and worsens with prolonged activity, making it difficult to walk her dog or climb stairs. Ibuprofen provides only minimal relief. She denies any morning stiffness but notes increasing fatigue in the afternoons. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril and a left knee arthroscopy 5 years ago. Her vital signs are: blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, heart rate 76/min, and temperature 37.2°C (99.0°F). On examination, there is tenderness over both greater trochanters and decreased range of motion in both hips. Laboratory investigations reveal normal inflammatory markers, and a pelvic X-ray shows mild degenerative changes in both hip joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of diffuse bone pain and fatigue. She also reports episodes of nausea and constipation. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies recent trauma or falls. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sternum and ribs. Laboratory tests show a serum calcium level of 11.8 mg/dL (reference range: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and decreased serum phosphate. Parathyroid hormone level is elevated. An X-ray of the thorax shows osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of progressive bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notes swelling in her wrists and fingers. She has also noticed firm nodules under her skin, particularly on her forearms. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, which is well-controlled on levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with palpable subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are positive. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.C: Confirm the diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis
options.E: Order an MRI of the hands

question: A 25-year-old female presents to the clinic with a one-week history of a rash on her lower legs and blood in her urine. She reports having a sore throat two weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. She also notes experiencing joint pain in her knees and ankles, but no redness or significant swelling. Vital signs are normal, and physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower extremities. Urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Laboratory workup demonstrates normal complete blood count and renal function, mildly elevated ESR, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that is progressively worsening. She reports stiffness in her back that is most severe in the morning and improves with physical activity as the day progresses. She denies any trauma or injury. Her past medical history is significant for irritable bowel syndrome. She works as a librarian and notes that her pain makes it difficult to sit for prolonged periods. On examination, there is limited flexion of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old female presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, unintentional weight loss of 5 kg over the past 3 months, and generalized weakness. She also reports occasional joint pain, particularly in her hands and knees, but denies morning stiffness. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. On physical examination, she appears pale, and her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 70 fL, reticulocyte count of 1%, and serum ferritin of 15 ng/mL. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated. Which of the following is most likely contributing to this patient's symptoms?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that began about a week ago. She recently recovered from a viral illness characterized by fever, sore throat, and headache. She reports difficulty sleeping due to the discomfort and feels exhausted even after a full night's rest. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. There is no joint swelling or erythema, but she describes tenderness upon palpation of various muscle groups. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness, most notably affecting her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Her current medications include ibuprofen as needed for pain. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and a reduced grip strength bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. X-rays of her hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue, hair loss, and difficulty concentrating over the past few months. She also reports experiencing cold intolerance and occasional joint stiffness in the morning that improves throughout the day. Her past medical history is significant for an episode of postpartum thyroiditis two years ago. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. A physical exam reveals mild periorbital edema and dry skin. Laboratory tests show elevated thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. Her complete blood count reveals normocytic anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive bone pain over the past 6 months. The pain affects her hips and lower back, especially after long periods of activity. She has been postmenopausal for 15 years and takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. She denies any recent falls or injuries. Her medical history includes hyperlipidemia and hypertension, managed with atorvastatin and lisinopril. Laboratory studies show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level of 320 U/L (reference range: 30-120 U/L) with normal calcium and phosphate levels. A bone scan shows increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with severe back pain that began suddenly after lifting a heavy box two days ago. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the middle of her back. She notes that she has lost an inch in height over the past year. She does not have a history of trauma or falls. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. She reports no history of steroid use. Her physical examination reveals tenderness over the mid-thoracic spine with limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory tests show normal calcium, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase levels, and a slightly decreased vitamin D level. Radiographs of the thoracic spine reveal a compression fracture of the T7 vertebra. What is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

options.B: Osteomalacia due to vitamin D deficiency

question: A 62-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy object at home. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the lower thoracic region, with no radiation. She reports a history of low-impact fractures and has been experiencing generalized fatigue and joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis, and she is currently taking alendronate and calcium supplements. She denies any recent trauma or falls. On examination, she has tenderness over the T12 vertebra. Neurological examination is unremarkable, and there are no signs of spinal cord compression. Her laboratory results show normal levels of vitamin D and calcium. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Initiate physical therapy for back strengthening
options.B: Obtain a spinal X-ray to assess for vertebral compression fracture
options.C: Order an MRI of the spine to evaluate for spinal cord involvement
options.D: Measure serum rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.F: Consult orthopedic surgery for possible intervention

question: A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of migratory joint pain affecting her knees, ankles, elbows, and wrists. She reports having a sore throat and fever just before the onset of her joint symptoms, which have since improved. She denies any rash, chest pain, or shortness of breath. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and her immunizations are up to date. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. Her joints are tender with mild swelling but no erythema. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a mild elevation in C-reactive protein (CRP). Rapid streptococcal antigen test is positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Initiate treatment with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
options.B: Start a course of oral prednisone

question:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic nasal congestion, cough, and fatigue for the past six months. She also reports occasional blood-tinged nasal discharge and a recent onset of facial pain. On further questioning, she admits to experiencing joint stiffness in the mornings and mild hematuria over the past few weeks. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies and hypertension. She denies any fever, night sweats, or significant weight changes. Vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 130/80 mmHg, pulse of 82/min, and respirations of 18/min. Physical examination shows tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and scattered crackles on lung auscultation. Laboratory studies reveal: 

Serum Creatinine: 2.1 mg/dL 
Urinalysis: RBC casts, mild proteinuria 

A chest radiograph shows bilateral patchy pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of chronic neck pain and intermittent numbness and tingling in her right arm and hand. She describes the pain as a constant ache that worsens with neck movement and occasionally radiates down her arm. Her medical history includes obesity, hypertension managed with lisinopril, and osteoarthritis of the knees. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome on the right, with reduced strength in the right biceps and wrist extensors. Reflexes are diminished at the right biceps. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that improves with exercise and is worse in the morning. She also reports occasional pain in her hips and buttocks. She denies any recent trauma, but notes fatigue and occasional mild fevers. Her father has a history of ankylosing spondylitis. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and decreased range of motion of the lumbar spine. Laboratory findings reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and a dry cough. She also reports increasing fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands, which she attributes to her work as a typist. Over the past month, she has noticed blood-tinged nasal discharge and has developed shortness of breath with exertion. Her medical history includes treated hypertension and allergic rhinitis. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.1°C (98.8°F), blood pressure of 128/82 mm Hg, pulse of 88/min, and respiratory rate of 20/min. Lung examination reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory tests show an elevated serum creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr. A chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Obtain a CT scan of the sinuses

question: A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 8 months. She describes the joint pain as symmetric, affecting both her wrists and knees, and notes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She also reports intermittent fevers and unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds over the last 6 months. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal joints and the wrists bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL, leukocyte count 7,800, platelet count 300,000, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 78 mm/hr. A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and numbness in her right arm. She reports that the pain radiates from her neck into her shoulder and down her arm, accompanied by intermittent tingling in her fingers. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, both well-controlled with medication. She works as a data analyst and spends prolonged periods at her computer. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the cervical paraspinal muscles and reduced range of motion in the neck. There is diminished sensation in the C6 dermatome of the right arm and weakness in the wrist extensors. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the right biceps. The rest of her neurological examination is normal. An MRI of the cervical spine shows degenerative disc disease with a herniated disc at C5-C6. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-week history of diffuse body aches and fatigue. She reports that these symptoms began shortly after recovering from a cold.
