She describes the pain as a deep, constant ache affecting multiple areas, including her shoulders, neck, and hips. She denies joint swelling or morning stiffness but mentions feeling more tired than usual. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent travel. On physical examination, she appears well-nourished, and there is tenderness to palpation in the trapezius and paraspinal muscles. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), C-reactive protein (CRP), and complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of scaly skin lesions on her elbows and knees for the past 6 months. She reports that these lesions occasionally bleed and are itchy. In addition, she has been experiencing joint pain in her fingers and toes that is worse in the mornings. She denies any significant swelling but notes that her fingers are sometimes stiff. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, there are several erythematous plaques with silvery scales on her extensor surfaces, as well as nail pitting. Laboratory tests reveal moderately elevated ESR and CRP levels, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to bilateral hand pain and swelling that have progressively worsened over the past six months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies and rheumatoid factor are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of persistent fever, fatigue, and generalized malaise. She reports occasional joint pain and swelling, particularly in the morning, but her main concern is the ongoing fever. She also notes unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds over the past month. Her medical history is significant for a resolved case of Helicobacter pylori infection treated with antibiotics two years ago. On examination, her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, pulse is 94/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Blood cultures are negative, and there is no obvious source of infection. Which of the following is the next best step in her management?

options.D: Screen for hepatitis B and C

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 6-month history of neck pain, tingling in her hands, and intermittent numbness in her fingers. She notes that the symptoms worsen with prolonged use of a computer and occasionally wake her up at night. She has no history of trauma or recent infections. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation in the thumb and first two fingers of both hands, as well as a positive Spurling's test. She has full range of motion in her neck, but there is mild tenderness to palpation over the cervical spine. Her complete blood count and basic metabolic panel are within normal limits. An MRI of the cervical spine is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Cervical radiculopathy due to disc herniation

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash that developed over the past few weeks. She reports that the rash started after she returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States, where she recalls removing a tick from her thigh. The rash is described as red and circular, resembling a bullseye, and is located on her left thigh. She has pain and swelling in her knees and wrists, which is worse in the morning. She denies any fever or recent illness. On examination, there is a large erythematous annular lesion on her left thigh and tenderness in the joints mentioned. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic lower back pain that has been progressively worsening over the last year. She mentions stiffness in her back that is more pronounced in the morning but improves with movement as the day progresses. She denies any significant history of trauma. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism and fibromyalgia. Her family history includes osteoarthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her blood tests show elevated C-reactive protein and a normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate. She reports occasional fatigue but no significant weight loss, fever, or night sweats. An X-ray of the lumbosacral spine shows sclerosis and narrowing of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on her face and joint pain. She reports that the rash becomes more pronounced after sun exposure and is associated with painful swelling in her hands and feet. She also notes fatigue and has developed sores in her mouth. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Her joints exhibit mild tenderness and swelling, especially in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal anemia and leukopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial test for diagnosing her condition?

A 54-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with persistent bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the last eight months. She describes it as a dull ache, more pronounced in the mornings and often alleviated by mild activity. There is minimal swelling and no significant redness noted. She denies any recent trauma but mentions having difficulty walking long distances due to discomfort. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. On examination, the pain is reproducible on palpation of the greater trochanteric region, and there is pain on internal rotation of the hips. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An X-ray of the hips shows no significant joint space narrowing or osteophytes. Which of the following conditions is most likely contributing to this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to worsening neck pain and tingling sensations in her arms over the past 3 months. She notes that the discomfort is persistent and worsens with neck movement. She sometimes feels weakness in her grip, making it difficult to hold her coffee mug. She has a history of intermittent headaches and notes occasional stiffness in the morning but denies any significant joint swelling. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril, and she takes ibuprofen for pain relief. On examination, there is reduced range of motion in her cervical spine and decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome bilaterally. Reflexes are diminished at the biceps and brachioradialis. A cervical spine MRI is performed and shows degenerative changes and mild spinal canal narrowing at C5-C6. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent fever of 38.5°C (101.3°F) for the past three weeks. She reports accompanying fatigue, weight loss, and occasional night sweats. She denies any recent travel, tick bites, or exposure to sick contacts. Her medical history is significant for occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, which she attributes to overuse. Her family history includes rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals a thin woman with mild pallor, without lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Her vital signs are stable except for the fever. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels, with normal white blood cell count and liver function tests. Blood cultures are negative, and chest X-ray is unremarkable. Further investigation is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She reports pain in her wrists and the small joints of her hands, which improves with activity throughout the day but worsens with inactivity. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She denies any recent infections or changes in medication. On examination, she has symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory findings reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old female presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of joint pain affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles over the past three days. She reports that the joints are swollen and painful, making it difficult to walk. She also notes a low-grade fever and fatigue. Her symptoms started shortly after returning from a vacation in the Caribbean. She denies any rash, photosensitivity, dry eyes, or mouth, and has no significant medical history. She does not smoke, and her only medication is an oral contraceptive. On examination, her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), and she has synovitis in her wrists and knees. Laboratory studies show a mild leukopenia, and elevated C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. A viral panel including tests for chikungunya, dengue, and Zika is ordered. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash and joint pain. She describes the rash as purple spots predominantly located on her lower legs. Additionally, she has noticed blood in her urine and mild abdominal pain, which she attributes to recent dietary changes. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, for which she takes over-the-counter antihistamines as needed. She denies any recent infections, fevers, or new medications. On physical examination, palpable purpura on the lower extremities is noted, and she has tenderness in the small joints of her hands and knees. Urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of widespread musculoskeletal pain for the past 8 months, accompanied by fatigue and difficulty sleeping. She describes the pain as persistent and diffuse, affecting both sides of her body, including her shoulders, neck, and back. She reports feeling unrefreshed after sleep and often wakes up with stiffness. Her physical activity has decreased significantly due to the pain. She denies any significant joint swelling or redness. She has a history of anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. Her current medications include sertraline and over-the-counter ibuprofen. Family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has tender points in multiple muscle groups, but no synovitis or significant joint inflammation is noted. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein levels. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are negative.

Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of increasing fatigue, mild joint pain, and new-onset hypertension. She has also noticed swelling around her ankles and occasional shortness of breath. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/95 mm Hg, and there is bilateral pitting edema in her lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL and mild proteinuria. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows normal size and echotexture. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past two months. She also reports morning stiffness lasting around an hour, which feels better as the day progresses. Additionally, she has noticed swelling around her eyes and mild discomfort in her wrists and knees. She denies any recent dietary changes, fever, or significant changes in her energy levels. On examination, she has periorbital edema and slight puffiness in her hands. Laboratory tests reveal normal thyroid function tests, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and low serum albumin. Urinalysis shows proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman visits her physician with complaints of chronic dry eyes and a dry mouth for the past 8 months. She has difficulty swallowing dry foods and needs frequent sips of water throughout the day. Additionally, she notes swelling near her jaw which has persisted for several months. She experiences fatigue and mild joint discomfort, particularly in her hands and wrists. She denies any recent weight loss, fever, or night sweats. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement and mild tenderness in the temporomandibular joints. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Testing for anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of unexplained weight gain over the last three months. She also reports puffiness around her eyes, particularly in the morning, and joint stiffness that lasts about an hour each day. She denies changes in appetite, but she has felt unusually tired and has noticed her rings fitting more tightly. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and mild tenderness in the joints of her hands, but no erythema or warmth. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and proteinuria on urinalysis. Which of the following conditions should be considered in the differential diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits her family physician with a rash and joint pain over the past three months. She mentions that the rash worsens after spending time in the sun and appears primarily on her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. She also reports joint pain in her wrists and knees, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting around 30 minutes. Her medical history includes recurrent mouth ulcers and fatigue, but no fevers or weight loss. Physical examination confirms the presence of a butterfly-shaped rash across her cheeks and tenderness in the affected joints. Laboratory tests show anemia and mild leukopenia. Which of the following laboratory tests is most likely to be positive in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of right shoulder pain and limited range of motion that began insidiously over the past few months. She reports difficulty reaching overhead or behind her back, and the pain has progressively worsened. She denies recent trauma, and her medical history includes mild hypertension managed with lifestyle changes. On physical examination, there is a noticeable reduction in active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, particularly in abduction and external rotation. The shoulder is not swollen or erythematous. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no significant abnormalities. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 14-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to a 2-week history of red spots on her legs and joint pain. Over the last few days, she has also noticed dark urine. She has had mild headaches and abdominal pain, but no fever or recent infections. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. There are numerous palpable purpuric lesions on her lower extremities and some mild swelling of the knees and ankles. Laboratory findings reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and a dry mouth for the past six months. She mentions that her eyes often feel gritty, and she finds it challenging to swallow dry foods without drinking water. Additionally, she reports swelling around her jawline, which she describes as uncomfortable but not painful. She has a history of intermittent joint pain in her hands, which she attributes to aging. Her family history includes rheumatoid arthritis in her mother and systemic lupus erythematosus in her sister. Vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement, dry oral mucosa, and dental caries. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8,000/mm^3, Platelet count 250,000/mm^3, ESR 45 mm/hr. Other tests show positive antinuclear antibodies and positive Anti-Ro antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of persistent nasal congestion, facial pain, and recurrent sinus infections. She reports occasional blood-tinged nasal discharge and mild dyspnea. She also mentions experiencing vague joint pain, particularly in her wrists and knees, accompanied by fatigue. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, which is managed with lisinopril. Vital signs show a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 130/85 mmHg, pulse of 72/min, and respirations of 20/min. Physical examination is significant for crusted nasal ulcers and inspiratory crackles over the lung fields. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and creatinine level of 1.9 mg/dL. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. A urinalysis reveals hematuria with red cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her legs and blood in her urine. She reports that the rash started as red spots on her lower legs and has since spread. Over the same period, she has noticed joint pain in her knees and ankles, which she attributes to hiking. She denies any recent infections or travel. On examination, there are palpable purpura on her lower extremities. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL, Leukocyte count 7,000/mm3, Platelet count 210,000/mm3. Urinalysis reveals 3+ hematuria with red cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers, fatigue, and generalized body aches. She reports intermittent swelling and tenderness of her wrists and knees. She also notes a gradual, unintended weight loss of 5 kg during this period. On examination, she has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal a leukocyte count of 15,000/mm3 with a predominance of neutrophils, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 72 mm/hr, and positive antinuclear antibodies. A chest X-ray is unremarkable for acute changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute left knee pain and swelling after participating in a weekend hiking trip. The pain began suddenly and has worsened over the past two days, making it difficult for her to walk. She reports no history of trauma or similar episodes in the past. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She denies fever, chills, or any recent infections. On examination, her left knee is swollen with an effusion and is warm to the touch. Range of motion is limited due to pain, but there is no erythema or skin changes noted. Laboratory studies reveal a white blood cell count of 8,500/mm³ (reference range: 4,500–11,000/mm³) and C-reactive protein is elevated at 15 mg/L (reference range: <10 mg/L). An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that is worse in the mornings and improves with activity throughout the day. She also reports feeling fatigued and has noticed some stiffness in her hips. Her menstrual cycles are regular. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her physician complaining of a gradual weight gain over the past 6 months, despite maintaining a consistent diet. She also reports periorbital edema, fatigue, and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, her blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, and she has noticeable puffiness around the eyes and mild non-pitting edema in her hands. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, but elevated inflammatory markers including an increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Thyroid function tests show a low free T4 with an elevated TSH level. Which of the following is most likely to be associated with this patient's presentation?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and weight gain over the past six months. She also reports feeling cold all the time and has noticed some swelling around her eyes in the morning. She denies joint pain or morning stiffness but mentions experiencing some muscle aches. Her past medical history includes iron deficiency anemia, which was treated two years ago with iron supplements. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. On physical examination, she appears slow in her movements, and her skin is cool and dry. Laboratory studies reveal a low free T4 level with an elevated TSH. Which of the following laboratory findings is most likely in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, fatigue, and joint pain affecting her knees, elbows, and wrists. She recently returned from a vacation in the Caribbean. She describes the pain as severe and debilitating, significantly limiting her daily activities. She also reports a maculopapular rash on her trunk that appeared a day before the joint symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she has a temperature of 38.3°C (100.9°F), and her joints are swollen and tender, but there is no erythema. Laboratory tests show leukopenia and thrombocytopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain and intermittent tingling in her hands for the past six months. She mentions that the pain radiates down her arms, and she occasionally experiences numbness in her fingers. The symptoms are worse in the mornings and after prolonged periods of looking downwards. She reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in her wrists and fingers, but denies any recent trauma to the neck or upper extremities. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, controlled on lisinopril, and she works as an accountant. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in the cervical spine, and Spurling's test is positive. Tinel's sign is negative at the wrist. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, elevated ESR, and high levels of C-reactive protein. Imaging of the cervical spine reveals degenerative changes and mild foraminal narrowing at C5-C6. What is the most likely diagnosis for her upper extremity symptoms?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of difficulty walking and tripping over her foot. She reports that her right foot seems to drop when she walks, making it hard to move around. She also mentions intermittent numbness and tingling in the dorsum of her right foot and lateral aspect of the leg. She denies any trauma or recent illnesses. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and obesity. Physical examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and diminished sensation in the L5 dermatome. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are intact. EMG and nerve conduction studies are ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects for the past 3 months. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has experienced some weight gain. She denies any recent infections or changes in medication. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, she has symmetric proximal muscle weakness in the shoulders and hips, but no joint tenderness or swelling. Her skin is clear without rashes. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,300/mm3
Creatine kinase 850 U/L
AST 45 U/L
ALT 40 U/L
TSH 1.8 µIU/mL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past 8 months. She describes the pain as primarily affecting her hands, wrists, and knees symmetrically, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She has had difficulty performing daily tasks due to the swelling and tenderness in her joints. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. On examination, there is evidence of synovial swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory testing reveals a normocytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 56 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. A hand X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 7 kg (15 lbs). She reports persistent swelling in her neck and under her arms. She denies any recent infections, but states she has experienced joint pain that migrates and typically worsens in the morning. She also describes a recurrent itchy skin rash that appears on her legs. On physical examination, there is non-tender, firm lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A biopsy of an enlarged lymph node is planned. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with a 3-month history of progressively worsening difficulty walking. She describes a sensation of weakness in her lower legs and occasional numbness in her feet. She denies any pain or recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, both well-controlled with medications. Her family history includes her mother who had rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has decreased strength (3/5) in bilateral hip and knee flexors, and hyporeflexia in the Achilles tendon reflexes. Sensory examination reveals decreased vibration sense in her feet. Laboratory tests show mild anemia with normal inflammatory markers, and her thyroid function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic with worsening back pain for the past two weeks. She describes the pain as sharp and severe, particularly when sitting or standing. She denies any trauma or falls. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis. She also reports generalized fatigue and some stiffness in her fingers, especially in the morning. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the thoracic and lumbar spine. An X-ray of the spine shows a compression fracture of the L3 vertebra. Laboratory tests reveal: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 6,200/mm3, Calcium 9.8 mg/dL, Phosphorus 3.4 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 95 U/L, and C-reactive protein 10 mg/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Vertebral compression fracture due to osteoporosis

question: A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic sinus congestion and difficulty breathing over the past 6 months. She reports experiencing persistent nasal discharge and has had several episodes of sinusitis that did not completely resolve with antibiotics. Additionally, she mentions fatigue and general malaise. Over the past few weeks, she has noticed decreased urinary output and swelling in her ankles. Past medical history is insignificant, and she denies smoking or alcohol use. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sinuses and bilateral crackles in the lung bases. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen, as well as anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. A chest X-ray reveals patchy pulmonary infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis based on this presentation?

A 68-year-old woman comes to the emergency department complaining of severe back pain that started suddenly while she was getting up from a chair. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her lower back, without radiation to the legs. She denies any trauma or recent falls. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis, for which she takes alendronate. She also has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. She reports occasional joint stiffness in her hands, but no swelling or redness. On physical examination, she appears to be in moderate distress due to pain. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 130/82 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 20/min. Tenderness is noted over the thoracic and lumbar spine, and there is decreased range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is unremarkable. An X-ray of the spine shows a compression fracture of the L1 vertebra. What is the best next step in management for this patient?

options.C: Order an MRI of the spine
options.D: Refer to rheumatology for suspected rheumatoid arthritis
options.E: Prescribe pain management and continue osteoporosis treatment

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain that has been present for the past year. She reports that the pain is particularly severe in the morning and improves slightly with physical activity. She also notes a sensation of stiffness in her lower back and hips upon waking, which lasts for more than an hour. The patient denies any recent trauma or history of infections but mentions experiencing occasional fatigue. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On examination, there is limited flexion of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), while rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She also reports experiencing low-grade fevers and fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent trauma. She has not experienced morning stiffness in her hands or wrists but does mention occasional knee discomfort. On physical examination, there is tenderness over both hip joints with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal a mild elevation in erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Plain radiographs of the hips show joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, generalized weakness, and joint pain in her wrists and knees over the past 3 months. She reports occasional episodes of dizziness and easy bruising. She denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, the patient appears pale with mild tenderness and swelling in the wrists and knees. Laboratory testing shows hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), white blood cell count of 3,000/mm³ (reference range: 4,500-11,000/mm³), and platelet count of 100,000/mm³ (reference range: 150,000-350,000/mm³). Her serum creatinine and liver function tests are within normal limits. The peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia with no blasts or abnormal cells. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her condition?

A 26-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent lower back pain for the past 2 months. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity throughout the day. She reports occasional pain in her buttocks that alternates sides. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. She has a family history of a 'spinal disease' in her father. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Her vital signs are within normal limits. A laboratory workup shows elevated C-reactive protein but normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a month-long history of fatigue, joint pain in her wrists and knees, and episodes of low-grade fever. She mentions feeling extremely tired even after a full night's sleep and has noticed some weight loss despite a stable appetite. Her past medical history includes a single cesarean section, and she takes a daily multivitamin. On examination, she appears pale and has palpable hepatosplenomegaly. Recent laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 10.4 g/dL, white blood cell count of 3,000/mm³, and platelet count of 90,000/mm³. Additional tests show elevated liver enzymes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint stiffness, especially in the morning. She reports a significant weight gain of 10 pounds over the past two months despite no changes in her diet or physical activity. Additionally, she has noticed swelling around her eyes upon waking. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears lethargic, with notable periorbital edema and mild puffiness of the hands. Vital signs are: temperature 36.7°C (98.1°F), blood pressure 112/70 mmHg, pulse 64/min, and respirations 18/min. Laboratory studies reveal elevated TSH and low free T4 levels. Which of the following is most likely related to the cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic for joint pain and a rash that developed after a recent camping trip in the northeastern United States. She mentions that she noticed a tick embedded in her skin about three weeks ago. Initially, she experienced fatigue and generalized muscle aches, which were followed by swelling and pain in her knees and wrists. She also developed a bull's-eye rash on her abdomen. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 115/70 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals mild swelling of the knees and a well-demarcated erythematous rash with central clearing. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past year. She notes that she frequently wakes up at night due to oral dryness and her eyes often feel gritty. She also describes a sensation of fullness in the area just below her ears. She denies any significant past medical history, except for occasional joint aches in her hands and knees. On examination, her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure is 112/70 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 18/min. There is bilateral parotid gland enlargement on palpation. Laboratory tests reveal positive anti-Ro (SSA) antibodies and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling following a weekend hiking trip. She describes the pain as sharp and worsening with movement. She has no history of trauma to the knee and denies any fever or systemic symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraines treated with over-the-counter ibuprofen. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, the right knee is swollen with a moderate effusion, and there is tenderness on palpation over the joint line. Range of motion is limited due to pain. Her vital signs are stable, and laboratory studies reveal a white blood cell count of 7,500/mm³, ESR of 20 mm/hr, and CRP of 2.1 mg/L. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 34-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and a recent onset of foot drop on the right side. She mentions that she has been experiencing generalized fatigue and occasional muscle cramps for the past few months. She also reports mild, intermittent joint pain in her knees and fingers. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies and she is currently taking loratadine as needed. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the right lower leg. Reflexes are 2+ throughout except for the right ankle, which is diminished. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that began after a recent viral illness. She reports that the viral symptoms resolved after about a week, but she has since felt persistent muscle pain and low energy. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She denies fever, rash, or joint swelling. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits, but she appears tired. The musculoskeletal exam reveals tenderness on palpation of the trapezius, lower back, and upper arms without swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 15 mm/hr (reference range: 0-20 mm/hr), and normal thyroid function tests. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. She describes a gritty sensation in her eyes and difficulty chewing dry foods. She reports bilateral parotid gland swelling and occasional joint pain without significant morning stiffness. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, she appears well-nourished with enlarged, non-tender parotid glands. Examination of the joints reveals mild tenderness without swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests show:

Serum:
Na+: 140 mEq/L
K+: 4.2 mEq/L
Cl-: 102 mEq/L
HCO3-: 24 mEq/L
BUN: 14 mg/dL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL
ESR: 30 mm/hr

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of skin discoloration on her legs and recurrent episodes of painful swelling in her calves. She reports a history of several miscarriages in the past and is concerned about her ability to conceive. She denies any recent infections or travel history. Her medication history includes oral contraceptives, which she stopped taking 6 months ago. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits, and a reticular, lacy rash is noted over the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal thrombocytopenia and prolonged prothrombin time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a rash and blood in her urine for the past week. She reports the rash began as small red spots on her lower legs and has progressively worsened. She also notes joint pain in her knees and ankles, which she attributes to a recent hiking trip. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes loratadine as needed. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower extremities and mild swelling in the knees. Urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers that have been appearing for the past two years. She also reports occasional genital ulcers and complains of joint pain in the knees and wrists. Her symptoms seem to flare periodically and resolve spontaneously. She denies any fever or weight loss. On physical examination, there are shallow, well-demarcated ulcers on the oral mucosa and similar lesions in the genital area. No skin rashes or nodules are noted. Laboratory tests reveal anemia with hemoglobin of 10 g/dL and elevated ESR at 35 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 70-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past several months. She is postmenopausal and reports no significant trauma or injury. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the pelvis and lower extremities. Laboratory tests show elevated alkaline phosphatase levels, but normal calcium and phosphate levels. A recent bone density test indicated decreased bone mineral density. A skeletal survey is performed and reveals cortical thickening and enlargement of the affected bones. Which of the following conditions is most likely contributing to this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic due to fatigue and joint pain over the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her wrists and fingers. She feels more tired than usual and has been unable to participate in her regular yoga classes. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent travel. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show normocytic anemia, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes the sensation as a 'pins and needles' feeling that is particularly bothersome at night. She also mentions feeling fatigued and having occasional joint stiffness, primarily in the morning. She denies any recent infections or new medications. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, for which she takes metformin. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory results show normal electrolytes, normal renal function, and a hemoglobin A1c of 8.1%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 65-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-month history of generalized bone pain, particularly affecting her pelvis and lower back. She has been postmenopausal for 15 years and has a history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in her physical activity. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show elevated alkaline phosphatase levels, while serum calcium and phosphate are within normal ranges. A bone scan reveals increased uptake in the pelvis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old female presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of persistent morning stiffness and swelling in her fingers and wrists for the past six months. She reports the symptoms improve slightly with activity as the day progresses. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent infections, fevers, or constitutional symptoms. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with mild ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody levels. Radiographs of her hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.B: Refer to orthopedics for joint replacement
options.F: Perform arthrocentesis for synovial fluid analysis

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive skin tightening on her fingers and intermittent episodes of color changes in her fingers upon exposure to cold. She describes the fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red as they warm up. She has difficulty opening jars and notes fatigue and morning stiffness. She denies any significant past medical history or family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, the skin of her hands appears thickened and shiny. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) assay with a nucleolar pattern. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in her knees, wrists, and ankles, which developed acutely over the past three days. She reports swelling and redness in these joints, as well as morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Her symptoms started shortly after returning from a hiking trip in the Appalachian Mountains. She denies a history of joint issues. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 99.3°F (37.4°C), blood pressure is 112/70 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. There is notable swelling and tenderness in her knees, wrists, and ankles, and some erythema is present. Laboratory tests reveal a white blood cell count of 9,000/mm³, elevated ESR, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic neck pain and tingling in her right arm and fingers for the past 6 months. She notes that the neck pain worsens with certain movements and is sometimes accompanied by headaches. The paresthesias are intermittent but seem to increase when she turns her head or looks down for prolonged periods. She has a history of hypothyroidism and takes levothyroxine. She works at a desk job and reports no recent trauma. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and tenderness over the cervical paraspinal muscles. Spurling's test reproduces the tingling sensation in her arm. Reflexes and muscle strength are normal. An MRI of the cervical spine shows mild degenerative disc disease at the C5-C6 level. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

options.A: Cervical radiculopathy due to herniated disc

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 7-month history of bilateral hip pain. She states that the pain is most severe in the morning and gradually improves as the day progresses. She has also experienced stiffness in her lower back. She reports occasional fatigue but denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for psoriasis, for which she uses topical corticosteroids. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in both hips and mild swelling of the fingers. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An X-ray of the pelvis reveals joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of diffuse bone pain that started 3 months ago. She has a history of postmenopausal osteoporosis, managed with calcium and vitamin D supplements. She mentions that her pain is most intense in her hips and thighs, and over-the-counter pain medications have not provided relief. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. The patient has noticed some fatigue but denies morning stiffness. Physical examination reveals tenderness over her pelvis and tibias. Laboratory studies show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level of 420 U/L (N: 30-120 U/L), normal serum calcium, and normal parathyroid hormone levels. A recent bone scan shows increased uptake in the pelvis and femur. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She notes that the symptoms are symmetric, affecting her wrists, fingers, and knees. The joint pain is most severe in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving. She reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fever. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. The patient's vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h and a C-reactive protein level of 22 mg/L. Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are also positive.

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Order an MRI of the hands and wrists
options.G: Refer to a rheumatologist for further evaluation

question: A 29-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of chronic lower back pain. She describes the pain as persistent and notes it is especially severe in the morning, improving slightly with movement throughout the day. She also reports feeling fatigued and experiencing occasional stiffness in her hips. She denies any recent trauma or specific injuries. Her medical history is significant for occasional episodes of bilateral heel pain. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility, and the Schober test shows reduced lumbar flexion. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and ESR levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past month. She reports persistent tiredness that is not relieved by sleep and aching pain mainly in her wrists and knees. She also notices small red spots on her skin and has had episodes of nosebleeds. She has no significant past medical history and denies any recent infections or travel. Her menstrual periods are regular. Physical examination reveals pallor, mild hepatosplenomegaly, and petechiae on her lower extremities. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 88/min, blood pressure is 112/68 mm Hg, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 2,800/mm3
Platelet count 45,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
Antinuclear antibodies: Positive
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of extreme fatigue and unintended weight loss of 15 pounds over the last three months. She also reports episodes of mild joint pain in her hands and wrists, but attributes this to her recent increase in workload as an office clerk. She denies any changes in bowel habits, fever, or night sweats. Her past medical history includes treated iron deficiency anemia, with her last hematology follow-up six months ago showing stable hemoglobin levels. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests are ordered and reveal:

Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL
MCV: 80 fL
Ferritin: 8 ng/mL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): 35 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor: 55 IU/mL

Based on these findings, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Colonoscopy to rule out gastrointestinal blood loss

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician complaining of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 4 months. She reports feeling increasingly tired despite adequate sleep and has lost 10 kg (22 pounds) without any changes to her diet or exercise routine. She has a history of iron deficiency anemia, for which she takes oral iron supplements. However, recent blood tests reveal persistent anemia with a hemoglobin level of 9.2 g/dL and low mean corpuscular volume. She also reports occasional joint stiffness in the morning that resolves within 20 minutes. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild hepatosplenomegaly but no lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of numbness and tingling in her feet and hands, which has been progressively worsening. She also reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees but denies any significant swelling or morning stiffness. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with metformin, and takes no other medications. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her blood pressure is 128/78 mm Hg, heart rate is 70/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin A1c of 7.5%. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 56-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of tingling and numbness in both of her feet and hands for the past few months. She describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' and notes it has been progressively worsening. She also reports mild joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her fingers, which is less severe than the numbness. Her medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, which is managed with metformin. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation in a stocking-glove distribution and mild tenderness in several proximal interphalangeal joints without significant swelling. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin A1c of 8.2%. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of swelling and intense pain in her left big toe over the past year. Each episode lasts about a week and seems to occur after she consumes seafood or red wine. She denies morning stiffness or symmetrical joint pain. On examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness at the metatarsophalangeal joint of the left big toe, and a small, firm nodule is noted on the helix of the right ear. Her medical history includes hypertension, treated with hydrochlorothiazide, and she has a family history of similar joint problems in her father. Laboratory studies show a serum uric acid level of 9.0 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic for new-onset hypertension. She reports increasing fatigue and swelling in her feet over the past month. She also notes occasional pain and stiffness in her hands, particularly in the morning, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for obesity and hyperlipidemia. She takes atorvastatin and amlodipine. On examination, her blood pressure is 162/98 mm Hg, and she has mild bilateral pitting edema. Laboratory tests reveal a serum creatinine level of 1.6 mg/dL (increased from 1.0 mg/dL six months ago), estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) of 45 mL/min/1.73 m², and a urine dipstick positive for protein. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her hypertension and renal abnormalities?

options.C: Rheumatoid arthritis with secondary renal involvement

question: A 41-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent neck pain and tingling in her right arm and hand. She describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' and notes that it worsens when she works at her computer for extended periods. She has also experienced occasional headaches and fatigue but denies any fever, weight loss, or recent trauma. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and diminished strength in her right hand grip. Spurling's maneuver reproduces the tingling sensation down her right arm. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and weakness in her right foot that has progressively worsened over the past month. She first noticed the problem after a long day of hiking, but initially attributed the weakness to fatigue. She describes trips and falls due to her 'foot dragging' and has since noticed increasing difficulty lifting her right foot. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine, and she denies any recent trauma, fever, or systemic symptoms. She works as a graphic designer and is physically active.

On examination, her vital signs are stable. Neurological examination reveals a foot drop with decreased dorsiflexion strength of the right foot, with 1/5 muscle strength. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric, and there is no fasciculation or muscle atrophy. Gait analysis shows a steppage gait pattern. Blood tests, including ESR and CRP, are within normal limits.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse bone pain and generalized fatigue for the past 3 months. She mentions experiencing increased thirst and frequent urination. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. The patient denies any recent trauma or changes in physical activity. Family history is unremarkable. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness over the back and legs with no swelling or erythema. Laboratory results show elevated serum calcium levels and a low parathyroid hormone level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of skin lesions and joint pain. She notes that over the past 6 months, she has developed silvery scale patches on her elbows and knees, which are sometimes itchy. She also reports joint swelling predominantly in her right hand and left knee, with occasional morning stiffness that lasts about an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she has no family history of autoimmune conditions. Physical examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales on extensor surfaces, dactylitis of the right index finger, and a limited range of motion in the left knee. There is no notable nodularity, and her nails show pitting. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely risk factor that predisposed this patient to her condition?

A 29-year-old woman is evaluated in the clinic for a 1-week history of purplish spots on her legs and blood in her urine. She denies any recent infections or travel history. She reports mild joint pain and fatigue but denies fever or weight loss. She has no significant past medical history and is not taking any medications. On examination, there are palpable purpuric lesions on the lower extremities. Laboratory tests show normal platelet count, elevated serum creatinine, and microscopic hematuria. Serum complement levels are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with new-onset hypertension and fatigue. She reports that over the past month, she has experienced increasing fatigue, occasional headaches, and mild joint pain in her hands and wrists. She denies any significant weight loss, fevers, or skin rashes. Her medical history is notable for hyperlipidemia, for which she takes atorvastatin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 160/95 mmHg, slight pallor, and bilateral mild ankle edema. There is no skin rash, and joint examination reveals no swelling or tenderness. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL, leukocyte count 5,400/mm3, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 38 mg/dL, and urinalysis shows proteinuria (2+) with no casts. Which of the following is the most likely next step in the management of this patient?

A 29-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash that appears to worsen with sun exposure. She also reports joint pain in her hands and knees, as well as mouth sores. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She denies recent illness or travel. On examination, she has an erythematous malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds and mild swelling of the MCP joints. Oral examination reveals shallow ulcers on the hard palate. Laboratory results show:

Hb: 11.0 g/dL
WBC: 8,500/mm3
Platelets: 150,000/mm3
ESR: 45 mm/hr
ANA: Positive (1:320 titer)
Anti-dsDNA: Positive
RF: Negative

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past 8 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that occasionally radiates to her thighs, and it is exacerbated by physical activity such as walking or climbing stairs. The patient denies any trauma or injury to her hips. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteopenia, for which she takes lisinopril and calcium supplements, respectively. She reports some morning stiffness lasting for about 30 minutes, but denies any fever, weight loss, or generalized fatigue. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the greater trochanteric region bilaterally and limited range of motion in both hips due to pain. Laboratory studies reveal mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fever for the past week. She reports having had a mild viral illness with sore throat and cough two weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. She denies any joint swelling but mentions mild stiffness in the morning that improves with movement. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Her vital signs include: temperature 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, heart rate 78/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Physical examination is notable for tender points in multiple muscle groups without synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal complete blood count (CBC). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers for the past 4 months. She also reports episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon exposure to cold. She has noticed some joint stiffness in the morning that lasts for about an hour. Her medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, her fingers appear shiny and tight. There are no signs of synovitis in the joints. Laboratory studies show a positive ANA and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care provider with complaints of burning sensations and tingling in her feet and hands for the past 3 weeks. She describes the symptoms as gradually worsening, particularly at night, which disrupts her sleep. She denies any recent trauma to her limbs or changes in her physical activity. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, for which she takes metformin. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, she has decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Her gait is unsteady, and she exhibits mild weakness in dorsiflexion of the left foot. Laboratory tests show normal blood glucose levels. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

options.A: Peripheral neuropathy due to vitamin B12 deficiency

question:
A 62-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is worse with activity and improves with rest. She denies any morning stiffness but mentions feeling fatigued lately. The patient has a history of hypertension and dyslipidemia, both well-controlled with medication. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, there is a reduced range of motion in both hips, and she exhibits an antalgic gait. No swelling or erythema is noted in the examined joints. Her vital signs are stable, and laboratory tests reveal: 
C-reactive protein 3 mg/L 
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 22 mm/hr 
Liver function tests are within normal limits. 
Which of the following is the most likely next step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Order magnetic resonance imaging of the hips
options.B: Initiate physical therapy for hip strengthening exercises
options.C: Refer to a rheumatologist for suspected systemic disease
options.F: Consider a bone density scan for osteoporosis
options.G: Conduct a hip joint aspiration and analysis

question: A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She reports accompanying fatigue and low-grade fevers. The patient had recently returned from a business trip to the Caribbean. She denies any recent sore throat, rashes, or urinary symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes only a daily multivitamin. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the wrists and knees, with mild warmth and erythema. Her vital signs are: temperature 37.8°C (100°F), heart rate 76/min, blood pressure 120/78 mm Hg. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and weight gain over the past 6 months. She also reports feeling cold more often than usual and has experienced occasional neck discomfort but no significant pain. She denies any changes in appetite or diet. Her medical history is significant for mild depression for which she occasionally takes sertraline. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, her temperature is 36.7°C (98.1°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, pulse is 64/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Her thyroid gland is diffusely enlarged but non-tender. Laboratory tests reveal a TSH level of 15 mIU/L (normal range: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) and free T4 of 0.6 ng/dL (normal range: 0.8-1.7 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of skin lesions and joint pain. She reports that over the past 4 months, she has developed red, scaly plaques on her elbows and knees. These lesions have been intermittently itchy. Additionally, she has experienced pain and swelling in her fingers, which seems to affect different joints at different times, predominantly on one side of her body. She denies any morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. She has no significant past medical history, and her family history is notable for a father with psoriasis. On examination, there are well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales on her elbows and knees, and tenderness in the distal interphalangeal joints of her right hand. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-week history of generalized bone pain and fatigue. She notes that the pain is worse in her back and hips and that it occasionally wakes her at night. She has a history of hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine. She has no personal history of fractures or recent trauma. Her mother had a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the lumbar spine and hips. Laboratory studies show:
Calcium 11.4 mg/dL
Phosphate 2.4 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 150 U/L
Parathyroid hormone 90 pg/mL (elevated)
25-Hydroxyvitamin D 15 ng/mL (low)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the office because of progressive difficulty walking and lower extremity weakness over the past 3 months. She mentions that she has also experienced occasional joint pain and fatigue. She has no significant medical history and currently takes no medications. She denies recent infections or trauma. Family history is significant for multiple sclerosis in her mother. On examination, she has decreased muscle strength (3/5) in both legs and slightly increased deep tendon reflexes. Babinski sign is negative bilaterally. Sensation is intact, and there are no cerebellar signs. A complete blood count is normal, while her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth. She reports that her eyes often feel gritty, and she frequently needs to sip water throughout the day due to her dry mouth. She also mentions occasional discomfort in her joints, particularly in her hands and knees. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, and she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, her vital signs are stable. Notably, there is bilateral parotid gland enlargement without tenderness. Her oral mucosa appears dry, and there are no oral ulcers. A Schirmer's test is performed and shows decreased tear production. Laboratory tests reveal a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA). Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of difficulty walking and a sensation of weakness in her right foot. She reports tripping over small objects and is unable to dorsiflex her right foot effectively. She has a history of recurrent shoulder and hip pain over the past year, but no joint swelling or morning stiffness. She denies recent trauma or changes in sensation. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and a steppage gait. Deep tendon reflexes are normal, and there is no muscle atrophy or deformity. Imaging of the right leg shows no evidence of fracture or dislocation. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse muscle and joint aches, fatigue, and low-grade fever for the past week. She recently recovered from an upper respiratory tract infection that resolved without antibiotics. She denies any swelling or significant stiffness in her joints. Her past medical history includes mild asthma, for which she occasionally uses an inhaler. She is otherwise healthy. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. There is no joint swelling, but she winces with palpation over her shoulders and hips. Laboratory findings include a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain, fatigue, and a 6-month history of easy bruising. She reports that her joint pain is intermittent, affecting her wrists and knees, and is usually accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She also mentions having frequent nosebleeds and feeling unusually tired despite adequate rest. On examination, there are petechiae on her extremities, mild pallor, and swollen wrists. Laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia with hemoglobin at 9 g/dL, a white blood cell count of 2,500 cells/mm³, and platelets at 80,000/mm³. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a persistent fever for the past 3 weeks. She describes the fevers as being higher in the evenings, reaching up to 101.3°F (38.5°C). She also complains of fatigue, loss of appetite, and unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds (2.3 kg) over the last month. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or changes in her routine. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, her temperature is 100.8°F (38.2°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly, but she has mild tenderness over the left costovertebral angle. Laboratory studies show a mild anemia, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and a C-reactive protein (CRP) of 40 mg/L. A chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of diffuse body aches and fatigue. She reports recovering from a viral illness characterized by fever and a sore throat about one month ago. Since then, she has been experiencing pain in her muscles and joints, but the pain does not seem to be localized to any specific area. She feels extremely tired, even after a full night's sleep, and has difficulty concentrating. She denies any swelling or redness in her joints. She takes no medications and has no significant past medical history. On examination, her vital signs are normal. She has tenderness in her muscles upon palpation but no swelling or erythema of the joints. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,800/mm3
Platelet count 240,000/mm3
CRP 2 mg/L (normal < 1 mg/L)
ESR 28 mm/h
Which of the following is the most likely cause of her current symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy presents with low back pain and pelvic discomfort for the past two months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, intensifying after prolonged standing or walking. She denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness lasting more than 15 minutes. There are no neurological symptoms and she has no history of trauma. Her prenatal course has been unremarkable, and she is otherwise healthy. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and a slight waddling gait. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Physical therapy focused on pelvic floor exercises
options.B: Magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine
options.D: Use of a pregnancy support belt

question:
A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain and a rash on her face. She describes the rash as red and covering her cheeks and the bridge of her nose, which worsens with sun exposure. Her joint pain is most prominent in her wrists and knees and is associated with morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She also reports feeling unusually tired and has lost 4 lb unintentionally over the past month. She denies any significant medical history but mentions her older sister has rheumatoid arthritis. Her medications include only an occasional ibuprofen for pain relief. On physical examination, she has a well-demarcated erythematous rash over the malar areas sparing the nasolabial folds, and tenderness in the wrists and knees without swelling. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
Leukocytes 4,200/mm3
Platelets 170,000/mm3
ANA test Positive
Anti-dsDNA Negative
Anti-Smith Positive
Rheumatoid factor Negative
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as deep and aching, exacerbated by weight-bearing activities and improved with rest. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in activity level.
