Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She reports mild fatigue but denies fever, morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes, or peripheral joint involvement. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the bilateral hip joints, with limited range of motion and pain on internal rotation. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and normal renal function. Pelvic X-rays are ordered to further evaluate the hip pain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also complains of stiffness and limited motion in her spine, which sometimes wakes her up at night. She denies any recent trauma or injuries. Her past medical history is significant for two previous episodes of uveitis. She is a non-smoker, and her family history is unremarkable. On examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and decreased lumbar flexion. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated C-reactive protein level. Radiographs of the pelvis show sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 12 pounds over the last 3 months. She reports feeling dizzy at times and has noticed her skin becoming paler. She also experiences occasional joint pain but does not recall any swelling or redness of the joints. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, she appears pale, and conjunctival pallor is noted. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 112/68 mm Hg, pulse is 102/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory investigations reveal the following:
Hemoglobin 9.2 g/dL
MCV 82 fL
Leukocyte count 5,200/mm3
Platelet count 210,000/mm3
Serum ferritin 220 ng/mL
Serum vitamin B12 150 pg/mL
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 65-year-old female presents to the emergency department with sudden onset severe back pain after slipping on her kitchen floor. She describes the pain as sharp and radiating around to her abdomen. She has a history of osteoporosis and has been non-compliant with her bisphosphonate therapy. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the mid-thoracic vertebrae. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory tests show normal calcium levels and mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase. A lateral X-ray of the thoracic spine reveals a wedge-shaped deformity of the T8 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of fatigue and an unintentional weight loss of 4.5 kg (10 lb) over the past 3 months. She also reports feeling short of breath on exertion and has noticed a pallor to her skin. She denies any joint pain or swelling. She has a history of iron deficiency anemia treated with oral supplements. On examination, her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 95/min, and blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg. She appears pale and thin. A complete blood count shows hemoglobin of 9 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 80 fL, and reticulocyte count of 2%. Further laboratory tests reveal her iron studies are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Measure serum vitamin B12 and folate levels
options.C: Test for celiac disease with tissue transglutaminase antibodies

question: A 47-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand while ice skating. She reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist, and she has been unable to move it due to severe pain. She denies any numbness or tingling in the fingers. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and osteopenia. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling at the distal radius with a visible deformity. The skin over the wrist is intact, and capillary refill time is less than 2 seconds. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered and shows a distal radius fracture. Which of the following is most likely associated with this patient’s presentation?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of chronic lower back pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. The pain has progressively worsened over the past six months, and she now experiences stiffness in her lower back that lasts for over an hour after waking. She denies any recent trauma or fever. The patient reports occasional pain in her hips and shoulders, which also seems to improve with movement. There is no significant family history of autoimmune disorders, and she denies any skin rashes or eye symptoms. Physical examination reveals reduced mobility of the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show: ESR 45 mm/hr, CRP 10 mg/L. HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.A: Order MRI of the sacroiliac joints
options.F: Refer to rheumatology for possible ankylosing spondylitis

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her physician with a 6-month history of painful, swollen joints in both hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour each day and difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning her shirt. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On examination, there is tenderness and synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that have persisted for two weeks following a viral upper respiratory infection. She reports that her symptoms have been worsening, and she now experiences difficulty with daily activities due to muscle and joint pain. She denies fever, weight loss, rashes, or morning stiffness. Her medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, she appears well-nourished and in no acute distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. There is no joint swelling or erythema, and she has full range of motion without tenderness. Laboratory studies reveal normal complete blood count and renal function. C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Prescribe a short course of corticosteroids

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain and skin lesions that have been worsening over the past 3 months. She describes the joint pain as intermittent but notes that it primarily affects her right knee and left ankle. She also reports feeling fatigued and has noticed new scaly skin patches on her elbows and knees. She denies any eye symptoms but mentions occasional swelling of the fingers. She has not sought prior medical attention for these symptoms. Her medical history is significant for a normal childbirth 5 years ago. Her vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knees. Her right knee and left ankle are slightly swollen with limited range of motion. Laboratory studies show a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg. She has also noticed increased pallor and occasional dizziness. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies gastrointestinal bleeding and her menstrual periods have been regular. On examination, she appears pale, with a blood pressure of 120/78 mmHg, heart rate of 92/min, and a respiratory rate of 18/min. On laboratory evaluation, her hemoglobin is 9.8 g/dL, MCV is 75 fL, and serum ferritin is low. Anti-nuclear antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a new rash on her ankles and wrists. She reports being bitten by a tick about two weeks ago while hiking in a wooded area. Since then, she has experienced fatigue and low-grade fevers. The rash is described as red and circular, and it has been expanding over the past few days. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and an ESR of 45 mm/hr. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in her wrists and knees. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her right big toe over the past year. She describes the episodes as sudden in onset, often waking her from sleep, and says the joint becomes red and extremely tender, making it difficult to walk. These episodes last a few days and resolve spontaneously. She has also noticed firm nodules on the outer ear and along her finger joints. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She drinks alcohol occasionally and denies smoking. On examination, there is a swollen, tender right first metatarsophalangeal joint and subcutaneous nodules on the auricle and fingers. Which of the following is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?

options.A: Measurement of serum uric acid levels
options.B: Analysis of synovial fluid for monosodium urate crystals
options.E: X-ray imaging of the affected joints
options.F: Testing for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies

question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and a 3-week history of joint pain, particularly affecting her knees and ankles. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her past medical history is significant for mild asthma. She is currently taking an inhaled corticosteroid and an albuterol inhaler as needed. On examination, the nodules are erythematous, tender, and consistent with erythema nodosum. Her vital signs are normal, and there is no lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a two-week history of joint pain that began in her knees and then migrated to her shoulders, elbows, and wrists. She describes the pain as moderate and associated with morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She has also experienced fever and fatigue. Two weeks ago, she had a sore throat that resolved without treatment. Her past medical history is significant for asthma, managed with inhalers. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the wrists and elbows, with no visible deformities. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), blood pressure is 120/76 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Anti-streptolysin O titers are positive. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with gradual onset of right shoulder pain and difficulty moving her arm for the past 3 months. She does not recall any specific injury. The pain is worse at night and disrupts her sleep. She also reports some mild stiffness in her shoulders that seems to be worsening. She denies swelling, redness, or fever. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She has no family history of autoimmune disease. 

On examination, her right shoulder shows reduced range of motion in all directions, particularly with external rotation and abduction. There is tenderness over the deltoid region but no obvious swelling or erythema. Neurovascular examination of the upper extremities is normal.

What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, stiffness, and swelling. She notes that her symptoms are worse in the morning with stiffness lasting over an hour, improving as the day progresses. She also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, as well as subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of both forearms. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and a few subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms that developed over the past two months. She also reports a moderate degree of weight loss and occasional low-grade fevers during this period. Her medical history is remarkable for chronic sinusitis. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Physical examination reveals multiple firm, non-tender nodules over the forearms and elbows. There is no significant lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests show a mild anemia with hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL), an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 72 mm/hr (reference range: 0–20 mm/hr), and normal liver and kidney function tests. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in both legs. She describes occasional muscle cramps and mild joint pain in her hands and wrists. Her medical history includes controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies numbness or tingling in her extremities. On examination, her vital signs are normal, and muscle strength is 3/5 in both lower extremities with normal upper extremity strength. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests reveal a mild elevation in creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with pain and swelling in her right big toe that started suddenly last night. She notes that similar episodes have occurred three times in the past year, each time affecting different joints, including her left ankle and knee. On examination, the right first toe is red, warm, and tender to touch. The patient has visible nodules on her ear helices. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, controlled with lisinopril and atorvastatin. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Laboratory tests show elevated serum uric acid levels and a normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Order an x-ray of the affected joint
options.E: Aspiration and analysis of synovial fluid

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue, bone pain, and recent episodes of nausea and constipation. She mentions that the bone pain is particularly prominent in her lower back and hips. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteoporosis treated with calcium supplements. She denies any recent trauma, infection, or significant weight loss. On physical examination, she is alert but appears fatigued. Her blood pressure is 132/86 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum calcium level, low serum phosphate, and normal renal function. A bone scan shows multiple areas of increased uptake. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her mother due to a new onset of purpura on her lower extremities and abdominal pain for the past two days. She also reports joint pain and has noticed dark-colored urine. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies recent trauma or medication use. On examination, she has palpable purpura on her legs and mild tenderness over her abdomen with no rebound tenderness. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 112/68 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 6-month history of scaly, erythematous patches on her scalp and elbows. She reports that approximately 3 months ago, she started experiencing pain and swelling in her right knee and left ankle, which sometimes makes it difficult to walk. She describes her joint pain as being worse in the morning but improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, there are well-demarcated plaques with overlying silvery scale on her scalp, elbows, and knees. Her right knee and left ankle are swollen with mild tenderness on palpation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain that has migrated from her knees to her ankles, and now affects her elbows. She initially developed a sore throat one month ago, which resolved on its own. She describes the joint pain as achy and notes associated swelling and warmth, though no redness. She has had low-grade fevers and feels generally fatigued. Physical examination reveals synovitis in the elbows and ankles, with mild swelling in these areas. Cardiac auscultation reveals a new systolic murmur. Her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with acute left knee pain and swelling for two days. She reports that the pain began after she completed a 5-mile run, which she does not do regularly. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies and mild asthma. She denies any recent injuries, fever, or joint pain elsewhere. On examination, her left knee is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation, with a reduced range of motion due to pain. An aspiration of the knee reveals synovial fluid that is turbid with elevated white blood cell count and neutrophil predominance. Her vital signs are: temperature 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure 118/78 mmHg, heart rate 84/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman visits the clinic due to joint pain and rash for 1 week. She recalls a tick bite while hiking in a wooded area 3 weeks ago. The rash began on her lower back and is now spreading to her arms and legs. She describes the joint pain as migrating and involving her knees and elbows. She has been feeling fatigued but denies any weight loss or fever. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals a distinct erythematous rash with a central clearing on her back and mild swelling of the knee joints. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and normal CRP. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent lower back pain. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She reports episodes of stiffness lasting over an hour after waking up and difficulty sleeping due to discomfort. She denies any recent trauma or injury. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated C-reactive protein level. HLA-B27 testing returns positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of lower back pain and morning stiffness that improves with activity. She describes the pain as deep and aching, located primarily in the buttocks and lower back. The stiffness typically lasts for more than an hour each morning. She reports occasional fatigue and denies any recent trauma or significant weight change. Her menstrual cycles are regular, and she has no significant past medical history. On examination, her vitals are stable, and there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Schober's test is positive. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and CRP. HLA-B27 is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman is referred to a nephrologist for evaluation of worsening renal function. Over the past 6 months, she has experienced persistent sinus congestion and a chronic cough. She reports brownish sputum and occasional blood streaks. Recently, she has felt increasingly fatigued and has noted ankle swelling. She denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history includes recurrent sinus infections and a remote history of asthma, for which she rarely uses an albuterol inhaler. Physical examination reveals bilateral crackles on lung auscultation and 1+ pitting edema in both legs. Laboratory studies show an elevated creatinine level. A chest radiograph reveals diffuse pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation?

options.D: Urinalysis for red blood cell casts

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, severe fatigue, and difficulty sleeping. She describes the pain as a persistent, dull ache that is worsened by physical activity. Despite feeling exhausted, she often struggles to fall asleep or wakes frequently throughout the night. She notes difficulty concentrating and feels "foggy-headed" during the day. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there are multiple tender points over the neck, shoulders, and hips, but no swelling or erythema is noted. Her laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, normal inflammatory markers, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with recurrent episodes of joint pain and swelling primarily affecting her right great toe over the past year. She describes the pain as sudden in onset, severe, and accompanied by redness and warmth of the affected joint. The episodes typically last 3-5 days and resolve spontaneously. She has noticed small, hard nodules developing on her ears and elbows. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is currently taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any history of trauma or significant weight loss. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated serum uric acid level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her left big toe for the past 6 months. She describes these episodes as sudden in onset, often occurring overnight, and resolving spontaneously within a week. Over the past year, she has also noticed small, firm nodules developing on her left ear helix and over her elbows. She has a history of hypertension managed with hydrochlorothiazide. She denies fever, morning stiffness, or joint deformities. Her BMI is 28 kg/m2. On examination, there is erythema and tenderness over the first metatarsophalangeal joint. Laboratory studies show elevated serum uric acid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling in her hands and knees over the past few months. She also reports a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure and painful ulcers in her mouth. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, there is a notable erythematous rash across the bridge of her nose and cheeks. Her oral mucosa shows shallow ulcers. Laboratory results reveal anemia and an elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and generalized weakness that began two weeks after recovering from a viral illness characterized by fever and sore throat. She reports that her joints feel stiff, especially in the morning, but improve as the day progresses. She denies any significant joint swelling or redness. She has no significant past medical history and is not currently on any medication. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness over multiple joints without obvious swelling. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, but rheumatoid factor is negative and antinuclear antibodies are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects for the past 3 months. She also reports generalized fatigue and mild swelling of her knees. She denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness. Her medical history includes hyperlipidemia and seasonal allergies. She is on atorvastatin and loratadine. On examination, she has symmetric proximal muscle weakness, particularly in the shoulders and hips. Her deep tendon reflexes are intact and there is no evidence of joint deformities. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase and aldolase levels, with normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Electromyography reveals myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of persistent fever, generalized fatigue, and occasional night sweats. She has been experiencing mild joint pain in her hands and knees, but denies any significant swelling or morning stiffness. She has no recent travel history or known sick contacts. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F) and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory studies show:
ESR: 90 mm/hr (normal 0-20 mm/hr)
CRP: 20 mg/L (normal < 5 mg/L)
Hemoglobin: 10.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 12,000/mm3
Platelet count: 450,000/mm3
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of increasing fatigue, swelling in her legs, and headaches. She has no prior history of hypertension. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis. She has a history of migraines but no other chronic conditions. Her medications include ibuprofen as needed for headaches. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals mild periorbital edema and 2+ pitting edema in the legs. Laboratory studies show: serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL, elevated blood urea nitrogen, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h. Urinalysis reveals proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation or management of this patient?

options.A: Initiate antihypertensive therapy with an ACE inhibitor

question: A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of left shoulder pain for the past 6 months. She reports a gradual onset of pain that is worse with movement and has limited her ability to reach overhead or perform daily tasks. She denies any trauma to the shoulder. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder and a marked reduction in both active and passive range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled on lisinopril. She denies fever, weight loss, or morning stiffness affecting other joints. Recent laboratory tests show a normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling following a recreational soccer match. She describes the pain as sharp and exacerbated by movement. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, the right knee is visibly swollen with a moderate effusion, tenderness along the joint line, and limited range of motion due to pain. There is no redness or warmth, and ligamentous testing reveals no instability. An x-ray of the knee is unremarkable. Her vital signs are normal, and her body temperature is 37°C (98.6°F). Laboratory tests show normal white blood cell count and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent lower back pain for the past six months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, which improves with exercise but worsens with prolonged rest, particularly in the mornings. She notes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which gradually improves as the day progresses. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes ibuprofen as needed for pain relief. The patient denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints with limited flexion of the lumbar spine. There is no swelling or tenderness in the peripheral joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level, but rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibodies are negative. A pelvic X-ray shows sclerosis and narrowing of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening fatigue and diffuse bone pain, most pronounced in her lower back and hips. She denies any recent trauma. She experiences occasional joint discomfort in her hands, particularly in the morning, but this resolves within 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She drinks two cups of coffee daily and occasionally consumes alcohol. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the lumbar spine and mild swelling of the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory investigations show a calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL and a parathyroid hormone level of 90 pg/mL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of a rash on her elbows and knees that has been present for the past 8 months. She notes that the rash is scaly and occasionally itchy. Over the last 3 months, she has also experienced swelling and pain in several joints, predominantly affecting the fingers of her right hand and her left ankle. The joint pain is asymmetric and tends to worsen after physical activity. She denies any morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for obesity and hypertension. She does not drink alcohol or smoke. Her mother has a history of psoriasis. Examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees. Some joints of the right hand are swollen, with tenderness upon palpation. Laboratory tests reveal negative rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of unexplained weight gain, facial puffiness, and joint stiffness, particularly in the morning. She reports feeling unusually tired and has noticed the swelling is most prominent around her eyes. She denies any changes in appetite or diet. On examination, there is noticeable periorbital edema and mild pitting edema of the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal:
TSH 8.5 mIU/L
Free T4 0.7 ng/dL
Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm/h
Rheumatoid factor negative
Based on the clinical findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe left wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while gardening. She reports immediate pain and swelling, with difficulty moving her wrist. Her medical history includes osteoporosis and hypertension. On examination, there is visible deformity of the wrist with significant tenderness and swelling. An X-ray reveals a distal radius fracture. The patient also mentions having occasional morning stiffness affecting her hands and knees but denies significant joint swelling, fever, or recent weight loss. She has no other injuries from the fall. What is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's wrist injury?

options.C: Application of a splint and orthopedic referral
options.F: Initiation of calcium and vitamin D supplementation

question: A 34-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of a mottled, lace-like rash on her legs that seems to worsen in cold weather. She also reports a history of recurrent miscarriages and deep vein thrombosis. Over the past few weeks, she has experienced increasing fatigue and occasional joint pain in her hands and wrists. She denies fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for migraines and a single episode of superficial thrombophlebitis. On examination, her vital signs are normal. Her skin shows livedo reticularis on both lower extremities, and there is mild swelling of her wrists without tenderness or significant warmth. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated ESR, and positive anticardiolipin antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, headache, and swelling around the ankles. She also notes a sudden increase in blood pressure, with home measurements averaging 160/100 mm Hg. Her medical history includes chronic sinusitis and a recent episode of bronchitis. On examination, her blood pressure is 165/105 mm Hg, and there is mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal a serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL and blood urea nitrogen of 35 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein and numerous red blood cell casts. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows no evidence of hydronephrosis. Which of the following is the next best step in the management of this patient?

A 30-year-old woman visits the clinic due to a 3-month history of purplish, mottled skin discoloration on her legs, which worsens with cold exposure. She reports fatigue, episodes of swelling and tenderness in her legs, and two recent episodes of deep vein thrombosis despite being on anticoagulation therapy. Her medical history is notable for recurrent miscarriages. She has no significant family history of autoimmune diseases. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show a positive lupus anticoagulant, a normal complete blood count, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is most likely to be additionally found in the patient?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling after a hiking trip. She mentions that the pain began suddenly after she twisted her knee while descending a steep trail. Her medical history is significant for mild osteoarthritis in her hands. On examination, her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. The right knee is swollen, warm to touch, and tender with a limited range of motion. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations. Laboratory tests reveal a white blood cell count of 9,500/mm³ and a C-reactive protein level of 2.1 mg/dL. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of a rash on her lower legs, joint pain, and dark-colored urine. She describes the rash as non-itchy and purplish, primarily affecting her lower extremities. The patient also reports fatigue and occasional abdominal pain. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. On examination, there are palpable purpura on the lower legs. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 112/70 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Blood tests show elevated inflammatory markers but normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to progressive bilateral hip pain over the past six months. She describes the pain as a dull ache, which worsens with prolonged activity and is relieved by rest. She denies morning stiffness or significant swelling in other joints. Her medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus and a previous episode of deep vein thrombosis five years ago, for which she was treated with anticoagulants. She takes metformin and atorvastatin regularly. Physical examination reveals reduced range of motion in both hips, but no significant swelling or erythema. Lab investigations show normal inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A pelvic X-ray demonstrates joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation at both hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with pain and swelling in her right wrist after falling on an outstretched hand. She reports the fall occurred on a wet surface while she was walking her dog. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and osteoporosis, for which she takes lisinopril and calcium supplements. She denies any previous joint pain or stiffness. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling over the distal radius, with limited range of motion due to pain. No other joint abnormalities are noted. Her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. An X-ray of the wrist shows a distal radius fracture with minimal displacement. What is the most appropriate next step in her management?

A 45-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital for evaluation of persistent fever for the past 3 weeks. She reports associated symptoms of profound fatigue and night sweats. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or use of illicit drugs. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with metformin, and hypertension controlled with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination shows a pale and fatigued woman with a temperature of 101.5°F (38.6°C), blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg, heart rate of 88/min, and respiratory rate of 18/min. There is mild tenderness over the lower abdomen, but no palpable organomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures have been negative. A chest X-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.D: Order a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
options.E: Screen for tuberculosis with a purified protein derivative test
options.F: Perform echocardiography to evaluate for endocarditis

question: A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers over the past 6 months. She also reports cold-induced color changes in her fingers, turning white and then blue before becoming red and painful upon rewarming. She has morning stiffness in her joints that lasts about 20 minutes and experiences fatigue by the afternoon. She denies any difficulty swallowing or shortness of breath. Physical examination reveals shiny, tight skin on the fingers and several telangiectasias on her face. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and elevated antinuclear antibody (ANA) titers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chest discomfort and bone pain for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as dull and persistent, primarily affecting her pelvis and lower back. She also notes some difficulty with balance and occasional headaches but denies any weight loss or fever. She is postmenopausal and takes calcium and vitamin D supplements regularly. Her physical examination reveals mild tenderness over the hips and lower spine. Laboratory studies show elevated alkaline phosphatase levels, but calcium and phosphate levels are within normal limits. A bone scan reveals focal areas of increased uptake in the pelvis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and fatigue for the past six months. She has also noticed a red rash on her cheeks that worsens with sun exposure and oral ulcers that have been persistent despite treatment with topical agents. She reports a low-grade fever and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the past two months. She denies any gastrointestinal symptoms or respiratory issues. Her medical history is significant for a urinary tract infection a year ago. Physical examination reveals bilateral tenderness and swelling of the wrists and small joints of the hands. The malar rash is noted, and a few shallow ulcers are present on the buccal mucosa. Her laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, leukocyte count of 3,500/mm3, and a positive antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the rheumatology clinic complaining of progressive skin tightening on her fingers over the last 6 months, accompanied by episodes of discoloration of her fingers in response to cold or stress. She describes the discoloration as initially turning white, then blue, and finally red upon warming. She also reports mild joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 20 minutes, but denies any fever or weight loss. Examination reveals thickened, taut skin over the fingers (sclerodactyly). Laboratory studies show a negative rheumatoid factor and elevated antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of progressively worsening joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting over an hour, but improves with activity throughout the day. She also notes swelling in her hands and wrists, and difficulty performing daily tasks. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension, controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling of the bilateral wrist joints and metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the urgent care clinic with acute onset of right knee pain and swelling following a brisk walk in her neighborhood. She reports the pain began suddenly two days ago and has progressively worsened, leading to difficulty bearing weight. She denies any previous episodes of knee pain or history of trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, treated with lisinopril, and hyperlipidemia, managed with atorvastatin. Her vital signs are: temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 130/82 mmHg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination reveals an erythematous, warm, and swollen right knee with decreased range of motion due to pain. Aspiration of the knee joint yields cloudy synovial fluid with a white blood cell count of 50,000/mm³. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past 4 months. She reports that the pain is most severe in the morning, lasting for about an hour, and improves as the day progresses. She has no history of trauma and denies any fevers, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of purpuric rash on her lower extremities and joint pain. She reports having dark-colored urine over the past 24 hours and mild abdominal pain. She denies any recent upper respiratory tract infections or use of new medications. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower legs and mild swelling of the knees and ankles. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 12.1 g/dL, platelet count of 250,000/mm3, and serum creatinine of 1.3 mg/dL. Urinalysis is positive for blood and protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purple spots on her lower legs and intermittent joint pain in her knees and ankles. She also notes dark-colored urine over the past few days. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or abdominal pain. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. On physical examination, she has palpable purpura on her lower extremities and mild joint swelling without erythema. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 12.5 g/dL, platelet count of 220,000/mm3, and a creatinine level of 1.3 mg/dL. A urinalysis reveals hematuria and proteinuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers over the past eight months. She reports that the ulcers heal with scarring and recur every few weeks. Additionally, she has been experiencing episodes of joint pain and swelling in her knees and ankles. She denies any significant family history of autoimmune conditions. On examination, multiple shallow ulcers are noted on the buccal mucosa, and there is hyperpigmented scarring on her labia. Her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, heart rate is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Complete blood count and basic metabolic panels are within normal limits, but her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute right knee pain and swelling after playing a soccer match the previous day. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the knee, with significant swelling that developed overnight. She denies any previous joint issues and is otherwise healthy. On examination, the right knee is visibly swollen, warm, and tender to palpation with decreased range of motion due to pain. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C). Laboratory results show a white blood cell count of 9,500/mm^3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 25 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 6 mg/L. A knee x-ray shows soft tissue swelling but no fracture. Aspiration of the knee joint reveals cloudy synovial fluid. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with weakness in her right foot and difficulty walking for the past 2 weeks. She reports that she has been tripping more often because her right foot seems to drag. She also mentions intermittent tingling and numbness in the same foot. She denies any back pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals a high-stepping gait and weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ throughout, with no clonus. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is diminished over the lateral aspect of the right calf and foot. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and intermittent numbness and tingling in her arms, especially in the mornings. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens with prolonged sitting and is occasionally associated with headaches. She has no history of trauma or recent infections. Her past medical history includes hypertension and migraines, managed with lisinopril and sumatriptan respectively. She works as a desk job employee and reports that her symptoms have started to affect her daily activities and productivity at work. On physical examination, there is reduced range of motion in the cervical spine, and sensory examination reveals decreased sensation in the fingertips bilaterally. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk in the upper extremities, and the Spurling test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purpura on her lower extremities and recent episodes of hematuria. She also reports joint pain primarily in her knees and ankles, as well as fatigue. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and a mild anemia. Urinalysis shows red blood cells and red blood cell casts. A skin biopsy of the purpura shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and describes swelling and tenderness in her joints. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a leukocyte count of 7,500/mm3, hemoglobin of 12 g/dL, and a platelet count of 300,000/mm3. An elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after a fall on an outstretched hand while hiking. She reports immediate pain and swelling in her right wrist and is unable to move it without significant discomfort. She denies any numbness or tingling in her fingers. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and seasonal allergies. She takes lisinopril and loratadine. Her vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure of 120/78 mmHg, heart rate of 75/min, and respirations of 14/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the distal radius with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is performed. What is the next most appropriate step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Apply a wrist splint and arrange orthopedic follow-up
options.C: Order MRI to assess ligamentous injury
options.D: Suspect high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.E: Consult orthopedic surgery for possible reduction

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of fever, night sweats, and weight loss. She has also noticed generalized body aches, especially in her joints. She has no significant past medical history. She reports feeling extremely fatigued and has difficulty performing daily activities due to joint stiffness in the mornings, which improves as the day progresses. She denies recent travel, animal exposure, or tick bites. On examination, her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 110/65 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. There is mild swelling in her wrists and knees, but no erythema or warmth. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 44-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 9-month history of progressively worsening bilateral hip pain. She describes the pain as a deep ache that is worse with prolonged periods of sitting and climbing stairs. In addition to the hip pain, she occasionally experiences mild morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes, but she denies significant swelling of her hands or feet. She is otherwise healthy with no history of trauma or inflammatory bowel disease. She works as a teacher and finds it increasingly difficult to stand for long periods. Her family history is significant for psoriasis in her mother. Physical examination shows tenderness over the greater trochanters bilaterally and a limited range of motion in internal rotation of the hips. Laboratory studies reveal normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent swelling in her neck and underarms, as well as night sweats and unintentional weight loss. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has experienced occasional low-grade fevers. She describes a mild, dull ache in her joints but denies any significant stiffness or swelling. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. She works as a teacher and lives with her partner. Family history is unremarkable. On examination, she has multiple palpable lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary regions. The nodes are firm, non-tender, and mobile. There is no hepatosplenomegaly or other significant findings. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels, but complete blood count is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Start empirical antibiotics for suspected infection
options.D: Refer to rheumatology for suspected rheumatoid arthritis

question: A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care physician reporting persistent swelling in her neck and underarms for the past 3 months. She describes the swellings as non-tender and denies any recent infections. She has also experienced persistent fatigue, night sweats, and an unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the last two months. Her medical history is significant for osteoarthritis, managed with acetaminophen as needed. On examination, the physician notes firm, mobile lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary regions and painless joint swelling in the hands and knees. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP. A biopsy of one of the lymph nodes is planned. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial diagnostic consideration?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with severe back pain that started suddenly after she bent over to pick up a heavy box. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her mid-back, with no radiation. She has a history of osteoporosis and was on bisphosphonates for the past two years, but stopped treatment six months ago due to gastrointestinal upset. The patient also reports mild discomfort in her wrists and knees, especially in the mornings, but it improves with activity. She denies any recent trauma besides picking up the box. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate is 18/min, and heart rate is 78/min. Physical examination reveals localized tenderness over the T10 vertebra with no neurological deficits. An X-ray of the thoracic spine shows a compression fracture at T10. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her current back pain?

options.D: Vertebral compression fracture due to osteoporosis

question:
A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with numbness and tingling in her hands and feet that has gradually worsened over the past two months. She also reports occasional joint pain in her fingers and wrists but denies any recent trauma or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes, both of which are managed with medication. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution, and mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show:

HbA1c: 8.1%
ESR: 20 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor: negative

What is the most likely cause of her peripheral neuropathy?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, weight loss, and tender nodules on her forearms. She mentions these nodules have developed on the extensor surfaces and are non-itchy but painful. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, along with generalized body aches. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), pulse is 85/min, and blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg. On physical examination, there are firm, nontender subcutaneous nodules palpated over the extensor aspect of the forearms and elbows. No joint swelling or deformities are noted. Laboratory findings include an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's condition?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with multiple small, firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and elbows. She has been experiencing generalized fatigue, low-grade fever, and a 5-pound weight loss over the past two months. She reports intermittent joint pains in her wrists and knees, which are more pronounced in the morning. The patient denies any significant past medical history aside from occasional tension headaches and seasonal allergies. Family history is notable for her mother having been treated for lupus. Physical examination reveals several subcutaneous nodules on the mentioned areas, mildly swollen wrists with warmth, and mild synovial thickening. Laboratory workup shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman visits the clinic due to a 2-month history of progressive fatigue and swelling in her legs. She has also noticed occasional headaches and dizziness. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. She denies any recent changes in her medications or lifestyle. Her blood pressure is 162/98 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and BMI is 30 kg/m2. Physical examination reveals bilateral pitting edema in the lower extremities and no jugular venous distention. Laboratory tests show:
Serum
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL
Blood urea nitrogen 35 mg/dL
Glucose 142 mg/dL
Urinalysis
Protein 3+
Red blood cells 10–15/hpf
White blood cells 2–3/hpf
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Chronic kidney disease due to diabetic nephropathy

question: A 32-year-old pregnant woman in her third trimester presents to the clinic with lower back and pelvic pain that has been increasing over the past month. She describes the pain as a dull ache that exacerbates with prolonged standing or walking and alleviates with rest. She denies any trauma or heavy lifting. Her medical history includes mild asthma controlled with an inhaler as needed. She is taking prenatal vitamins daily. There is no history of rheumatoid arthritis in the family. On examination, she has an antalgic gait and tenderness over the sacroiliac joint. Neurological examination is unremarkable. Her vital signs are within normal limits, and her recent complete blood count is normal. Given her pregnancy and symptomatology, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' that worsens at night. She also reports occasional fatigue and joint stiffness in her fingers, particularly in the morning. She has a history of hypothyroidism, which is controlled with levothyroxine. Her family history is significant for diabetes mellitus and rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On examination, there is decreased pinprick and vibration sensation in a stocking-glove distribution. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal serum glucose levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Begin physical therapy for joint stiffness

question:
A 39-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 5-month history of skin tightening on her fingers and hands. She has also noticed changes in color in her fingers turning white and then blue when exposed to cold temperatures. She reports feeling tired and has occasional joint pain. She does not have a primary care physician and has not seen a doctor for these symptoms until now. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 125/78 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, there is thickening and tightening of the skin on the fingers with decreased range of motion. The nailfold capillaries show abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,800/mm3
Platelet count 230,000/mm3
ESR 45 mm/hr
ANA positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to joint pain and skin lesions. She reports a six-month history of increasing pain and swelling in her right knee and left wrist. She also notes fatigue and morning stiffness lasting about 15 minutes. Additionally, she has developed patches of red, scaly skin on her elbows and scalp. She has no significant past medical history and denies recent infections. Her family history is significant for a father with psoriasis. On physical examination, there are well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on her elbows and scalp, and there is swelling of the right knee and left wrist. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's joint symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a three-month history of unexplained weight gain, particularly around her face, and periorbital puffiness. She also reports experiencing joint stiffness, especially in the morning, which lasts for about an hour. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any changes in her diet or exercise habits. On examination, she has a blood pressure of 130/85 mm Hg, pulse 76/min, and her BMI is 32 kg/m2. Mild swelling is noted around her eyes and her hands show slight puffiness with tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count, an elevated C-reactive protein level, and normal renal function tests. Urinalysis demonstrates 2+ proteinuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this patient's condition?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of increasing fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising. She reports pain and swelling in her wrists and knees, along with fatigue that has progressively worsened despite adequate sleep. She also notes frequent gum bleeding and occasional nosebleeds. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, she appears pale with scattered petechiae on her lower extremities. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.0 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,000/mm³, and platelet count of 75,000/mm³. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of a rash and joint pain. She describes the rash as red and scaly, appearing on her face after sun exposure. The joint pain is predominantly in her hands and knees and is associated with morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She also reports painless oral ulcers that have been persistent. She denies fever, weight loss, or new medications. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 110/68 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals a malar rash and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Her blood tests show mild anemia and positive antinuclear antibody (ANA). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Advise strict sun protection and topical steroids
options.F: Refer to rheumatology for further evaluation

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She notes that these sensations are worsening and are occasionally accompanied by a burning pain. The symptoms are getting more frequent and now interfere with her ability to type at her job as a data analyst. She has also experienced some joint stiffness in the mornings, which resolves after about an hour. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Reflexes are diminished in the upper and lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal normal blood glucose levels and a negative antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers over the past 6 months. She also reports color changes in her fingers triggered by cold weather, turning white and then blue before returning to normal. She has noticed occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any recent illnesses or significant weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. She takes loratadine as needed. Physical examination shows shiny, taut skin over the fingers with decreased wrinkling and mild joint tenderness in the hands. Capillary examination of the nail beds shows dilated loops. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm³
Platelet count: 230,000/mm³

Serum:
Sodium: 138 mEq/L
Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-week history of progressive difficulty walking. She describes a dragging sensation in her right foot, and her right foot often trips over small obstacles. She has a history of fatigue and occasional tingling in her hands and feet. She denies any recent trauma. Her past medical history is unremarkable, with no known chronic illnesses. Her vital signs are normal, and physical examination reveals weakness in ankle dorsiflexion and eversion of the right foot. There is mild muscle atrophy in the anterior compartment of the right leg. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ at the knees and 2+ at the ankles bilaterally. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is reduced over the dorsum of the right foot. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness, which is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. She reports swelling in her wrists and hands bilaterally. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On physical exam, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the MCP and PIP joints in both hands. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated ESR and CRP, and serology tests are positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and a 10-pound weight loss over the past two months. She reports feeling tired throughout the day, despite getting adequate sleep. She also notes occasional joint pain, particularly in her knees and hands, which she attributes to her daily routine as a hairdresser. There is no history of fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, or recent travel. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies smoking, alcohol use, or drug use. Physical examination reveals pale conjunctiva and mild tenderness in her knees upon palpation. Her laboratory results are as follows:

Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 29%
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV): 82 fL
Reticulocyte count: 1.0%

Further investigation shows a low serum ferritin level. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.F: Refer to a dietitian for nutritional evaluation

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of diffuse body pain and fatigue lasting for the past 8 months. She describes the pain as a constant aching sensation affecting her neck, shoulders, and lower back. The pain is accompanied by persistent fatigue and unrefreshing sleep. She denies any joint swelling or redness. The patient has a history of irritable bowel syndrome and reports occasional tension headaches. Her mother was diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals tenderness at multiple soft tissue points, but no joint swelling or deformity. Laboratory studies show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. The pain is worse in the morning and improves somewhat with activity. She reports difficulty gripping objects and occasional swelling. She denies any fevers or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals bilateral swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints, as well as the presence of subcutaneous nodules on her elbows. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with difficulty walking and a noticeable foot drop on the left side, which has developed over the past month. She reports some mild pain and tingling in her left leg and foot, but denies any recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, and she mentions occasional joint pain and morning stiffness that improves throughout the day. On examination, there is weakness in the dorsiflexors of the left foot. Her deep tendon reflexes are intact, but her gait is steppage due to the foot drop. You note mild swelling in the metatarsophalangeal joints. The patient expresses concern about her symptoms worsening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with an eight-month history of persistent skin lesions and joint pain. She reports having red, scaly patches on her elbows and scalp that occasionally itch but do not bleed. She also experiences stiffness and swelling in the fingers of her right hand and in her left knee, particularly in the mornings. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there are well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on her elbows and scalp. Her right index finger appears swollen, and she has tenderness in the left knee without significant effusion. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are both negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of headaches and a recent onset of swelling in her ankles. She also reports fatigue and joint pain in her fingers, wrists, and knees. Her blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg. She denies any changes in urination or recent weight gain. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL six months ago) and proteinuria on urinalysis. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is on no medications. Physical examination shows tender and swollen proximal interphalangeal joints along with 2+ pitting edema in the lower extremities. Which of the following conditions is most likely contributing to her joint symptoms?

options.D: Chronic kidney disease secondary to diabetes

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of diffuse musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. She describes the pain as a constant ache that affects her back, neck, and legs, and it doesn't improve with rest. She has been feeling extremely tired during the day and reports difficulty falling asleep at night. She denies joint swelling or redness but notes stiffness in the morning that lasts about 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for depression, for which she takes sertraline. On physical examination, there is tenderness at multiple points over the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and medial fat pad of the knee. Muscle strength and neurological examination are normal. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight loss over the past three months. She also reports occasional dizziness and heavy menstrual periods. She denies any recent illnesses but notes that her mother has hypothyroidism. On physical examination, the patient appears pale with a heart rate of 98 bpm, blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, and mild conjunctival pallor. Her BMI is 19 kg/m². Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 9.0 g/dL (reference range: 12.0-16.0 g/dL), mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 72 fL (reference range: 80-100 fL), and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/h (reference range: 0-20 mm/h). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's anemia?

options.C: Testing for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question:
A 60-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of swelling and pain in her left knee. She reports that the pain began 18 months ago and occurs in episodes lasting about a week, with the knee becoming red, hot, and very tender. These episodes have been progressively more frequent, with the most recent one occurring just two weeks ago. She also notes the presence of firm, nodular swellings on her elbows and fingers. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies fever, weight loss, or any other joint involvement. On physical examination, the left knee is swollen and tender with some erythema; there are several subcutaneous nodules on her elbows. Laboratory studies show:

Serum uric acid: 9.2 mg/dL
White blood cell count: 8,500/mm^3

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending to pick up a light object from the floor. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her mid-lower back. She denies any trauma, fever, or recent illness. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis, for which she is treated with calcium and vitamin D supplements. She has a 20-pack-year smoking history but quit 10 years ago. She denies alcohol use. Her blood pressure is 124/78 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the T11 and L1 vertebrae with muscle spasms, but no neurological deficits. An X-ray of the thoracolumbar spine shows a compression fracture of the T12 vertebra. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-week history of difficulty walking and a noticeable dragging of her right foot. She works as a librarian and reports that her symptoms have gradually worsened, causing her to struggle with daily activities. She denies any recent trauma or infections. On further questioning, she mentions some intermittent lower back pain and mild stiffness in her hands. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism. Physical examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation along the lateral aspect of the leg and dorsum of the foot. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ bilaterally except for a 1+ ankle reflex on the right. There is no joint swelling or erythema. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy over the past 2 months. She reports experiencing night sweats and unintentional weight loss of about 5 kg (11 lbs) during this time. Additionally, she occasionally feels fatigued and has mild joint discomfort, particularly in her wrists and fingers. She denies any rash, oral ulcers, or significant hair loss.
