Her past medical history is notable only for seasonal allergies. She does not smoke and occasionally drinks alcohol. Physical examination reveals palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes that are non-tender and mobile. Laboratory studies show mild anemia, an elevated ESR of 75 mm/hr, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Antinuclear antibody test is positive. A chest X-ray is performed, showing mild bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling which began after she completed a half-marathon yesterday. She describes the pain as sharp and notes that it worsens with movement and weight-bearing. She denies any history of trauma to the knee. She has experienced some fatigue over the past week but no fever or other systemic symptoms. Her medical history is notable for asthma. On examination, her right knee is swollen with an effusion and is warm to touch. Range of motion is limited due to pain. There is no erythema. Laboratory tests show an elevated white blood cell count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive weakness in her hip and shoulder muscles. She notes that getting up from a chair and lifting objects above her head has become increasingly difficult. She denies any joint pain but mentions feeling unusually fatigued. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. Review of systems reveals a mild, persistent rash over her knuckles and eyelids. On examination, there is noticeable weakness in the proximal muscles of her arms and legs, but no swelling or tenderness of the joints. Laboratory investigations show elevated creatine kinase and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a red rash on her cheeks and nose that worsens with sun exposure. She also complains of joint pain affecting her fingers and knees, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She reports feeling fatigued and experiencing occasional fevers. She denies any significant past medical history. She recently started a new job as a lifeguard. On examination, she has a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds and tenderness of the small joints in her hands. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure and joint pain in her hands and knees. The rash, which she describes as red and butterfly-shaped, appeared approximately three weeks ago after a hiking trip. She also reports generalized fatigue and intermittent fevers. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, she has a well-demarcated erythematous rash over her cheeks and nasal bridge sparing the nasolabial folds. Swelling and tenderness are noted in several proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory results reveal a positive antinuclear antibody test and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of swollen lymph nodes in her neck and persistent low-grade fevers. She also reports night sweats and unintended weight loss of about 5 kg during this period. She denies any sore throat or recent infections. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medications. On examination, she appears tired and has palpable, non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy. The rest of her physical exam is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show a mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A chest X-ray reveals enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a rash on her cheeks and joint pain over the past few months. She describes the rash as red and appearing after sun exposure but says it does not itch. Her joint pain is primarily in the hands and knees and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She also reports feeling more fatigued than usual. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is a malar rash across her cheeks, sparing the nasolabial folds. There is slight swelling in her metacarpophalangeal joints, and tenderness is noted in the knees. Laboratory tests show: hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, white blood cell count 4,200/mm³, platelet count 150,000/mm³, and normal renal function. Urinalysis shows no proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of generalized musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and non-restorative sleep for the past 6 months. She reports difficulty concentrating and has noticed that her symptoms worsen with stress. She denies any specific joint swelling. She has been previously healthy with no significant medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her family history is notable for her mother’s rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she appears tired but in no acute distress. Tenderness is noted in multiple tender points including the trapezius and lateral epicondyles, but there is no synovitis or joint deformity. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands. She describes the pain as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. She has difficulty making a fist in the morning due to stiffness. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, controlled with levothyroxine. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her temperature is 98.1°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 115/75 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hour and C-reactive protein level of 10 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful red lumps on her shins and generalized fatigue. She reports joint pain primarily in her wrists and knees, along with low-grade fever and malaise. The patient has a past medical history of celiac disease, for which she maintains a gluten-free diet. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals tender, erythematous nodules on the extensor surfaces of both legs. Auscultation of the lungs is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A tuberculin skin test is negative. Which of the following is the most likely condition causing this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She reports fatigue and occasional bilateral knee pain but denies any swelling or redness in the joints. Her pain has progressively worsened, limiting her ability to perform daily activities. She has tried over-the-counter ibuprofen with minimal relief. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her sister. Physical examination reveals reduced range of motion in the lumbar spine, with tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and negative rheumatoid factor, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. An X-ray of the lumbar spine shows characteristic changes of sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of pain and swelling in multiple joints, including her wrists, knees, and ankles. She recently returned from a trip to South America. She reports mild fever and fatigue but denies any gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms. On examination, her joints are warm and tender with visible swelling. There is no rash or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal a leukocyte count of 9,000/mm3, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 60 mm/h, and C-reactive protein of 70 mg/L. Tests for rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks of gestation comes to the clinic with complaints of lower back and pelvic pain for the past two months. The pain is described as dull and aching, worsening with prolonged standing and at night. She denies any joint swelling, fever, or morning stiffness, though she does experience occasional fatigue. Her prenatal course has been uncomplicated, and she is taking prenatal vitamins. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the pelvic girdle and lumbar paraspinal muscles, but no synovitis or edema in the joints. Her laboratory studies, including a complete blood count and basic metabolic panel, are within normal limits. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated at 40 mm/hr. Based on this presentation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Initiate physical therapy focused on strengthening exercises
options.C: Prescribe a trial of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs
options.E: Initiate pelvic support with a maternity belt
options.F: Refer to an obstetrician for immediate evaluation
options.G: Order X-rays of the lumbar spine and pelvis

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 6-month history of neck pain and numbness in her right hand and forearm. She describes a tingling sensation that worsens when using her computer for extended periods. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies recent trauma, headache, or visual changes. On examination, the cervical spine shows reduced range of motion, especially with rotation to the right. Spurling's test is positive, and there is decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome. Reflexes are 2+ in the biceps but diminished in the triceps. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 67-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of generalized bone pain and fatigue for the past few months. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. She is postmenopausal and does not take hormone replacement therapy. She denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the ribs and pelvis. Laboratory studies show an elevated alkaline phosphatase level, while calcium and phosphate levels are normal. A bone scan reveals increased uptake in the skull and pelvis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Order a dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan
options.G: Start calcium and vitamin D supplementation

question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and arthralgia affecting her knees and ankles. She reports feeling generally fatigued and has had a low-grade fever. She denies any recent travel but mentions a sore throat that resolved without treatment a month ago. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. On examination, there are numerous erythematous, tender nodules on the anterior aspects of both lower legs. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg, pulse is 84/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A throat culture taken during her visit is positive for group A Streptococcus. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 29-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to swelling around her eyes and stiffness in her joints. She reports a 10-pound weight gain over the last two months, despite no changes in her diet or exercise routine. Over the same period, she has experienced periorbital edema and stiffness in her fingers and knees, particularly in the morning. Her medical history is notable for seasonal allergies. She denies any history of trauma or recent travel. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Physical examination shows bilateral periorbital edema and mild effusion in the knee joints. Laboratory tests reveal low serum albumin and elevated creatinine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent bone pain that has been worsening over the past 3 months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, primarily affecting her hips and lower back. She is postmenopausal and has a history of mild hypertension for which she takes lisinopril. On examination, she has tenderness over the pelvis and lower spine. Her laboratory studies reveal elevated serum alkaline phosphatase, normal serum calcium, and normal parathyroid hormone levels. Her vitamin D levels are within normal limits. A recent dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows normal bone mineral density. X-rays of the pelvis demonstrate cortical thickening and bone expansion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue. She reports that the pain is primarily in her lower back and hips, and it has been worsening over the past few months. She denies any recent falls or trauma. She is postmenopausal and takes calcium supplements daily. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, she appears uncomfortable and has tenderness over her lumbar spine. There is no joint swelling or erythema, and her range of motion is intact. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 6,700/mm3, Platelet count 250,000/mm3, Serum calcium 9.0 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 220 U/L (elevated), ESR 20 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of chronic pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists. The pain is most severe in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before improving throughout the day. She reports fatigue and difficulty performing daily tasks. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive rheumatoid factor. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and erosions at the affected joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of progressive difficulty walking and weakness in her lower extremities. She reports occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any recent febrile illness, changes in bladder or bowel habits, or sensory loss. On physical examination, the patient has 4/5 strength in the proximal muscles of her legs. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric in the lower extremities. Gait is slow and cautious. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and mildly elevated ESR. An EMG shows evidence of myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old female college student presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling over the past week. She notes that the pain has been affecting different joints at different times, starting in her knees, then her elbows, and now her wrists. She denies any recent injuries but recalls having a sore throat about two weeks ago that resolved without treatment. She is otherwise healthy with no significant past medical history. She takes no medications regularly and denies alcohol or drug use. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 118/72 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, there is mild swelling and tenderness over the wrists and knees. Laboratory results show an elevated CRP and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. She reports that these symptoms began shortly after recovering from what she described as a "bad flu," characterized by fever, sore throat, and malaise. She denies any joint swelling, stiffness, or redness but feels achy all over, particularly in her upper arms and thighs. She has no significant past medical history and takes only a daily multivitamin. Her family history is unremarkable. On physical examination, she appears tired but in no acute distress. Vital signs are normal. Musculoskeletal examination reveals diffuse tenderness over the muscles of the upper arms and thighs, but no significant joint abnormalities. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
White blood cell count 5,200/mm3
ESR 30 mm/h (elevated)
C-reactive protein 1.2 mg/dL (elevated)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for her current condition?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent oral and genital ulcers. She reports painful ulcers on the inner aspect of her lips and similar lesions in the genital area that recur every few weeks. She also notes occasional joint pain in her knees and ankles, but denies any morning stiffness. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She denies recent travel, fever, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals shallow, painful ulcers in the oral mucosa and erythematous, tender ulcers in the genital region. There are no signs of synovitis in her joints. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a six-month history of chronic sinusitis, persistent cough, and fatigue. She reports episodes of hematuria and a gradual decrease in urine output over the past month. She also mentions experiencing joint stiffness in the morning, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections and she currently takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 37.4°C (99.3°F), pulse 76/min, respirations 18/min, and blood pressure 142/88 mmHg. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles, and labs show elevated creatinine levels. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Considering her presentation, what is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with new-onset swelling in her legs and fatigue for the past month. She has a history of intermittent joint pains and has noticed her blood pressure readings have been higher than usual, with a current measurement of 160/100 mmHg. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. Urinalysis shows hematuria and proteinuria. Her anti-nuclear antibody test is negative, but there is evidence of anemia. Considering her symptoms and lab findings, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and a rash. She reports that her knees, elbows, and wrists are tender and swollen. She also notes a red, expanding rash on her left thigh that appeared several days after a hiking trip in a wooded area. The rash was initially small but has developed into an annular lesion with central clearing. She recalls noticing a tick on her leg approximately one month ago, which she removed without difficulty. Her medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 16/min. The left thigh shows an erythematous rash with a bullseye appearance measuring 8 cm in diameter. Swelling and tenderness are noted in the wrists and knees. Which of the following is the next best step in management?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, chronic cough, and increasing fatigue. She reports occasional blood-streaked sputum and has had three episodes of sinusitis this year, each treated with antibiotics without lasting relief. She also describes a dull pain in her joints, particularly in the morning. Her past medical history includes seasonal allergies, but she is otherwise healthy. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 125/82 mmHg, and pulse is 78/min. Auscultation of the lungs reveals scattered crackles bilaterally. Laboratory studies show:

Creatinine: 2.1 mg/dL
BUN: 32 mg/dL
Hematocrit: 29%
Leukocyte count: 7,200/mm3

Urinalysis shows 3+ hematuria and RBC casts. A chest X-ray reveals patchy bilateral infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Refer to nephrology for renal biopsy
options.E: Start antibiotic therapy for bacterial pneumonia

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of fever and generalized fatigue over the past three months. She has noticed swelling in her neck and under her arms, which she describes as painful. These episodes are often accompanied by night sweats and short-lasting joint pain in her wrists and knees. She denies any weight loss, rash, or recent travel. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. The rest of the examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but normal complete blood count and liver function tests. Serologic tests for infectious mononucleosis and HIV are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening over her fingers, fatigue, and sensitivity to cold weather for the past 6 months. She notes that her fingers often turn white and then blue when exposed to cold, and this is accompanied by pain and tingling. She also reports joint pain in her hands and wrists, although the skin changes are her primary concern. Physical examination reveals taut, shiny skin over the fingers and hands, with some digital pitting and ulcerations. There is no synovitis noted. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive difficulty walking over the past 2 months. She reports weakness in her legs that has worsened gradually and occasional tingling in her feet. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history is notable for occasional migraines. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, she has decreased strength in the lower extremities bilaterally with brisk reflexes. Her gait is unsteady, and there is decreased sensation to light touch in her feet. She has mild fatigue but no joint pain or swelling. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers but normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue for the past 6 months. She has a history of menopause, which began at age 52. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the pelvis and lower back. Laboratory tests show elevated alkaline phosphatase levels, with normal calcium and phosphorus levels. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with medication. She denies any recent trauma or falls. A bone scan reveals increased uptake at the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain and stiffness for the past 6 months. She notes that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. The pain sometimes radiates to her buttocks. She denies any recent trauma or significant injuries. Her family history is notable for a father with ankylosing spondylitis. On examination, she has limited spinal mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 110/75 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated C-reactive protein level. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and swelling in her knees, wrists, and ankles. She describes the pain as migratory and notes that it started shortly after returning from a hiking trip in the Appalachian Mountains. She reports feeling generally unwell with intermittent fevers and fatigue. She denies any tick bites or rashes. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medications. On examination, she has a temperature of 100.5°F (38.1°C), and her joints are swollen with decreased range of motion. Routine blood tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following tests is most likely to assist in confirming the diagnosis?

options.F: Measurement of serum uric acid levels

question:
A 29-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and an unintentional weight loss of 8 pounds. She reports intermittent joint pains primarily affecting her wrists and knees, but denies morning stiffness. She also notes occasional episodes of palpitations and sweating. Her medical history is significant for iron deficiency anemia diagnosed in the past, but her symptoms have worsened despite compliance with oral iron supplementation.

Physical examination reveals a thin woman appearing older than her stated age, with pallor and mild tachycardia. Her joint examination shows tenderness without visible swelling or deformities. Laboratory findings demonstrate hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 70 fL, and low serum iron levels with normal ferritin. Further, her thyroid function tests show suppressed TSH and elevated free T4.

What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past 3 weeks. She mentions that these symptoms started shortly after recovering from a flu-like illness that lasted about a week. Besides body aches, she reports feeling persistently tired and experiencing difficulty sleeping at night due to discomfort. The patient denies any significant joint swelling or morning stiffness exceeding 30 minutes. She is otherwise healthy and takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness to palpation over multiple muscle groups but no joint deformities or swelling. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and normal renal and liver function tests. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is mildly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of recurrent fevers reaching 39.0°C, night sweats, and fatigue. She also reports experiencing generalized joint pain and a sore throat. Her medical history is unremarkable aside from occasional migraines. She returned from a study abroad program in Southeast Asia 2 months ago. She denies any recent weight loss, skin changes, or gastrointestinal symptoms. She describes having swollen and tender lymph nodes in her neck and under her arms. Physical examination reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
White blood cell count 12,500/mm³
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 65 mm/h
C-reactive protein 11 mg/L
A monospot test is negative.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of gradually worsening weakness in her shoulders and hips over the past six months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She has also experienced some joint pain and fatigue, but denies any swelling or redness in her joints. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. Vital signs are normal, and on physical examination, she exhibits decreased muscle strength in her proximal muscles of both the upper and lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and positive antinuclear antibody. Electromyography shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking. She reports weakness in her legs that has been gradually worsening. She also experiences occasional joint pain in her knees and fatigue. The patient denies any numbness or tingling. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, she has 4/5 strength in the hip flexors and knee extensors bilaterally. Mild tenderness is noted in the knee joints, but no swelling or redness is observed. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain predominantly affecting her fingers and knees, as well as scaly skin patches on her elbows and scalp. The joint pain is asymmetrical and occasionally accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about 20 minutes. She reports feeling fatigued but denies any fevers or weight loss. She has no family history of autoimmune diseases, and her current medications include ibuprofen as needed for pain. On examination, there are erythematous, silvery plaques on her elbows and diffuse swelling of several finger joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 66-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset lower back pain that began while she was lifting a light object. She describes the pain as sharp and worsening with movement. She denies any trauma or injury. Her past medical history includes osteoporosis and hypertension. She is postmenopausal and takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. Her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 130/75 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine and limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is normal, without evidence of radiculopathy. Her laboratory results show normal calcium, phosphorus, and thyroid function tests. Spinal radiographs reveal a compression fracture of the L2 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition contributing to her current presentation?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past six months. Additionally, she has noticed increased sensitivity to cold and dry skin. She mentions occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, stating it is not severe but more bothersome in the morning. She denies any fever, recent illness, or significant stress. Her past medical history is notable for a hysterectomy five years ago and occasional migraines. She takes sumatriptan as needed and a daily multivitamin. On examination, her temperature is 36.5°C (97.7°F), blood pressure is 128/76 mm Hg, heart rate is 70/min, and respirations are 16/min. She has dry, coarse skin, and her reflexes are slow. Laboratory tests reveal: TSH of 10 mIU/L (high), low free T4, and normal ESR. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of gradually worsening right shoulder pain over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache, which is exacerbated by movement and limits her ability to perform overhead activities. She denies any history of trauma to the shoulder. She mentions morning stiffness lasting for about 20 minutes and feeling fatigued. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the right shoulder and limited active range of motion in all directions, but passive range of motion is also restricted. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no acute fracture or dislocation, but there is narrowing of the subacromial space. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.B: Physical therapy focused on range of motion exercises
options.E: Administration of an intra-articular corticosteroid injection

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and stiffness, particularly affecting her wrists and fingers. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for about an hour before improving. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her family history is significant for her mother having an autoimmune condition, though she is unsure of the specifics. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory findings reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serology is positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the physician with a 2-week history of fatigue, low-grade fever, and painful lumps on her forearms. She also reports generalized malaise and joint pain that worsens in the morning. On examination, there are multiple small, tender subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), and she appears fatigued. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 52-year-old woman visits the clinic due to recent-onset hypertension and increasing fatigue over the past 3 months. She reports swelling in her ankles and occasional headaches. Her past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/98 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL and a urine analysis showing mild proteinuria without hematuria. She reports no joint pain or morning stiffness. Physical examination reveals no signs of joint deformities or synovitis. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as dull and constant, with some stiffness in the morning lasting about 30 minutes. The pain worsens with activity and improves with rest. She has difficulty getting up from a seated position and climbing stairs. She reports occasional fatigue but denies any significant joint swelling or skin rashes. She has a history of mild osteopenia but no history of trauma. Her BMI is 28 kg/m2. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. X-rays of the hips show joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and swelling in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She recently returned from a vacation in the Caribbean, where she enjoyed hiking and beach activities. She reports low-grade fevers and feeling unusually tired. The pain is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She denies any rashes, hair loss, or oral ulcers. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals warm, swollen joints with limited range of motion due to pain. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic neck pain and tingling in her right arm and fingers that has been worsening over the past 4 months. She describes the pain as a constant dull ache, with intermittent sharp pains radiating to her hand. Her past medical history is significant for obesity and hypertension. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or significant weight loss. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in the cervical spine, decreased sensation over the right thumb and index finger, and mild weakness in wrist extension on the right side. Reflexes are 1+ in the biceps and triceps bilaterally. A cervical spine X-ray shows degenerative changes with decreased disc height at C5-C6. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

options.A: Cervical radiculopathy due to disc herniation

question:
A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. She notes that her sleep is often unrefreshing, and she feels tired throughout the day despite getting 8 hours of sleep each night. She reports intermittent headaches and difficulty concentrating at work. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. She denies joint swelling or redness. On examination, there are tender points in the trapezius, neck, and hips, but no synovitis or joint deformity is observed. Laboratory studies show:

ESR: 12 mm/hr
CRP: 3.6 mg/L
ANA: Negative
Rheumatoid factor: Negative

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 30-year-old woman who is 28 weeks pregnant comes to the clinic with a complaint of worsening lower back and pelvic pain over the past two months. The pain is described as dull and aching, and it radiates down her legs. It worsens with prolonged standing and walking, and she finds some relief when lying down. She denies fever, chills, or any trauma. Her past medical history includes mild asthma managed with an albuterol inhaler as needed. She also reports increased fatigue and occasional swelling of her hands and feet. On examination, she is afebrile, blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and fetal heart tones are reassuring. There is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her physician with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth for the past six months. She has also noticed some swelling and tenderness around her jawline, particularly near the ears, and reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. She denies any recent infections or use of new medications. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, but she is otherwise healthy. On examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands with minimal tenderness. The rest of her physical exam is unremarkable. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Initial blood tests reveal mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following tests would best confirm the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a mottled purplish discoloration on her legs that has been present for several months. She reports recurrent episodes of swelling and pain in her calves and has been diagnosed with deep venous thrombosis twice in the past year. She also experiences fatigue and occasional joint pain. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis and her father had a history of stroke. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities and tenderness upon palpation of the calves. Laboratory studies show elevated levels of D-dimer and a normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of low-grade fever, fatigue, and joint stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and seems to improve slightly throughout the day. She also notes occasional abdominal discomfort and a 5-pound weight loss over the past month. She has no significant past medical history and is not taking any medications. On examination, her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. There is mild tenderness and swelling in the small joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 9,500/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h, C-reactive protein 3.5 mg/dL. Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of a rash on her face and joint pain for the past few months. The rash appears more pronounced after spending time in the sun. She also reports feeling unusually tired and has noticed some swelling around her ankles. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. On physical examination, a malar rash covering her cheeks and bridge of the nose is noted, sparing the nasolabial folds. Her joints, particularly the wrists and knees, are tender and slightly swollen. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, leukocytes at 4,000/mm3, platelets at 150,000/mm3, elevated ESR, positive antinuclear antibody (ANA), and anti-Smith antibody positivity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of a rash that worsens with sun exposure, along with joint pain and mouth sores. She notes that the joint pain, affecting her knees and wrists, is more pronounced in the morning and improves throughout the day. She denies any recent weight changes, fever, or fatigue. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies managed with over-the-counter antihistamines. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, a maculopapular rash is noted on her face and neck, and there are multiple shallow ulcers in her mouth. Her laboratory studies show mild anemia and positive antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old pregnant woman, currently at 28 weeks of gestation, presents to the clinic with lower back and pelvic pain that has gradually worsened over the last month. She describes the pain as a dull ache, exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking, and partially relieved by rest. She denies any recent trauma, dysuria, or abdominal pain. Her pregnancy has been otherwise uncomplicated, and she is on prenatal vitamins only. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and a waddling gait. There is no swelling or erythema over the joints. Laboratory tests show mild anemia but are otherwise unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands, wrists, and knees that has developed over the past six months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. Her medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus, controlled with metformin. She does not smoke, and her alcohol consumption is limited to occasional wine on weekends. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen, tender, and have limited range of motion. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). The physician discusses the likely diagnosis and potential treatments with her.

What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of persistent fatigue and weight gain despite a decreased appetite. She also reports feeling cold more often than usual and experiencing occasional joint stiffness in the mornings, which improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. She denies any recent travel or infections and reports no alcohol or illicit drug use. On examination, she has coarse skin and thinning eyebrows. Her temperature is 97.1°F (36.2°C), blood pressure is 145/90 mmHg, pulse is 60/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory tests show elevated thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman, who is 28 weeks pregnant, comes to the clinic with complaints of back and pelvic pain that has worsened over the past month. She describes the pain as a dull ache that radiates from her lower back to her thighs and is exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking. She denies any history of trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications besides prenatal vitamins. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the lower lumbar region and sacroiliac joints, but no swelling or redness. Her gait is slightly waddling, and there is increased lumbar lordosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.G: Schedule an MRI of the lumbar spine

question: A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with joint pain that has been migrating from her knees to her elbows and now to her wrists over the past two weeks. She experienced a sore throat about a month ago that resolved without treatment. She mentions feeling fatigued and having a low-grade fever intermittently. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any regular medications. On examination, her temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F), blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the affected joints. There is no rash, and the remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of recurrent fevers and fatigue. She also reports experiencing night sweats and has noticed that some of her neck glands are swollen. She denies any recent travel or significant weight loss. Her medical history is notable for seasonal allergies. She does not take any medications and has no known drug allergies. On physical examination, she appears tired but is in no acute distress. Her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respiration is 16/min. There is cervical lymphadenopathy, and the lymph nodes are non-tender and mobile. Examination of the joints reveals no swelling, tenderness, or restriction in movement. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count 11,200/mm3
Segmented neutrophils 60%
Lymphocytes 28%
Monocytes 7%
Eosinophils 4%
Platelet count 340,000/mm3
C-reactive protein 8 mg/L (N: <3 mg/L)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lb). She has also noticed swelling and tenderness in her neck and under her arms. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is unremarkable, except for mild seasonal allergies. Her vital signs are: temperature 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg, pulse 88/min, and respirations 14/min. On examination, there are multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
Platelet count 240,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
Lactate dehydrogenase 450 U/L
A chest X-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.A: Perform a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
options.D: Conduct a fine-needle aspiration of the lymph node
options.F: Refer for an excisional lymph node biopsy
options.G: Prescribe a trial of antibiotics for potential bacterial infection

question: A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and a spreading rash on her right leg. The rash started as a small red spot and has gradually expanded to form a round shape with a clear center and an erythematous rim. She reports that she recently went hiking in Connecticut and recalls removing a tick from her leg about 3 weeks ago. She also complains of fatigue and headaches but denies any fever, nausea, or vomiting. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, she has mild swelling and tenderness in her knees and wrists, and the rash is consistent with erythema migrans. Laboratory tests show a mild leukocytosis and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Start treatment for Rocky Mountain spotted fever
options.E: Perform a skin biopsy of the rash
options.F: Refer to an infectious disease specialist

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive muscle weakness, particularly affecting her shoulders and hips. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead but denies any joint pain. She also notes occasional mild fatigue and a 5-kg (11-lb) unintended weight loss over the past two months. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Her family history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness with preserved distal strength. Her temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 84/min, blood pressure is 118/74 mm Hg, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Laboratory studies show an elevated creatine kinase level and a negative rheumatoid factor. Electromyography reveals a myopathic pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before improving. She denies any recent trauma or new activities. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She has no known allergies and does not smoke or consume alcohol. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain that has been moving from one joint to another over the past two weeks. She recalls having a sore throat about three weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Currently, she reports pain and swelling in her knees and wrists, which improve with ibuprofen. She denies any fever or rash. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. Her family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, her vital signs are normal, and there is mild swelling and tenderness in the affected joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rapid streptococcal antigen test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive difficulty in walking due to weakness in her lower extremities. She reports occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any numbness or tingling in her legs. She has no history of recent trauma or infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with amlodipine. On examination, there is bilateral weakness of the hip flexors and knee extensors. Reflexes are normal, and there is no muscle atrophy. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatine kinase level. Electromyography shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past 3 months. She reports diffuse aches primarily in her back and hips, which are worsening. Her medical history includes hypertension controlled with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or changes in physical activity. Her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL and a creatinine level of 1.1 mg/dL. Serum alkaline phosphatase is elevated. A radiograph of the lumbar spine shows osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past 3 months, accompanied by noticeable swelling around her eyes. She also reports stiffness and discomfort in multiple joints, particularly in the morning, along with a persistent sense of fatigue. The patient has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril, and she recently recovered from a viral respiratory infection. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and mild swelling in the interphalangeal joints. Laboratory results show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain that seems to move from joint to joint, along with a low-grade fever. She had a sore throat approximately 3 weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is otherwise healthy. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the knees and elbows, but no effusion. There is a faint, erythematous rash over her trunk. Vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show a leukocyte count of 9,800/mm3, C-reactive protein level of 35 mg/L, and anti-streptolysin O titer is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and tender joints in both her knees and ankles. She reports these symptoms started about a week ago and have been progressively worsening. She has also been experiencing fatigue and a low-grade fever. She works as a nurse and is otherwise healthy with no significant past medical history. Her temperature is 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure is 112/75 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination shows erythematous, raised nodules on her lower legs and tenderness of the affected joints. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and unintended weight loss over the past 4 months. She describes feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and has lost approximately 6 kg (13 lbs) without changes in diet or exercise. She has had intermittent joint pain in her wrists and knees, which she describes as mild and not affecting her daily activities. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She denies any gastrointestinal symptoms, fever, or night sweats. On physical examination, she appears pale, and there is mild tenderness over her wrists and knees without swelling or redness. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 80 fL, and a low serum ferritin level. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. She reports intermittent joint pain in her wrists and knees. She also notes swelling in her neck and underarms. She has lost 5 kg (11 lb) over the past two months without trying. She denies any significant past medical history and takes no medications. She works as a teacher and has not traveled recently. On examination, she appears tired. Vital signs show a temperature of 38.2°C (100.8°F), pulse 88/min, blood pressure 120/78 mm Hg, and respirations 16/min. Palpation reveals cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show:
Complete blood count:
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
Leukocytes 15,000/mm3
Platelets 250,000/mm3
ESR 90 mm/hr

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman visits the clinic complaining of joint pain and skin lesions for the past 6 months. She describes the joint pain as being intermittent and affecting different joints at different times, with notable swelling and tenderness in the fingers, wrists, and knees. She mentions that the pain is worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. The patient also reports red, scaly patches on her elbows and scalp that have been progressively worsening. She denies any significant past medical history and does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of both elbows and mild swelling of the distal interphalangeal joints. Her laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP, but negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman visits her physician with complaints of recurrent fevers and sore throat over the past two months. She reports significant fatigue and has noticed several swollen and tender lymph nodes in her neck and armpits. She denies night sweats, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history is unremarkable except for mild seasonal allergies. On examination, she has a temperature of 38.2°C (100.8°F), multiple cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal anemia with a hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL, leukocytosis, and elevated C-reactive protein. An antinuclear antibody test is positive, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of generalized bone pain and fatigue for the past 3 months. She also reports occasional joint stiffness, predominantly in her knees and wrists, which is worse in the morning but improves throughout the day. She has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. Her mother had a history of osteoporosis. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sternum and lower back. She is 168 cm (5 ft 6 in) tall and weighs 70 kg (154 lb). Laboratory studies show: Calcium 12.1 mg/dL, Phosphorus 2.4 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 155 U/L, 25-hydroxyvitamin D 22 ng/mL, Parathyroid hormone 85 pg/mL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure, joint pains, and sores in her mouth. The rash appeared two weeks ago and is described as erythematous and photosensitive. She has a history of recurrent miscarriages. The joint pain is non-specific, involving her wrists and knees, and is associated with morning stiffness. On examination, her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. A malar rash is noted on her face, sparing the nasolabial folds, and there are multiple shallow ulcers on her oral mucosa. Laboratory tests show:

Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 4,500/mm^3
Platelet count: 150,000/mm^3

Serum:
Na+: 138 mEq/L
Cl-: 100 mEq/L
K+: 4.1 mEq/L
HCO3-: 22 mEq/L
BUN: 18 mg/dL
Creatinine: 0.9 mg/dL

ANA is positive with a titer of 1:320 in a speckled pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent miscarriages and a recent episode of deep vein thrombosis. She also reports a mottled, purplish skin discoloration on her legs, which has been present for several months. She experiences joint pain occasionally, but it is not severe and does not limit her activities. She denies any significant fever, weight loss, or photosensitivity. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis, but there is no family history of blood disorders. Her blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C). Physical examination is significant for a livedo reticularis rash on her lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, but her antiphospholipid antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. She also reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has mild joint pain in her hands and knees. She denies any significant morning stiffness. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement and decreased tearing. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Her rheumatoid factor is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's symptoms?

options.B: Test for anti-SSA and anti-SSB antibodies

question:
A 25-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-week history of reddish-purple spots on her legs and intermittent abdominal pain. She also reports dark urine for the past few days. She denies any recent upper respiratory infections or significant changes in her health prior to this. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, there are palpable purpuric lesions localized to the lower extremities. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal:
Hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
Leukocyte count 7,000/mm3
Urinalysis shows 2+ blood, 1+ protein, and no casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressive skin tightening of her fingers, accompanied by episodes of finger discoloration when exposed to cold. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, and eventually red once warm. She also reports intermittent joint pain and morning stiffness lasting for about an hour, along with fatigue. She denies any gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms. Her medical history is unremarkable except for mild hypertension treated with lisinopril. On examination, her blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, heart rate is 78/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). Physical examination reveals thickened, tight skin on the fingers bilaterally, and there is digital pitting at the fingertips. Laboratory findings show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test with a nucleolar pattern. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden lower back pain. She describes it as a sharp, stabbing pain that began after she bent over to pick up a heavy bag. The pain worsens with movement and improves slightly when lying down. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis, for which she takes alendronate. She also has a history of hypothyroidism controlled with levothyroxine. She denies any trauma or recent falls. On examination, there is localized tenderness over the lumbar spine and her movements are restricted due to pain. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show a normal white blood cell count, but her C-reactive protein is mildly elevated. An X-ray of the lumbar spine reveals an acute vertebral compression fracture. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis or related step in management?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists, stiffness lasting more than an hour each morning, and general fatigue. She denies any recent fever or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her vital signs are normal. Laboratory results reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A serological test shows positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. 

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread muscle pain and fatigue. She reports that the pain is persistent and often described as a dull ache, with varying intensity throughout the day. She also notes experiencing unrefreshing sleep and difficulty concentrating at work. Her medical history is significant for anxiety, and she mentions no significant relief from over-the-counter pain medications. Physical examination reveals multiple tender points, particularly over the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and medial fat pad of the knee. There is no synovitis or joint swelling. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count, normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 58-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to persistent bone pain and increased fatigue over the last three months. She reports the pain is diffuse but particularly affects her lower back and hips. She denies any trauma. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, managed with lisinopril and levothyroxine respectively. She is a non-smoker and drinks wine occasionally. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness over the lumbar spine, but no visible deformities or swelling of the joints. Vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show the following:

Calcium: 11.5 mg/dL (normal 8.5-10.2 mg/dL)
Phosphorus: 2.5 mg/dL (normal 2.5-4.5 mg/dL)
Alkaline phosphatase: 130 U/L (normal 44-147 U/L)
Parathyroid hormone: 150 pg/mL (normal 10-65 pg/mL)

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and generalized joint pain. She describes the joint discomfort as dull and persistent, primarily affecting her knees and ankles. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent travel. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Upon examination, she has several erythematous, tender, subcutaneous nodules on the anterior surfaces of both legs. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of right shoulder pain that has progressively worsened. She describes the pain as a dull ache that intensifies with movement, particularly when reaching overhead or lifting objects. She denies any trauma or injury to the shoulder. Her medical history is significant for intermittent episodes of fatigue and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder and a noticeable reduction in the range of motion, especially in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests reveal a normal CBC and slightly elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 37-year-old woman visits the clinic because of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unexplained weight loss over the past 6 months. She reports a low-grade fever that comes and goes. She has also noticed swelling in her neck and under her arms that has not resolved. Her medical history is significant for mild joint pain in her hands and knees, which has been manageable with over-the-counter medications. Physical examination reveals non-tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
Lymphocytes 45%
Eosinophils 3%
Serum
Lactate dehydrogenase 280 U/L
C-reactive protein 2.5 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a three-month history of joint pain and a facial rash that worsens upon sun exposure. She also reports recurrent oral ulcers that have been present for about two months. She has experienced fatigue and noted some swelling in her knees and wrists. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 14/min. A malar rash is observed on her face, sparing the nasolabial folds. There is tenderness and mild swelling of the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and elevated inflammatory markers. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is positive, anti-double-stranded DNA is positive, and rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of skin changes and finger pain. She describes episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red when exposed to cold or stress. She also notes progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers and hands, making it difficult to bend them fully. Recently, she has experienced fatigue and mild joint pain. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals thickening of the skin on her fingers and telangiectasia on her face. Laboratory tests are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of worsening fatigue, swelling in her ankles, and new-onset high blood pressure. She reports a dull bilateral flank pain and has noticed foamy urine. Her medical history is significant for mild osteoarthritis, which she manages with occasional acetaminophen. She denies any changes in diet or exercise habits. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/95 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 14/min. There is bilateral pitting edema to the mid-calf. Laboratory tests reveal:

Serum:
Na+: 138 mEq/L
K+: 5.1 mEq/L
Cl-: 102 mEq/L
HCO3-: 24 mEq/L
BUN: 32 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL
Glucose: 95 mg/dL

Urinalysis shows 3+ proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Renal ultrasound demonstrates normal kidney size with increased echogenicity. What is the most likely underlying cause of her renal abnormalities?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and weight gain over the past 6 months. She also reports experiencing cold intolerance, dry skin, and mild joint pain, particularly in the mornings. She denies any significant hair loss or changes in bowel habits. Her medical history is notable for a previous episode of postpartum thyroiditis 10 years ago, which resolved spontaneously. On physical examination, her thyroid gland is slightly enlarged and non-tender. Laboratory tests reveal a serum TSH level of 8.5 μU/mL (normal: 0.4–4.0 μU/mL) and a low free T4 level. Her CBC and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and intermittent episodes of fingers turning white and blue in cold weather over the past six months. She also reports swelling of her fingers and occasional joint pain in her hands. She denies fevers or weight loss but does mention fatigue. Physical examination reveals thickened skin over the fingers, with loss of normal wrinkles. The capillary refill time is prolonged in the affected fingers. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with persistent purplish mottling on her legs and recurrent episodes of deep vein thrombosis over the past year. She reports occasional joint pain and fatigue but denies fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for one spontaneous abortion at 10 weeks of gestation. She takes no medications except for occasional over-the-counter pain relief. On examination, her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Skin examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies are pending. Which of the following is most likely to be found in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and mild joint pain. She reports that the nodules appeared suddenly and have been accompanied by fatigue and low-grade fever. She denies any recent trauma or insect bites. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She is a nonsmoker and does not drink alcohol. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals several erythematous, tender nodules on the anterior aspects of both lower legs, and tenderness of the knees and ankles without swelling. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, with an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain that seems to move from one joint to another. She initially experienced pain in her knees, which has since resolved, but now she is experiencing pain in her wrists and ankles. She also reports a recent sore throat that resolved without antibiotics, mild fever, and fatigue. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She is not on any medication, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 37.8°C (100°F) and swelling with tenderness in the wrists and ankles. A throat culture is negative for Group A Streptococcus. A rapid plasma reagin (RPR) test is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Order anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody test
options.C: Start low-dose aspirin and monitor symptoms
options.D: Prescribe antibiotics for suspected bacterial infection
options.E: Refer for joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis

question: A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe pain in her right wrist after slipping on ice and falling onto her outstretched hand. She describes the pain as sharp and persistent, and it worsens with movement. She denies any previous similar injuries. Her medical history includes osteoarthritis and hypertension. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the right wrist with a visible deformity. Bruising is present but there is no open wound. Neurovascular examination is intact. An X-ray of the wrist is obtained. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.G: Closed reduction and casting in the emergency department

question: A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes the sensation as a 'pins and needles' feeling that is constant and sometimes painful. She reports no joint pain or morning stiffness but mentions occasional fatigue and difficulty concentrating. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, which is well-controlled with levothyroxine. She denies alcohol use and has no known family history of neurological disorders. On physical examination, her vital signs are normal, but there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in both hands and feet. Laboratory tests show normal glucose levels, normal thyroid function tests, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. She mentions a constant need to use eye drops and drinks water frequently throughout the day to keep her mouth moist. She also reports recent swelling in front of her ears, which has been persistent. Her medical history includes occasional joint pain in her hands but no significant medical conditions. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement and diminished salivary pool. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and positive antinuclear antibody. What is the most likely underlying condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute right knee pain and swelling following a tennis match yesterday. She reports the knee has been increasingly painful and difficult to bear weight on. The pain is sharp and worsens with movement, particularly when climbing stairs. She has no history of trauma or similar episodes. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lifestyle modifications, and she takes no medications. On examination, her right knee is warm, swollen, and tender with reduced range of motion. There is no erythema or palpable effusion elsewhere. Laboratory studies reveal a normal white blood cell count and ESR of 32 mm/hr. A knee aspiration is performed, showing cloudy synovial fluid with elevated neutrophil count. What is the most likely diagnosis?
