A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purplish spots on her legs and blood in her urine. She also reports joint pain in her wrists and ankles and some fatigue. She denies any recent infections or trauma. Her past medical history is unremarkable, except for occasional migraines for which she takes ibuprofen. On physical examination, palpable purpura are seen on the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal a normal platelet count, elevated serum creatinine, and the presence of red blood cells in urinalysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, postnasal drip, and recurrent episodes of sinusitis. She reports a dry cough and occasional wheezing, which have progressively worsened over the past two months. She also notes experiencing fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 8 pounds. Past medical history reveals episodes of joint pain and stiffness primarily affecting her hands and knees. She denies any hemoptysis or fever. On physical examination, her vital signs are within normal limits, with a blood pressure of 122/76 mmHg, pulse rate of 82/min, and respiratory rate of 16/min. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine at 1.8 mg/dL, and urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 12-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because of a rash on her lower legs and blood in her urine over the past week. She has been complaining of mild abdominal pain and joint aches. The mother mentions that the patient had an upper respiratory infection a week ago. On examination, the girl appears well, with palpable purpura on both her legs and buttocks. There is no significant swelling of the joints, and her vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies show normal platelet count, elevated serum creatinine, and urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her knees and ankles that started about a week ago. She reports that the joint pain is worse in the mornings but tends to improve throughout the day. She has felt more fatigued than usual but denies fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her past medical history is notable for seasonal allergies. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there are several erythematous, tender nodules on the anterior aspects of her lower legs. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition causing her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and weight loss. She also reports intermittent joint pain and feels fatigued most of the time. On physical examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Her temperature is 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 96/min, and respirations are 20/min. Laboratory tests reveal anemia and elevated inflammatory markers including erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray shows mild hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's condition?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fever, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. She reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has noted some joint stiffness in her hands and wrists, particularly in the morning. She denies recent travel or contact with anyone with similar symptoms. She has a history of mild asthma managed with inhalers as needed. On examination, her temperature is 38.7°C (101.7°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, pulse is 92/min, and respiratory rate is 20/min. There is mild tenderness over the small joints of her hands but no visible swelling. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels, but normal complete blood count and liver function tests. A chest X-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 9-month history of fatigue and weight gain. She reports feeling cold even on warm days and has noted some swelling in her neck. She denies any joint pain, but mentions that her skin has become dry and her hair is thinning. She is also experiencing constipation more frequently than before. Her family history is significant for autoimmune disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Her physical examination reveals a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. Laboratory tests show an elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level with low free thyroxine (T4). Which of the following is the most likely contributing factor to her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with intermittent fevers and fatigue for the past month. She reports feeling chills without any obvious source of infection and has noticed several swollen, tender lymph nodes in her neck and axillae. No recent travel or new medications are reported. She denies any significant weight loss, night sweats, or joint pain, but describes feeling generally unwell. Her past medical history is significant for mild seasonal allergies, but she is otherwise healthy. On examination, she is febrile with a temperature of 100.4°F (38°C). Multiple cervical and axillary lymph nodes are palpable and mildly tender. No hepatosplenomegaly is noted. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful ulcers in her mouth that have recurred over the past 4 months. She also reports painful genital sores that appeared around the same time. She has experienced joint pain in her knees and ankles, which she describes as intermittent and mild. There is no significant past medical history, and she denies recent travel, fever, or weight loss. She is sexually active with one partner and uses condoms consistently. Her temperature is 98.1°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals shallow, round ulcers on the buccal mucosa and labial mucosa, and one ulcer on the genitals. No lymphadenopathy is noted. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, Leukocytes 6,500/mm3, Platelets 230,000/mm3, ESR 28 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet over the past 6 months. She describes a 'pins and needles' sensation that is more pronounced at night. She also reports intermittent joint pain and fatigue. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies alcohol use and smoking. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the distal extremities bilaterally, and normal reflexes. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin A1c of 5.4%, normal vitamin B12 levels, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent nasal congestion, sinus pain, and intermittent nosebleeds for the past 4 months. She also reports a recent onset of fatigue and joint pain in her knees and ankles. Over the past 2 weeks, she has noticed hematuria and decreased urine output. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Vital signs reveal a temperature of 37.7°C (99.9°F), blood pressure of 150/95 mmHg, a pulse of 88/min, and respirations of 20/min. A chest x-ray shows multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated serum creatinine of 2.3 mg/dL and a positive c-ANCA. A urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain and stiffness, which is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity throughout the day. She denies any traumatic injury to her back. In addition to the back pain, she reports occasional pain and swelling in her knees and fatigue. She works as a software developer and finds it difficult to sit for prolonged periods due to discomfort. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals reduced flexion of the lumbar spine. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and weight gain over the past year. She has also noticed increased sensitivity to cold, and her skin feels dry. She denies any muscle weakness or change in appetite but reports occasional joint pain in her hands and knees. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Vital signs are as follows: temperature 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure 128/82 mm Hg, heart rate 72/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Physical examination reveals dry, coarse skin and mild periorbital puffiness. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 5,400/mm³, Platelets 250,000/mm³, TSH 12 µIU/mL (normal 0.4-4.0 µIU/mL), Free T4 0.6 ng/dL (normal 0.8-1.8 ng/dL), and ESR 25 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy. She has also noted night sweats and unintentional weight loss of about 5 pounds over the past month. She reports a persistent sore throat and mild joint pain in her hands and knees, which she attributes to seasonal changes. She is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and denies any recent travel or high-risk behaviors. Her family history is significant for an aunt with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she has palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes that are tender and slightly enlarged. Laboratory findings show elevated C-reactive protein and a negative monospot test. A chest X-ray is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's symptoms?

options.G: CT scan of the chest and abdomen

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent lower back pain and morning stiffness lasting approximately 30 minutes. The stiffness seems to improve with physical activity, but she struggles with limited movement mainly in her lumbar spine. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On examination, she has a decreased range of motion in her lumbar spine, and Schober's test is positive. There is no tenderness or swelling in peripheral joints, and her gait is normal. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) but negative anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) test. Radiographs of the lumbar spine show evidence of syndesmophytes and sacroiliitis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She describes a burning sensation that worsens in the evenings and disrupts her sleep. She also reports generalized fatigue and occasional joint pain in her wrists and fingers but denies morning stiffness. She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin. On examination, there is decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Her joint examination is unremarkable, and strength is preserved. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, hemoglobin A1c of 7.9%, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her legs and joint pain that has persisted for the past two weeks. She reports a low-grade fever and fatigue. The nodules are tender to touch and predominantly located on the anterior aspects of her lower extremities. Her medical history is significant for recurrent episodes of pharyngitis. She is a non-smoker and has no known allergies. Her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals multiple erythematous nodules on her shins, bilateral ankle swelling, and tenderness. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 12 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 9,000/mm^3
C-reactive protein: 15 mg/L
Anti-streptolysin O titers: elevated

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care provider with a 3-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. She has also noticed bilateral swelling in her face, particularly around the jawline. She reports occasional joint pain and stiffness, especially in the mornings. She denies any fevers, weight loss, or skin rashes. On examination, there is notable bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and a positive ANA test. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of fever and lymphadenopathy over the past few months. She reports that the fevers occur intermittently, with temperatures reaching up to 39.5°C (103.1°F). She also mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing night sweats. On examination, she has palpable, non-tender lymph nodes in her cervical and axillary regions. Her joints are mildly tender, but there is no obvious swelling or deformity. Laboratory studies reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels, with a normal white blood cell count and differential. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy. She reports feeling fatigued and has experienced unintentional weight loss of 5 kg over the same period. She mentions occasional night sweats but denies any recent travel. She has mild joint pain in her fingers and wrists but no morning stiffness or swelling. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and her family history does not include any autoimmune diseases. She does not smoke or use recreational drugs. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 92/min, and blood pressure is 120/76 mm Hg. Multiple cervical and axillary lymph nodes are palpable, non-tender, and mobile. Laboratory findings reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. A chest X-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurring oral and genital ulcers over the past year. The oral ulcers are painful, shallow, and occur on the inner cheeks and lips, healing in about two weeks without scarring. The genital ulcers are painful and recur every few months. She also reports joint pain in her knees and ankles and fatigue. She denies any recent travel, new medications, or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly.

On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. Oral examination shows multiple shallow ulcers with erythematous borders on the buccal mucosa. Genital examination reveals two healing ulcers on the labia majora. There is no active synovitis or joint deformities. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level. Tests for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative, while ANA is also negative.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers. She also describes episodes of joint pain, predominantly affecting her knees and ankles, which tend to improve with rest. She denies any fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, there are multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and similar lesions in the genital area. There is no synovial swelling noted in the joints, but tenderness is present. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and negative serology for syphilis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Test for anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies
options.F: Refer for biopsy of an oral ulcer

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet, which she describes as 'pins and needles.' She reports occasional severe fatigue and some intermittent joint pain in her hands. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She denies any recent illnesses, fevers, or weight loss. On examination, decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick is noted in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Reflexes are reduced in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis related to her peripheral neuropathy?

A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain, primarily affecting her wrists, knees, and ankles. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent travel, weight loss, or fever. On examination, her joints are symmetrically swollen and tender with warmth noted, but without erythema. Initial laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 10.5 g/dL, white blood cell count of 7,000/mm3, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/hr. A radiograph of the hands shows marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash on her cheeks that worsens with sun exposure. She notes that the joint pain is mostly in her hands and knees and is more pronounced in the morning. Her past medical history is significant for a recent episode of pleuritic chest pain. Vital signs are stable, and physical examination reveals an erythematous rash over the malar area sparing the nasolabial folds and slight swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a mild normocytic anemia and a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past several months. She describes needing to frequently use eye drops and sipping water throughout the day to alleviate the dryness. She also reports swelling near her jawline, which she noticed worsening over the past few weeks. The patient denies fever, joint pain, or rashes. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her family history is notable for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of worsening joint pain and stiffness, particularly in her hands and wrists, that has progressively worsened over the past year. She describes morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves with activity. She also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has a history of rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed 5 years ago and is currently on methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints with ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), and positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following findings is most likely to be observed in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fever persisting for two weeks. She reports having a viral illness with sore throat and fever three weeks ago, from which she recovered without complications. She denies joint swelling or redness. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly.

On examination, vital signs are within normal limits, with a temperature of 37.6°C (99.7°F). She appears fatigued but in no acute distress. There is mild tenderness to palpation over her shoulders and hips, but no joint effusions or deformities are present. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/h, and normal liver and kidney function tests.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for more than an hour before improving. She denies any recent trauma or injury to her hands. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, well-controlled with medication. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician complaining of fatigue, muscle weakness, and bone pain that has progressively worsened over the past 3 months. She reports recently needing to cut back on her daily walks due to the increasing pain in her lower back and legs. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and she has been postmenopausal for 15 years. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness upon palpation over the lumbar spine and proximal femurs. Laboratory tests show elevated serum calcium levels, low parathyroid hormone levels, and normal renal function. X-rays of the spine reveal osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports that the pain is more pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before it improves. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally, but it does not provide sufficient relief. On examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Further testing reveals positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Radiographs of the hands show mild joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a week-long history of non-blanching purpura on her lower extremities and dark-colored urine. She reports recent joint pain in her knees and ankles, along with fatigue over the past month. She denies fever, weight loss, or any recent infections. She is otherwise healthy and takes no regular medications. Her mother has a history of systemic lupus erythematosus. Vitals are stable, and physical examination reveals palpable purpura on both shins and mild ankle edema. Urinalysis shows hematuria and red blood cell casts. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated ESR and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and swelling in her knees and ankles. She reports feeling fatigued and mentions that the joint pain worsens with activity but improves with rest. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. She denies recent travel or insect bites but recalls having a sore throat a month prior. Physical examination reveals multiple tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior aspects of both legs. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein level, but rheumatoid factor is negative. A chest X-ray is performed and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman with a history of chronic sinusitis presents to the clinic due to persistent cough and hemoptysis for the past 2 weeks. She reports episodes of fatigue and nasal congestion over the last few months. Recently, she has noticed swelling in her ankles and a decrease in urine output. She denies any fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Her medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Vital signs show temperature 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure 140/85 mm Hg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 18/min. On physical examination, there is mild periorbital swelling and bilateral lower extremity edema. Auscultation of the lungs reveals crackles in the right lower lobe. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin 11 g/dL, leukocytes 9,500/mm3, platelets 230,000/mm3, creatinine 2.3 mg/dL, and urinalysis showing hematuria with red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 5-year history of bilateral hand and wrist pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in multiple metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, in addition to subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, a positive rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-ray of the hands demonstrates erosive changes in the affected joints. Which of the following findings is most specific for her condition?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of worsening hip pain over the past 5 months. The pain is bilateral and is particularly severe when she first gets out of bed in the morning and after sitting for extended periods. She describes the pain as a deep ache that improves with movement. She also reports intermittent fatigue and has difficulty climbing stairs. She has no significant medical history and takes no medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the hip joints with decreased range of motion. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 15 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of persistent fever and fatigue. She reports experiencing night sweats and weight loss of about 6 pounds over the past month. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional tension headaches. She denies recent travel, sick contacts, or changes in her medication regimen. On examination, her temperature is 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Physical exam is notable for tenderness over the left temporal artery and a new systolic murmur over the right upper sternal border. Which of the following is the likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of tightness in her fingers and color changes in her hands over the past few months. She describes episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red when exposed to cold or stress. She also notes occasional joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, but denies any significant swelling. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. On examination, there is skin thickening over the fingers and hands, and a digital ulcer is noted on her right index finger. Laboratory studies show a negative rheumatoid factor and an elevated anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old female is brought to the clinic due to sudden onset of severe headache and visual disturbances. She has a recent history of fatigue and mild joint pains. Her blood pressure in the clinic is 190/120 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory tests reveal serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen of 34 mg/dL, and potassium of 5.3 mEq/L. Fundoscopy shows arteriolar narrowing and retinal hemorrhages. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Chronic kidney disease secondary to hypertension

question: A 47-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty in walking over the past month. She describes a 'slapping' gait and has noticed that her right foot drags, requiring her to lift her knee higher than usual to prevent tripping. Additionally, she reports some intermittent tingling in the right leg and occasional lower back pain. She denies any trauma, fever, or recent illness. Her past medical history includes obesity and hypertension. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the right foot. No joint tenderness or swelling is noted, and her deep tendon reflexes are normal except for diminished ankle reflex on the right. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 6-month history of painful purplish patterns on her thighs and recurrent episodes of leg swelling. She reports experiencing intermittent joint pain and fatigue but attributes the fatigue to her busy work schedule. Her medical history is significant for two miscarriages in the past year, both occurring in the first trimester. She takes no medications regularly and seldom uses over-the-counter pain relievers for joint discomfort. On physical examination, she has a mottled, lace-like purplish pattern on the skin of her lower extremities, known as livedo reticularis. Her blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg, and her pulse is 78/min. There is mild edema in the lower extremities, and her joints show no signs of swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and a positive lupus anticoagulant test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 41-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of gradual weight gain over the past 4 months, accompanied by facial puffiness particularly around her eyes. She also notes morning stiffness in her hands and knees that improves as the day progresses. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies and hypothyroidism for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any changes in diet or physical activity and has had no recent illness or travel. Vital signs reveal a blood pressure of 132/85 mm Hg, heart rate of 78/min, and temperature of 36.8°C (98.2°F). On examination, she has periorbital edema and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, normal renal function tests, and an elevated antinuclear antibody titer. What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this patient's condition?

options.E: Antibody testing for systemic lupus erythematosus

question: A 16-year-old female comes to the clinic with her mother due to joint pain and swelling. She states that the pain moves from one joint to another and began a week after recovering from a sore throat. She also reports mild fevers and fatigue. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the right knee and left wrist. Her throat is erythematous, but no exudates are present. Cardiac examination reveals a soft systolic murmur at the apex. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. An anti-streptolysin O titer is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-month history of skin changes and joint pain. She reports red, scaly patches on her elbows and knees that occasionally itch. The joint pain is localized to her right knee and left ankle, with morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any significant fatigue or weight changes. Her family history is significant for her mother having arthritis. On examination, there are well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of her arms and legs. Musculoskeletal exam reveals swelling and tenderness of the affected joints. Laboratory studies show an ESR of 25 mm/hr. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive weakness in her upper arms and thighs over the past 6 months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She also experiences mild joint pain and morning stiffness in her hands, which diminishes after about an hour. Her medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, she has symmetric proximal muscle weakness and tenderness of the shoulder girdle muscles. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatine kinase levels. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 140/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old female presents to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores for the past 6 months. She describes the oral ulcers as small, painful, and occurring on her lips and inside her cheeks at least once a month. The genital ulcers have been similarly painful and recurring. She denies any discharge or itching but reports joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the knees and wrists, which improve with movement. She also experiences periods of fatigue and malaise. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, there are aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity and healing ulcers on the labia. There is mild swelling and tenderness in her knees and wrists, but no synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. She reports episodes of joint pain affecting her wrists and ankles but denies morning stiffness. The patient mentions unintentional weight loss of 4 kg (8.8 lb) over the past 2 months. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her family history is unremarkable. On physical examination, her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 112/70 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and she appears fatigued. There is bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. A chest x-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of joint swelling and stiffness in her hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past 3 months. She notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for nearly an hour. She denies any trauma, and there is no significant family history of joint disease. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Autoantibody testing reveals a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) and elevated rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with fatigue and unintended weight loss of 10 lbs over the past 3 months. She reports feeling increasingly tired despite getting adequate sleep and has had episodes of dizziness. She denies any fever, night sweats, or significant dietary changes. She has a past medical history of iron-deficiency anemia treated with oral iron supplements, gastroesophageal reflux managed with omeprazole, and occasional joint pain. Her family history is significant for thyroid disorders. On examination, she is pale with conjunctival pallor. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations are unremarkable. Laboratory results show:

Hemoglobin: 9.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 28%
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV): 70 fL
Reticulocyte count: 1.2%
Ferritin: 8 ng/mL
TSH: 1.5 mIU/L

Which of the following is the most likely cause of her anemia?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain that seems to move from one joint to another. She reports having a sore throat approximately three weeks ago and did not seek medical attention at that time. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. On physical examination, there is mild swelling in her left knee, which is also tender to the touch. No skin rashes or nodules are noted. The remainder of her examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show:
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 2.5 mg/dL
Antistreptolysin O titer 560 IU/mL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 4-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. She also reports mild joint stiffness in the mornings and fatigue. Recently, she has noticed swelling and discomfort in the area around her jaw. She denies any significant weight loss or fever. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. Vital signs show: temperature 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure 118/75 mm Hg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 14/min. On examination, she has bilateral parotid gland enlargement and mildly tender submandibular glands. Schirmer's test is positive. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive anti-Ro/SSA antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old female presents with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain accompanied by tingling and numbness in her right arm and hand, which have been progressively worsening. She states that the symptoms are aggravated by long periods of sitting and relieved by lying down. She also notes occasional headaches and morning fatigue but denies significant joint swelling or redness. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and diminished sensation to light touch in the right C6 dermatome. Reflexes are 1+ in the right biceps. Laboratory studies show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A cervical spine MRI reveals degenerative disc disease with foraminal narrowing at C5-C6.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of recurrent mouth ulcers and painful genital ulcers for the past 6 months. She reports that the ulcers occur approximately every 2-3 weeks and heal within 1-2 weeks, leaving some scarring. Additionally, she has been experiencing joint pain in her knees and ankles, which she initially attributed to physical activity. She denies any significant weight loss, fever, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her family history is unremarkable, and she does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use recreational drugs. Physical examination shows multiple small, shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa and vulva. There is no noticeable synovitis or swelling of the joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to a 3-month history of left shoulder pain. The pain is described as aching and is exacerbated by overhead activities. She notes progressive limitation in shoulder movement, and she's unable to reach above her head or reach behind her back. The patient has a history of hypothyroidism and is on levothyroxine. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or systemic symptoms. Physical examination reveals a reduced range of motion in all planes of the left shoulder, with significant discomfort at the extremes of movement. An X-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She describes the pain as affecting her hands, wrists, and knees symmetrically, with morning stiffness lasting for about an hour. She has also noticed some swelling in these joints. She has no history of significant trauma or infections and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 10.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 84 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 3-month history of shoulder pain and stiffness, primarily affecting her right shoulder. She notices the pain is worse in the morning and generally improves throughout the day, but she still has considerable difficulty performing overhead activities, such as reaching for items on a high shelf. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and she currently takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. Physical examination reveals a limited range of motion in the right shoulder with both active and passive movement, particularly with abduction and external rotation. There is no significant warmth or swelling of the shoulder joint. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 30 mm/hr and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 10 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to persistent fatigue, joint pain, and increasing bone pain over the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is particularly troublesome in her lower back and hips. She has also noticed increased thirst and more frequent urination. Her medical history includes hypertension and mild osteoarthritis. She denies recent trauma or significant weight changes. On examination, blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.4 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL), a serum parathyroid hormone level of 80 pg/mL (normal: 15-65 pg/mL), and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her feet, which sometimes extends to her lower legs. She describes the sensation as a 'burning' feeling, especially at night, and mentions occasional sharp, shooting pains. She also reports mild joint stiffness in her hands in the morning that resolves after a few hours. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed five years ago, for which she takes metformin. She denies any significant changes in her diet or recent travel. Her physical examination reveals decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch in a stocking distribution on her legs. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin A1c level of 8.5%. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 32-year-old woman presents to her family physician with complaints of chronic fatigue and weight gain over the past 6 months. She notes feeling unusually cold even during warm weather and mentions difficulty concentrating at work. She also reports some joint stiffness in the mornings, which resolves by noon. Her medical history includes mild seasonal allergies, and she takes no regular medications. Family history is notable for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On physical examination, her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 115/76 mmHg, pulse is 58/min, and respirations are 12/min. A firm, non-tender thyroid gland is palpated. Laboratory tests reveal: TSH 8.5 µIU/mL (elevated), Free T4 0.8 ng/dL (low). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.E: Referral to endocrinology for further evaluation

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and weight gain over the past 6 months. She also reports feeling cold more often and experiencing dry skin and hair loss. She denies any joint swelling, but mentions occasional joint stiffness in the mornings that improves throughout the day. Her past medical history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Her vital signs reveal a blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, a heart rate of 60/min, and a temperature of 36.5°C (97.7°F). On physical examination, she appears tired and has dry skin. Reflexes are delayed bilaterally. Laboratory workup shows a low free T4 and elevated TSH levels. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman visits her primary care physician because of swelling and pain in her hands and wrists for the past 3 months. She mentions that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and takes over an hour to improve. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr and C-reactive protein (CRP) of 15 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody is also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in her arms and hands. She describes the neck pain as a dull ache that worsens with prolonged computer work. The tingling in her hands is especially bothersome at night, occasionally waking her from sleep. She also reports generalized fatigue but denies any significant trauma or injury. Her medical history includes hypertension and migraine headaches, for which she takes amlodipine and sumatriptan as needed. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion of the cervical spine and decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome bilaterally. There is no joint swelling or deformity noted. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. An MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates multilevel degenerative changes with foraminal narrowing at C5-C6. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of headaches and swelling in her legs over the past two weeks. She reports recent onset of hypertension, which was diagnosed at a routine check-up one month ago. Her past medical history includes osteoarthritis and hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 168/92 mm Hg, bilateral lower extremity edema, and mild tenderness in the knees and wrists. Laboratory studies show elevated blood urea nitrogen and creatinine levels. Her urinalysis indicates moderate proteinuria. Abdominal ultrasound demonstrates normal-sized kidneys with no hydronephrosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluating her renal function abnormalities?

options.F: Magnetic resonance angiography of renal arteries

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with fatigue and joint pain that has persisted over the last six months. She reports stiffness in her hands, wrists, and knees, which is most severe in the morning and usually lasts for over an hour. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with amlodipine. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her vital signs are normal, and her hands show swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7,200/mm3, platelet count of 220,000/mm3, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. A rheumatoid factor test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers over the past six months. She also reports episodes of her fingers turning white and then blue in response to cold temperatures, which return to red and painful as they warm. She has experienced joint stiffness in the mornings that improves throughout the day and denies any recent skin rashes except for the tightening. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable. On examination, there is shiny, taut skin over her fingers and hands. Her blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C). Capillary refill is delayed in the fingers. Which of the following tests is most likely to help establish the diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the obstetric clinic at 28 weeks of gestation with complaints of low back and pelvic pain that has gradually worsened over the past month. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking. The patient denies any recent trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her obstetric history includes two previous cesarean sections. On examination, her vital signs are stable, and she appears in no acute distress. There is tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints, but no erythema or swelling. Her gait is normal, but there is discomfort with hip abduction. Laboratory tests show a mild anemia, normal inflammatory markers, and a negative rheumatoid factor. An ultrasound of the pelvis shows no abnormalities. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling that began a week ago. She reports pain mainly in her wrists, knees, and ankles. She mentions returning from a two-week holiday in the Caribbean three days before symptom onset. She denies any prior history of similar symptoms. She also has fever, fatigue, and a mild rash across her cheeks. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals warm, swollen joints and a maculopapular rash on her face sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate, but rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old pregnant woman in her second trimester comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of worsening lower back and pelvic pain. She describes the pain as a dull ache that sometimes radiates to her hips. The pain is worse at night and with prolonged standing. She denies any recent trauma. Her obstetric history includes two previous uncomplicated pregnancies. She has no significant medical history and is not on any medications. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 118/72 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and reduced range of motion in the lower back. A waddling gait is observed. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.A: Refer to a physical therapist specialized in pregnancy
options.B: Schedule an MRI of the lumbar spine
options.E: Order erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein
options.H: Advise use of a maternity support belt

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with lower back pain and stiffness that has been gradually worsening over the past six months. She reports the pain is most severe in the morning and improves with activity. She also notes occasional pain in her hips and shoulders. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She denies any recent trauma or infections. Her family history includes a father with psoriasis. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory results show elevated ESR and CRP levels. HLA-B27 antigen is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive bilateral hip pain over the past 8 months. She states that the pain is dull and aching, worsens with activity, and is relieved by rest. She denies any history of trauma but mentions morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She has difficulty climbing stairs and has started using a cane for support. Her past medical history is significant for obesity and hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 138/86 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in both hips with pain on internal rotation. There is no swelling or deformity of the joints in her hands or knees. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 7,200/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 22 mm/h
C-reactive protein 1.1 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of persistent low-grade fever, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of approximately 10 pounds. She also reports generalized fatigue and the recent development of painless lumps in her neck and armpits. She denies joint pain, skin rashes, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, she is afebrile with normal vital signs. There is palpable, non-tender cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal a mild normocytic anemia and elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman visits the clinic with complaints of fatigue and multiple subcutaneous nodules that have appeared on her elbows and knees over the past few weeks. She notes occasional mild joint pain but has not experienced any significant joint deformities. Her previous medical history is unremarkable. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the last month. The patient is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. On examination, scattered firm, non-tender nodules are palpated on the extensor surfaces of her arms and legs. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to further evaluate this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old female comes to the clinic due to chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in her arms and hands for the past several months. She reports the symptoms are worse after prolonged periods of sitting and occasionally wakes up at night due to discomfort. She denies any significant morning stiffness or hand deformities. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and hypoesthesia in the bilateral upper extremities. Basic laboratory studies, including complete blood count and metabolic panel, are within normal limits. MRI of the cervical spine shows disc degeneration with mild foraminal stenosis at C5-C6. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

options.D: Serum rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibody tests

question:
A 53-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 3 months. She also reports occasional joint pain, predominantly affecting her wrists and knees, but denies any morning stiffness. She has a history of iron deficiency anemia, for which she has been taking oral iron supplements. Despite this, her hemoglobin levels have not improved, and she continues to feel weak. She denies any gastrointestinal bleeding, changes in bowel habits, or overt signs of blood loss. Her family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild tenderness of the wrists. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 9.5 g/dL
Mean corpuscular volume 78 fL
Serum ferritin 150 ng/mL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition causing this patient's symptoms?

A 32-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of a red rash on her face that became more pronounced after spending time in the sun. She also reports joint pain in her hands and knees, accompanied by fatigue and low-grade fevers over the past month. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medication. Physical examination shows a well-demarcated malar rash that spares the nasolabial folds, mild synovitis in the small joints of her hands, and no other rashes or lymphadenopathy.

Laboratory studies reveal anemia, leukopenia, and elevated ESR. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is positive at a high titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe lower back pain after bending to lift a heavy box at home. She describes the pain as sharp and debilitating, making it difficult for her to stand upright. She has a history of osteoporosis for which she has been prescribed calcium and vitamin D supplements, but she admits to inconsistent use. She denies any recent falls or trauma. Her medical history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis, managed with hydroxychloroquine and occasional ibuprofen for flare-ups, and hypertension controlled with lisinopril. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the lower lumbar spine but no neurological deficits. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory results show normal calcium and phosphate levels, and her ESR is mildly elevated. X-ray of the lumbar spine reveals a vertebral compression fracture at L4. What is the most likely underlying cause of her current condition?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of skin changes in her fingers and recurrent episodes of finger discoloration upon exposure to cold. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red, often accompanied by throbbing pain. She also complains of joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings, and fatigue. Physical examination reveals thickened, shiny skin on her fingers and hands, with some evidence of digital pitting scars. Capillary nailfold examination shows dilated capillary loops. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness lasting over an hour and swelling in her fingers and wrists. She reports chronic fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers but denies any notable weight loss or recent infections. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, she has symmetric swelling and tenderness of the MCP and PIP joints bilaterally with no skin rashes. Laboratory studies show the following: ESR 45 mm/hr, CRP 12 mg/L, positive rheumatoid factor, and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show soft tissue swelling without erosions or joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain, particularly in the hips and lower back, that has worsened over the past few months. She describes the pain as dull and achy, and it is not relieved by over-the-counter analgesics. She reports no recent trauma or history of fractures, but mentions increased fatigue and occasional mild joint stiffness in the mornings. Her medical history is significant for menopause at age 50. On physical exam, there is tenderness to palpation over the spine and pelvis. Laboratory studies reveal elevated serum alkaline phosphatase and normal calcium and phosphate levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of a rash on her legs and blood in her urine. She also reports joint pain in her knees and ankles and a mild fever. She denies recent upper respiratory infections or any history of kidney issues. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower extremities and mild swelling of the knees and ankles. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 110/65 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Urinalysis shows hematuria and mild proteinuria. Laboratory studies reveal normal serum creatinine and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of headaches, fatigue, and swelling in her ankles. She also reports increased frequency of urination and a dry cough. Her medical history is significant for recurrent episodes of otitis media and sinusitis. She denies any joint pain or stiffness. Examination reveals a blood pressure of 160/98 mm Hg, bilateral pitting edema of the lower extremities, and mild pallor. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 11.5 g/dL, creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL, and a urinalysis revealing red blood cell casts and mild proteinuria. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows normal-sized kidneys without any structural abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. She reports having had a sore throat and fever three weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Since then, she has experienced generalized muscle pain and stiffness, which is particularly worse in the morning but tends to improve slightly as the day progresses. She denies joint swelling, rash, or other systemic symptoms. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well-nourished with normal vital signs and no abnormalities noted. Laboratory studies show mild leukocytosis and slightly elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and unintended weight loss over the past 3 months. She mentions feeling exhausted despite adequate sleep and has noticed a gradual decline in her appetite. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine, and hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent changes in medication or dietary habits. Physical examination reveals pallor, and her vital signs include a blood pressure of 120/80 mm Hg, heart rate of 85/min, and temperature of 98.2°F (36.8°C). Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, hematocrit 29%, mean corpuscular volume 68 fL, and serum ferritin of 8 ng/mL. Based on the clinical presentation and laboratory findings, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of progressively worsening joint pain and deformity, primarily affecting her hands and wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities. Her past medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies alcohol use and is a non-smoker. Physical examination reveals ulnar deviation of the fingers, swelling, and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. Her rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling in her knees, wrists, and ankles that have been occurring intermittently over the past month. She notes that the pain seems to move from joint to joint and is often accompanied by fatigue. Three weeks ago, she experienced a sore throat that resolved without intervention. She denies any fever, skin rashes, or changes in urination. Physical examination reveals warm, swollen joints with mild tenderness. Her throat examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive shoulder pain over the past 3 months. She reports stiffness and difficulty reaching overhead, which is affecting her daily activities. She has no significant past medical history but mentions feeling unusually fatigued over the past few weeks. Physical examination reveals restricted range of motion in the left shoulder with notable discomfort during abduction. There is no swelling, erythema, or warmth over the shoulder joint. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 15 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein of 0.2 mg/L. X-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with difficulty walking and weakness in her right foot for the past 3 weeks. She states that the foot seems to drag when she walks, and she has noticed that she frequently trips over small obstacles. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in her daily activities. On physical examination, there is weakness of dorsiflexion in the right foot and a high-stepping gait is observed. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ throughout, and there is no muscle tenderness or swelling. Laboratory tests reveal normal serum electrolytes and thyroid function tests, but elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Electromyography demonstrates decreased conduction velocity in the right peroneal nerve. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient’s symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with persistent fatigue and generalized weakness. She reports a 2-month history of low-grade fevers, night sweats, and an unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lbs). She describes experiencing intermittent joint pain, particularly in the wrists and knees. On examination, she has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions, but no splenomegaly. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 110/68 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 12/min. Laboratory tests reveal a mild anemia and elevated ESR. A chest X-ray shows mild hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of rash on her lower legs and blood in her urine. She also reports experiencing joint pain, primarily in her knees and ankles, along with recent episodes of abdominal pain and nausea. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for a recent upper respiratory tract infection. Vital signs are stable, with blood pressure 122/76 mm Hg, heart rate 88/min, respiratory rate 16/min, and temperature 37.1°C (98.8°F). On examination, there are palpable purpuric lesions on the lower extremities and mild tenderness in the abdomen. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria, elevated creatinine, and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of persistent pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past 8 months. She notes stiffness in these joints lasting for over an hour each morning, which gradually improves throughout the day. She also reports occasional fatigue and a low-grade fever. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory studies show: Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, Leukocytes 9,000/mm3, Platelets 250,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 60 mm/h, C-reactive protein (CRP) elevated. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody tests are positive. Radiographs of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is present at rest and worsens with activity, particularly when climbing stairs or walking for prolonged periods. She has morning stiffness lasting about 15 minutes. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. She has a family history of osteoarthritis affecting her mother. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the greater trochanteric area bilaterally and a reduced range of motion in both hips. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, normal serum calcium, and a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Radiographs of the pelvis show joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in both hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of joint pain and a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She also reports mouth ulcers and fatigue. She denies any recent illness or new medications. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, erythematous patches are noted on her cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. There is tenderness and mild swelling of the small joints of her hands. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 3,200/mm3, Platelet count 140,000/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain. She notes that the pain started gradually and is now associated with a restriction in the range of motion. The patient finds it particularly challenging to lift her arm overhead or reach behind her back. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or systemic symptoms. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder and significant limitation in both active and passive range of motion. An MRI of the shoulder is obtained. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as aching and affecting both hands and wrists, with stiffness lasting more than an hour in the morning. She denies fever or weight loss. She has a history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests show:

Complete blood count:
Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL
Leukocytes: 7,800/mm3
Platelets: 230,000/mm3
ESR: 48 mm/hr
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-CCP: Positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in her arms. She describes the pain as dull and aching, worsened by prolonged sitting and relieved somewhat by rest. The tingling is most pronounced in the fingers and is occasionally accompanied by a feeling of weakness in the hands. Her medical history is notable for episodic joint stiffness, especially in the mornings, lasting about an hour. On examination, there is decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and tenderness over the paraspinal muscles. Neurological examination reveals diminished sensation to light touch in the C5-C6 dermatomes and reduced grip strength bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 62-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of progressive deformity and pain in her hands and wrists over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty opening jars. She has no history of trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. On examination, her metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints are swollen and tender, with notable ulnar deviation. Laboratory results reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are also elevated. X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of intermittent purplish discoloration on her legs, which she describes as a lacy pattern. She reports having had two miscarriages in the past year and has experienced several episodes of leg swelling and tenderness. She denies any known clotting disorders and is currently not on any medications. Her family history is significant for lupus in her mother. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on her lower limbs and mild swelling in her right calf. Laboratory investigations show elevated D-dimer levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain in her hips and lower back over the past 4 months. She describes the pain as dull and constant, not relieved by over-the-counter analgesics. She has been postmenopausal for 10 years and denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-managed with medication. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the pelvic region, but no joint swelling or redness. Laboratory results reveal elevated alkaline phosphatase levels but normal calcium and phosphorus levels. A bone scan shows increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of right shoulder pain and decreased range of motion. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens with overhead activities and at night, disrupting her sleep. She denies any recent trauma or fever, and she has no significant past medical history. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder. Passive range of motion is limited, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no erythema or warmth. Neurological examination of the upper limbs is normal. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 14 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein level of 0.6 mg/L. A shoulder X-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain, a rash, and mouth sores. She reports that the rash is more pronounced on her face and worsens with sun exposure. Additionally, she notes experiencing fatigue and intermittent fevers over the past few months. Her medical history is unremarkable except for occasional migraines. She takes ibuprofen for joint pain relief. On examination, she has a butterfly-shaped rash on her cheeks and nose, and small ulcers on the oral mucosa. Laboratory findings reveal mild anemia, positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test, but negative rheumatoid factor (RF). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and joint pain for the past 3 months. She reports feeling more tired than usual, despite sleeping 8 hours every night. She also notes feeling colder than normal and difficulty concentrating at work. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals dry skin and a slightly enlarged thyroid gland. Her laboratory results show: TSH 8.2 µIU/mL (normal 0.4-4.0), free T4 0.6 ng/dL (normal 0.7-1.9), and positive anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and fatigue that has worsened over the past three weeks. She mentions a recent hiking trip, after which she noticed a rash resembling a bullseye on her left leg.
