There is tenderness over the T12 vertebrae, and she has a limited range of motion in her thoracic spine due to pain. Neurological examination of the lower extremities is normal. An X-ray of the thoracic spine shows a wedge-shaped vertebral body compression fracture at T12. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, calcium level of 9.2 mg/dL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 28 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with skin discoloration on her legs that has been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. She describes the pattern as web-like and purplish. She also reports recurrent episodes of leg pain and swelling, which she attributes to clots, as she had a deep vein thrombosis diagnosed 1 year ago. Her medical history includes two miscarriages in the second trimester. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities and tenderness in the left calf. Laboratory tests show a positive lupus anticoagulant and elevated anticardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is most likely the underlying condition associated with her presentation?

A 29-year-old pregnant woman at 25 weeks' gestation presents to her obstetrician complaining of worsening lower back and pelvic pain over the past two months. She describes the pain as a deep ache that is exacerbated by standing for extended periods and improves slightly with rest. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her medical history is significant for mild scoliosis, but she has not had any significant back issues prior to pregnancy. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and a waddling gait. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and mildly elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent oral ulcers and a more recent onset of painful genital ulcers. She reports that these episodes are associated with episodes of fatigue and joint pain, particularly in her knees and ankles. She denies any fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. She has no significant past medical history and is not taking any chronic medications. On physical examination, oral aphthous ulcers are seen on the buccal mucosa, and there are similar ulcerations noted in the genital area. There is no synovitis or joint swelling observed during the examination. Laboratory studies reveal a negative rheumatoid factor and negative antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of intermittent fevers over the past 3 weeks. She reports fatigue, night sweats, and occasional joint pain in her knees and wrists. She has lost 5 pounds unintentionally during this period. She denies any cough, dyspnea, or recent travel history. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg, pulse is 92/min, and respirations are 16/min. There is no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations are unremarkable. Laboratory studies show:

Leukocyte count: 10,500/mm³
Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL
Platelet count: 190,000/mm³
ESR: 68 mm/hr
C-reactive protein: 4.5 mg/dL
Antinuclear antibody: Negative
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-CCP: Negative
Blood cultures: Negative

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of increasing fatigue, joint pain in her wrists and knees, and occasional low-grade fevers. She reports feeling weak and has noticed easy bruising over the last few weeks. She denies any recent infections, travel, or changes in her medications. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes loratadine as needed. On physical examination, she appears pale with mild tenderness and swelling over her wrists and knees. There is no rash or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory work reveals a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,900/mm³, and a platelet count of 95,000/mm³. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 45 mm/h. Peripheral blood smear shows normocytic, normochromic anemia with occasional teardrop cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute onset of right knee pain and swelling following a weekend hiking trip. She reports that the pain began suddenly after a slight twist of her knee while descending a steep trail. She notes some difficulty bearing weight on the right leg and a sensation of instability in the knee. She denies any previous knee injuries or surgeries. Her medical history is significant for mild seasonal allergies. Examination reveals an effusion in the right knee with tenderness along the medial joint line. McMurray's test is positive, and there is a decreased range of motion due to pain. Her vital signs are stable, and laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old woman presents to the clinic with severe back pain that started suddenly after she bent over to pick up a light object. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-back region. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis, which she has been managing with calcium and vitamin D supplements. She denies any recent trauma other than the bending incident. She also reports difficulty sleeping due to the pain and has noticed some fatigue over the past few weeks. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the thoracic spine, but no neurological deficits are noted. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and renal function. X-ray of the spine shows a compression fracture at T8. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of progressive back pain. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her lower thoracic spine, worsening with movement and when lying flat. She also reports a reduction in height over the past year and occasional wrist pain. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and menopause at age 52. She denies any history of trauma or recent illness. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the T12 vertebra. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory tests show:
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL
Phosphorus 3.5 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 90 U/L
Serum protein electrophoresis shows no monoclonal gammopathy. X-ray of the spine reveals a compression fracture at T12. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening pain and swelling in both of her hands and wrists. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to pain. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the presence of subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue lasting for three weeks. She reports that the symptoms began shortly after recovering from a viral illness characterized by fever, sore throat, and cough. She denies any joint swelling or redness but describes a dull, persistent ache in her muscles, particularly in her back and thighs. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, she has tenderness on palpation of multiple muscle groups but no joint deformities or synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal a mildly elevated ESR and normal CRP. Additional serologic tests are pending. What is the most likely cause of her current symptoms?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past 6 months. She notes that the pain is particularly severe in the mornings and gradually improves throughout the day. She also reports feeling more fatigued and has had difficulty with daily activities due to stiffness in her hands and wrists. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Based on these findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent nasal congestion, sinus pain, and episodes of hemoptysis. She reports fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 4.5 kg (10 lbs) over the same period. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. She denies any recent infections or travel. On examination, her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. Auscultation of the lungs reveals crackles in the right upper lobe. Laboratory studies show elevated serum creatinine and an active urinary sediment with red blood cell casts. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her legs and joint pain over the last three weeks. She reports fatigue and a low-grade fever but denies any recent infections or travel history. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She is a non-smoker and works as a school teacher. On examination, there are multiple tender erythematous nodules on her shins and mild swelling of the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers but are otherwise normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her ankles and knees. She reports the nodules are tender and appeared suddenly. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her medical history includes mild seasonal allergies. She takes no regular medications and drinks alcohol socially. On physical examination, there are several erythematous, indurated nodules on both shins. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. A chest X-ray is performed and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past year. She notices that her symptoms are worse in the evening and that her eyes often feel gritty. Additionally, she mentions swelling around her jaw, which has become more pronounced over the past few months. She denies any recent fevers, weight loss, or other systemic symptoms but admits to occasional joint pain in her hands and wrists, which are not swollen. On examination, there is bilateral parotid gland enlargement, and her oral mucosa appears dry. Schirmer's test reveals reduced tear production. Laboratory tests show a mild normocytic anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and a negative rheumatoid factor test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old woman presents to the clinic with diffuse bone pain that she has noticed for the past six months. She describes the pain as dull and persistent, affecting primarily her pelvis and lower back. She has also noted some recent difficulty when bending over to pick items from the floor, though she denies any significant joint swelling or morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She is postmenopausal and does not take hormone replacement therapy. She denies alcohol use but reports a decreased appetite and occasional fatigue. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the lumbar spine and pelvis. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated alkaline phosphatase level with normal calcium and phosphate levels. A plain X-ray of the lumbar spine shows cortical thickening of the vertebrae. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with concerns of joint pain and a rash. She reports that the symptoms began two weeks ago after a camping trip, during which she recalls being bitten by a tick. The joint pain is predominantly in her knees and ankles, and she describes it as dull and constant. She also has a red, circular rash with central clearing on her left thigh, which appeared a few days after the trip. She denies fever, weight changes, or fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications. On examination, the rash is noted to be erythematous with a target-like appearance. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers but negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 2-year history of persistent pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. The pain is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for over an hour before improving slightly with activity. She has a history of hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies tobacco or alcohol use. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 8,000/mm3
Platelet count 320,000/mm3
ESR 45 mm/hr
CRP 3.2 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Anti-CCP antibody: Positive
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of worsening bilateral hip pain for the past 6 months. She reports that the pain is most severe in the evenings and often disrupts her sleep. She also notes morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes. She denies any recent trauma or history of significant physical activity. The pain is described as a deep ache that occasionally radiates to the knees. Her medical history includes mild hypertension, managed with lifestyle modifications. On examination, she has limited range of motion in both hips and tenderness with internal and external rotation. Laboratory tests show a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), but rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are negative. An X-ray of the pelvis shows joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the clinic with intermittent fevers, fatigue, and painless subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms that have developed over the past 3 months. She describes a vague discomfort in her wrists and knees but denies any morning stiffness. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and migraine headaches. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 128/84 mm Hg, and pulse is 88/min. The subcutaneous nodules are firm and mobile, with no associated erythema or warmth. Laboratory studies show a white blood cell count of 7,800/mm3, hemoglobin 13.2 g/dL, and elevated C-reactive protein. Chest x-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and no pulmonary infiltrates. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman visits her physician due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She reports weakness in her legs, which has been worsening and causing frequent tripping. Additionally, she has experienced occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, but no swelling. She denies fever or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, there is noticeable muscle weakness in the lower extremities, particularly in the hip flexors. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished bilaterally in the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and normal liver function tests. A serum creatine kinase level is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman visits the clinic due to persistent joint pain in her hands and feet for the past 6 months. She describes the pain as most severe in the mornings, lasting for approximately 2 hours before subsiding. She also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. She has no significant past medical history, and her family history is notable for autoimmune conditions, including her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels are also elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute pain and swelling in her right knee that began after a tennis match yesterday. She describes the pain as sharp and severe, making it difficult for her to bear weight on the affected leg. She denies any previous episodes of joint pain or morning stiffness. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, the right knee is swollen, erythematous, and warm to the touch with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no rash, and other joints are not affected. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up visit due to new-onset hypertension and increasing fatigue over the past 2 months. She reports occasional headaches and dizziness. Her medical history is significant for obesity and controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. On examination, her blood pressure is 158/96 mm Hg, heart rate is 85/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. A urinalysis reveals proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Serum creatinine is elevated at 2.1 mg/dL, and her estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) has decreased from 80 mL/min to 55 mL/min over the past year. The patient also reports mild morning stiffness in her wrists that improves throughout the day. She denies any recent infections or medication changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of headaches and fatigue. She also reports a recent increase in blood pressure readings at home, averaging 150/95 mm Hg. She has noticed some swelling in her ankles and occasional joint stiffness in the morning that lasts for about 30 minutes. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional tension headaches. On physical examination, she appears well but has mild bilateral ankle edema. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine level and proteinuria on urinalysis. She is currently taking no medications. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent nasal congestion, facial pressure, and ear fullness over the past 6 months. She reports a productive cough with yellowish sputum and a significant decrease in exercise tolerance. She also notes frequent episodes of blood in her urine, but denies any visible hematuria. She has experienced increased fatigue and occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals bilateral maxillary sinus tenderness and scattered crackles in the lung bases. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7,800/mm³, and a creatinine level of 1.8 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows dysmorphic red blood cells and red blood cell casts. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 31-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that began a week after recovering from an upper respiratory viral illness. She reports feeling generally unwell and mentions that her muscles are sore upon touch, especially around her shoulders and hips. She has no significant past medical history and is not currently on any medications. Her vital signs are: temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 120/76 mmHg, heart rate 72/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Physical examination reveals diffuse tenderness over the muscles, but no joint swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with persistent sinusitis and a cough for the past several months. She reports occasional nosebleeds and has noticed blood in her urine. She also complains of fatigue and joint pain, primarily in her wrists and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, but she is otherwise healthy. Vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure of 122/80 mmHg, pulse of 76/min, and respirations of 14/min. Physical examination shows tenderness in the joints mentioned and bilateral crackles upon lung auscultation. Laboratory studies reveal:

Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): 34 mg/dL
Creatinine: 2.1 mg/dL
Urinalysis: RBC casts, proteinuria
Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl is brought to the primary care clinic by her mother because of intermittent joint pain for the past 2 weeks. The pain started in her knees and ankles, and over the last few days, it has also affected her wrists. She recently recovered from a sore throat that occurred about 3 weeks ago. She reports feeling generally tired and has not had any fever or rash. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. Physical examination shows mild swelling and tenderness of the affected joints without erythema. A rapid strep test was negative, but serology is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with pain and swelling in her hands and wrists. She reports that the symptoms have been present for the last 3 months and are worse in the morning, lasting for at least 1 hour before improving as the day progresses. She also notes feeling fatigued and has difficulty performing daily tasks due to reduced grip strength. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. There is no synovial fluid aspiration done, but laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain that seems to move from one joint to another. Initially, she noticed pain in her left knee, which resolved after a few days, only to be followed by pain in her right wrist and left elbow. Her mother reports that the patient had a sore throat approximately one month ago, which resolved without medication. The patient denies any fever, skin rashes, or weight loss. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. There is mild swelling and tenderness in the right wrist, but no erythema. Her heart sounds include a soft systolic murmur at the apex. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of weight gain, periorbital edema, and joint stiffness that has developed over the past 6 months. She describes the stiffness as more pronounced in the morning, lasting about 30 minutes, and affecting her hands and knees. She denies any recent changes in diet or exercise. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, she has significant periorbital edema, mild pitting edema in both lower extremities, and tenderness in her proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count: 7,500/mm^3 with normal differential, Serum creatinine: 0.8 mg/dL, Serum albumin: 2.8 g/dL, and Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH): 1.5 mIU/L. Urinalysis shows 4+ proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a three-day history of fever, joint pain, and swelling in her knees and wrists. She recently returned from a vacation in the Caribbean. She denies any rashes, weight changes, or new medications. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she has a temperature of 101.3°F (38.5°C), a blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, a pulse of 92/min, and respirations of 18/min. There is significant swelling and erythema in both knees and her left wrist, with reduced range of motion due to pain. Laboratory studies reveal a normal white blood cell count, elevated C-reactive protein, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her knees and ankles for the past week. She reports feeling fatigued and has had a low-grade fever. She has a history of recurrent sinusitis and has been experiencing a dry cough for the past two weeks. She denies any recent travel or new medications. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), blood pressure is 120/75 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Physical examination reveals multiple erythematous, tender nodules on both shins. Her lung fields are clear to auscultation. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR of 60 mm/hr and an elevated CRP. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Erythema nodosum due to streptococcal infection

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with right shoulder pain that has progressively worsened over the past 3 months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, with occasional sharp exacerbations when reaching overhead or behind her back. She notes that her shoulder feels stiff, particularly in the morning, but loosens up slightly with movement. Her previous medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism. Her medications include lisinopril and levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals a limited range of motion in the right shoulder, especially with abduction and external rotation. There is no visible swelling, erythema, or warmth. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of painless, non-blanching purpura on her lower extremities and dark urine. She reports recently having a sore throat, which resolved without antibiotics. She denies any joint pain or swelling but mentions feeling unusually fatigued. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. A physical exam reveals scattered purpuric lesions on the legs and feet. Urinalysis indicates hematuria and mild proteinuria. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old African American woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and diffuse bone pain over the past 4 months. She mentions feeling more tired than usual and has experienced some difficulty climbing stairs due to weakness. She denies any recent falls or injuries. There is no significant past medical history, and she takes only over-the-counter vitamin supplements. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, she appears fatigued but is in no acute distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum calcium levels of 11.8 mg/dL (reference range: 8.5-10.5 mg/dL) and reduced serum phosphate levels. Her parathyroid hormone level is elevated. X-rays show diffuse bone demineralization. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 32-year-old pregnant woman in her third trimester visits her OB/GYN clinic with complaints of persistent lower back and pelvic pain over the past two weeks. She describes the pain as dull and aching, worsened by prolonged standing or walking. She also reports increasing fatigue but denies any fever, joint swelling, or morning stiffness. The patient is otherwise healthy, and her pregnancy has been uncomplicated. She is a nonsmoker and does not consume alcohol. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, but the range of motion is preserved. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show:
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
Leukocytes 9,000/mm3
Platelets 220,000/mm3
ESR 40 mm/hr
CRP slightly elevated
No autoantibodies detected
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her endocrinologist with complaints of fatigue, weight gain, and joint pain over the past six months. She also reports feeling colder than usual and experiencing dry skin. She denies any morning stiffness in her joints but notes a consistent level of discomfort throughout the day. Her past medical history includes migraines and seasonal allergies. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases. Her vital signs are: temperature 97.6°F (36.4°C), blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, pulse 74/min, respirations 14/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination reveals dry skin and mild edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory results indicate elevated TSH and low free T4 levels. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and hands that she has noticed over the past 4 months. She also experiences episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon rewarming, especially during colder months. She reports joint pain and fatigue but denies any significant morning stiffness. She has a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease and occasional heartburn. On examination, there is a noticeable thickening of the skin on her fingers, with visible telangiectasias. Her fingers show signs of sclerodactyly, but there are no synovial effusions. Laboratory tests reveal elevated antinuclear antibody levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate test or finding to confirm the underlying diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain affecting her wrists, elbows, and knees for the past 8 months. She notes that her symptoms are particularly severe in the morning, taking several hours to improve. She also reports occasional low-grade fevers and unintended weight loss. Physical examination reveals swollen and tender joints in a symmetric distribution, with a slight decrease in range of motion. Laboratory studies show normocytic anemia with hemoglobin at 10.8 g/dL and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 45 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of joint pain, swelling, and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She notes that the symptoms predominantly affect her wrists and the proximal finger joints on both hands. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. On examination, there is synovitis of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints with a reduced range of motion. Laboratory findings show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody test. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with severe back pain that has developed over the last week. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her lower back without radiation. She denies any recent trauma or fall. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and osteopenia. She takes lisinopril, calcium, and vitamin D supplements. She reports difficulty bending and lifting objects due to the pain. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the lumbar vertebrae and limited range of motion. Neurological examination is normal. An X-ray of the lumbar spine reveals a compression fracture of the L3 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to new-onset high blood pressure and generalized fatigue over the past few months. She has a history of hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis and her father had hypertension. The patient reports occasional joint pain and stiffness, which she attributes to aging. Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 160/98 mmHg, and mild edema of the lower extremities. Laboratory tests show:
Serum creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (normal 0.6-1.2 mg/dL)
Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): 28 mg/dL (normal 7-18 mg/dL)
Urinalysis shows 2+ proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's renal findings?

A 30-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of fatigue and weight gain. She reports feeling unusually tired despite getting enough sleep and has noticed hair thinning and dry skin. She denies any joint pain or swelling and has no history of autoimmune disease. Physical examination reveals a slightly enlarged, non-tender thyroid gland. Laboratory results show elevated TSH and low free T4 levels. Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Order an MRI of the pituitary gland
options.F: Refer to nutritionist for weight loss program

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the last 3 months. She reports mild joint discomfort in her hands and knees that she attributes to aging. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She denies alcohol use, smoking, or illicit drug use. On physical examination, she appears pale and has mild conjunctival pallor. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.0 g/dL and mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 82 fL. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to increasing fatigue, joint pains, and recurrent nosebleeds over the past month. She has noted easy bruising and has felt more tired than usual, attributing it to her busy work schedule. She reports bilateral knee and wrist pain, which she describes as a dull ache, and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any recent infections, and her past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, she appears pale, and there is mild tenderness over both knees with no swelling. Laboratory results show hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL, white blood cell count 2,800/mm³, and platelet count 70,000/mm³. Peripheral blood smear reveals normocytic anemia and reduced cell lines. Which of the following is the next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores that have been occurring for the past 6 months. She describes them as shallow and says they resolve without scarring but recur every few weeks. Additionally, she reports occasional joint pain and swelling, mostly in her knees and ankles, which is worse in the morning. She denies any significant past medical history or family history of similar conditions. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, multiple aphthous-like ulcers are noted in the oral cavity and erythematous genital lesions are present. There is mild tenderness in her knees without swelling. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels, but negative rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic nasal congestion, recurrent sinusitis, and a persistent cough over the past 6 months. She also reports hemoptysis and shortness of breath that have gradually worsened over the last few weeks. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and seasonal allergies. She denies any history of smoking or occupational exposures. On physical examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 140/85 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 20/min. Examination reveals tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and scattered crackles in both lung fields. Laboratory studies show a serum creatinine of 2.3 mg/dL and urinalysis reveals hematuria with red blood cell casts. Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (c-ANCA) are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand while jogging. She describes the pain as sharp and intense, rated 8/10, and it worsens with any attempt to move the wrist. She denies any numbness or tingling in her fingers. Her medical history includes hypertension, which is controlled with lisinopril. She reports no history of joint problems or prior fractures. On examination, the right wrist is swollen, with visible deformity and tenderness over the distal radius. Her vital signs are stable. An X-ray of the wrist is obtained. Which of the following is the most likely finding on the X-ray?

options.C: Colles' fracture of the distal radius
options.G: Wrist soft tissue swelling without fracture

question: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a six-month history of scaly, erythematous plaques on her elbows and knees, along with joint pain predominantly affecting her right knee and left wrist. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes and noticeable fatigue. Her medical history is significant for childhood asthma. She does not recall any recent infections or new medications. She denies alcohol and drug use, and her family history is non-contributory. Physical examination reveals well-demarcated, silvery scaly lesions over the extensor surfaces and mild swelling of the right knee and left wrist, with tenderness on palpation. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-year history of recurrent episodes of joint pain. She reports sudden onset of severe pain, redness, and swelling in her right great toe, occurring approximately 3-4 times a year, each episode lasting about a week. She has also noticed the development of nodules around her elbows and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, and she takes lisinopril. She denies fever, chills, or morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes. Physical examination shows a painful, swollen right great toe and multiple subcutaneous nodules over both elbows. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, normal liver function tests, uric acid level of 10.2 mg/dL, and negative rheumatoid factor. X-rays of the affected toe show soft tissue swelling without erosions.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with discoloration of her skin, which she describes as a purple, net-like pattern on her legs that becomes more pronounced in the cold. She also reports a history of two miscarriages in the past year and has had episodes of deep vein thrombosis, the most recent being three months ago. Her medical history reveals no significant past illnesses. She denies smoking or alcohol use. On examination, her vital signs are normal. Her physical examination is notable for livedo reticularis on her lower extremities. Her laboratory tests show thrombocytopenia and elevated partial thromboplastin time. Which of the following is most likely to be elevated in this patient?

A 60-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of neck pain and tingling in her right hand. The pain is constant and worsens with movement of the neck. She also reports occasional numbness in her fingers, especially after using the computer. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism. Her medications include levothyroxine and lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma or falls. On examination, there is limited range of motion in the cervical spine, and Spurling's maneuver elicits pain radiating to her right arm. Neurologic examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch over the right C6 dermatome. An MRI of the cervical spine shows disc prolapse at the C5-C6 level with mild spinal canal stenosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers and episodes of color changes in her hands when exposed to cold over the past year. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red upon rewarming. She also reports joint pain and fatigue but denies any significant morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with amlodipine. Physical examination reveals shiny, taut skin over the fingers and decreased capillary refill time in the nail beds. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody test and mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old woman presents to her primary care office with severe back pain after lifting a heavy box at home two days ago. She describes the pain as sharp and located in the mid-back region, worsening with movement and deep breathing. Her past medical history is significant for osteoporosis, which she has been managing with calcium and vitamin D supplements. She denies any recent trauma other than lifting the box and has no fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the thoracic vertebrae with no neurological deficits. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR of 55 mm/hr, normal white blood cell count, and normal renal function. X-rays of the thoracic spine reveal a compression fracture at T8. Which of the following is the most likely finding if rheumatoid arthritis was also present in this patient?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the last two weeks following a viral upper respiratory infection. She describes muscle pain that is constant and widespread, affecting her shoulders, back, and thighs. She denies any joint swelling, but reports that her joints feel stiff, particularly in the morning. Additionally, she has experienced decreased energy and difficulty sleeping due to discomfort. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She smokes occasionally and drinks alcohol socially. Physical examination reveals generalized tenderness over multiple muscle groups without redness or swelling of the joints. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and thyroid-stimulating hormone, but elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Order rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.D: Consider a diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis
options.E: Reassure and provide symptomatic treatment with NSAIDs

question: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy. She reports a sore throat and fatigue. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She denies recent travel or contact with sick individuals. On examination, her temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), blood pressure is 120/78 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. She has palpable cervical and axillary lymph nodes that are nontender. Laboratory studies reveal a leukocyte count of 4,500/mm3 with an elevated lymphocyte percentage. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of a persistent net-like rash on her thighs and recurrent episodes of pain and swelling in her right calf over the past year. She also reports occasional headaches and joint pain in her wrists and knees. She has a history of two first-trimester miscarriages. Her temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on her lower extremities and mild swelling of the right calf. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and elevated D-dimer levels. Antinuclear antibody test is positive, and coagulation studies show a prolonged partial thromboplastin time. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.A: Duplex ultrasonography of the lower extremities
options.D: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain

question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of a lacy, purplish discoloration on her lower extremities that becomes more pronounced in cold weather. She also reports a history of recurrent deep vein thromboses and three first-trimester miscarriages. Her only medication is low-dose aspirin, which she started taking after her last miscarriage. She denies any joint pain, skin rashes, or photosensitivity. Family history is unremarkable. On examination, her vital signs are stable, and her BMI is 22 kg/m2. Examination of her skin reveals reticular, violaceous lesions on her thighs. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. Her ANA is negative, but she has a positive lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of skin tightening on her fingers and color changes in her fingers when exposed to cold. She describes the fingertips turning white, then blue, followed by red upon rewarming. She also mentions occasional joint pain and fatigue. She has had difficulty swallowing and a feeling of tightness in her skin over the hands and forearms. On examination, there is thickening and tightening of the skin on her fingers, and small ulcerations at the tips of several fingers. Nailbed capillary microscopy reveals dilated loops and avascular areas. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

A 55-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain, particularly affecting her hands and wrists. She notes morning stiffness lasting for over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a C-reactive protein level of 12 mg/L and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 50 mm/h. Serum testing reveals the presence of anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe left wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand while gardening. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her wrist, with swelling and bruising that developed shortly after the fall. She denies any numbness or tingling in her fingers. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius with visible deformity and limited range of motion due to pain. Neurovascular exam of the hand is normal. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. She reports weakness in her lower extremities, particularly noticeable when climbing stairs. She also mentions occasional tingling sensations in her feet and a general feeling of fatigue. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She has no significant family history and denies recent infections or travel. Her temperature is 98.1°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Neurological examination reveals 3/5 strength in the hip flexors and quadriceps bilaterally, with diminished deep tendon reflexes in the knees. Sensation is intact to light touch and pinprick. An MRI of the lumbar spine shows no evidence of compressive lesions. Blood tests reveal a normal complete blood count, normal electrolytes, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. She describes experiencing aching pain in her muscles and joints, which she says is worse in the morning. There are no specific areas of swelling or redness, and she denies any fevers or weight loss. She reports feeling unrefreshed after sleep and struggles with headaches and impaired concentration. Her past medical history is unremarkable, except for an episode of mononucleosis in college. There is a family history of rheumatoid arthritis in her mother. On physical examination, she appears fatigued, and tenderness is noted at multiple soft tissue sites, but there is no synovitis. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count, normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management for this patient?

options.B: Prescribe a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI)
options.E: Order imaging to rule out osteoarthritis
options.F: Start physical therapy focusing on aerobic exercises
options.G: Conduct sleep study for obstructive sleep apnea

question: A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with diffuse body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fevers for the past 3 weeks. She reports having a viral upper respiratory infection about a month ago but has since noticed persistent joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings. She experiences occasional headaches and difficulty concentrating. On examination, she appears tired but in no apparent distress. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. There is tenderness to palpation of the wrists and knees without significant swelling or redness. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman is referred to the hospital for evaluation of a fever of unknown origin. She reports intermittent fevers up to 39.5°C (103°F) for the past 3 weeks, along with fatigue and weight loss. She has had occasional joint pain but denies any rash, cough, or night sweats. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals a temperature of 38.7°C (101.7°F), blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse of 88/min, and respiration rate of 16/min. There is no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly, but there is mild tenderness in the wrists and knees without swelling. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures, urinalysis, and chest X-ray are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to further investigate this patient's condition?

A 17-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to joint pain and swelling. She reports that over the past two weeks, she has experienced pain in different joints, including her knees, wrists, and elbows, which seem to improve in one joint only to worsen in another. She recalls having a sore throat and fever about three weeks ago, but did not seek medical attention. Physical examination reveals erythema and swelling of the left knee, tenderness of the right wrist, and a faint erythematous rash over her trunk. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of difficulty walking and tripping over her left foot. She reports that she has to lift her left leg higher than usual to avoid dragging her foot. She describes a tingling sensation in the outer aspect of her left leg and reports some mild lower back pain. She has a history of hypothyroidism, well-controlled on levothyroxine. She is otherwise healthy and active. On examination, there is weakness of foot dorsiflexion and eversion on the left side, as well as decreased sensation in the distribution of the superficial peroneal nerve. Her reflexes are 2+ and symmetric throughout, except for an absent ankle jerk on the left. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-week history of fever, fatigue, and generalized body aches. She also notes experiencing night sweats and unintentional weight loss of about 4 pounds over the past month. Her past medical history is notable for hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies alcohol use, smoking, or recreational drug use. Family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, she has a temperature of 38.3°C (100.9°F), a pulse of 90/min, and blood pressure of 122/76 mm Hg. There is mild tenderness over the proximal interphalangeal joints of the hands, although no significant swelling or redness is observed. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

options.A: Repeat complete blood count in 2 weeks

question: A 32-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy presents to the clinic with worsening lower back pain and pelvic discomfort over the past 6 weeks. She describes the pain as dull and aching, exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking. She denies any history of trauma or lifting heavy objects. She reports occasional joint stiffness in the mornings lasting about 15-20 minutes and mild fatigue. On examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and the lower lumbar region. Her gait is altered with a waddling pattern, and she has difficulty with single-leg standing. Laboratory findings show normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent miscarriages and skin changes over the past year. She describes the skin changes as a net-like, bluish pattern on her lower extremities, which she noticed about six months ago. She also reports a history of deep vein thrombosis two years ago, for which she was treated with anticoagulation. She experiences occasional joint pain in her hands and knees, but there is no morning stiffness. Her family history is significant for an aunt with lupus. On examination, she is afebrile, with stable vital signs. Her skin reveals livedo reticularis on her lower legs. Laboratory results show moderate thrombocytopenia and an elevated partial thromboplastin time. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with concerns about new skin lesions and joint pain. She reports the appearance of red, scaly patches on her elbows and knees over the past 3 months. She also experiences pain and swelling in her right knee and left wrist that worsens with activity and has been gradually increasing in severity. Morning stiffness lasts less than 30 minutes. She denies any significant past medical history and is on no regular medications. Physical examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knees. The right knee is swollen with a mild effusion, and the left wrist is tender to palpation. Nail pitting is noted on several fingers. Laboratory tests show negative rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of skin tightening on her fingers and toes. She also reports episodes of finger discoloration, turning white and then blue in cold environments, which are followed by redness upon warming. She has noticed some joint pain and stiffness, especially in her hands, but denies any swelling. She feels more fatigued than usual but attributes it to her stressful job. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 135/85 mm Hg and her heart rate is 78/min. Skin examination shows taut, shiny skin on the fingers with some telangiectasia on the face. Laboratory tests reveal a positive ANA with a nucleolar pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past 6 months. She mentions that her eyes feel gritty, and she struggles with swallowing dry foods. Additionally, she has noticed bilateral swelling in the areas around her jaw that become painful when eating. She denies any significant joint pain but reports occasional fatigue. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not currently taking any medications. On examination, the physician notes bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient's condition?

options.D: CT scan of the parotid glands

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings. She reports that the stiffness lasts for about an hour after she wakes up and affects her hands, wrists, and knees symmetrically. She denies any recent fevers or weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman with no significant past medical history presents to the clinic with new-onset hypertension and generalized swelling. She has been experiencing fatigue and mild joint pain for the past month. The patient mentions significant changes in her urine output and a frothy appearance. Her vital signs include a blood pressure of 160/100 mm Hg, heart rate of 78/min, and a temperature of 37.2°C (99°F). Physical examination reveals bilateral lower extremity edema and mild tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show:

Serum creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, Urinalysis reveals 4+ proteinuria with occasional red blood cell casts, Anti-nuclear antibody test is positive, Rheumatoid factor is elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 42-year-old woman presents to her physician with complaints of fatigue, unexplained weight loss of 10 pounds over the past two months, and a feeling of lightheadedness. She has also noticed paleness and has had episodes of shortness of breath on exertion. Her medical history is notable for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild tachycardia. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 80 fL, and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Serum ferritin is low, and a peripheral blood smear shows microcytic, hypochromic red blood cells. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?

A 30-year-old pregnant woman, currently at 28 weeks gestation, presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of lower back and pelvic pain. She describes the pain as dull and aching, worse with prolonged standing or walking, and somewhat relieved by rest. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms. Her medical history is significant for a prior uncomplicated pregnancy, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited range of motion due to discomfort. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.C: Refer to a physical therapist specializing in pregnancy

question: A 45-year-old woman reports to the clinic with a six-month history of progressive difficulty walking and lower extremity weakness. She notes that she experiences occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, she has 4/5 strength in her hip flexors and quadriceps bilaterally, and her knee reflexes are diminished. There are no sensory deficits. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and normal inflammatory markers. An electromyography study is performed and shows myopathic patterns. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain in her ankles and knees for the past 2 weeks. She describes the joint pain as aching and notes that it is worse with walking and standing. She has no history of trauma. She reports mild fatigue but denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, there are several tender erythematous nodules on the anterior aspects of both shins. The joints are tender to palpation but show no significant swelling or warmth. Laboratory tests show a mild increase in erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Erythema nodosum associated with streptococcal infection

question: A 48-year-old woman visits her physician due to progressive weakness in her shoulders and hips over the past 8 months. She finds it increasingly difficult to climb stairs and lift items above her head. She denies any significant joint pain, skin rashes, or recent illness. Her medical history reveals hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. She does not smoke and consumes alcohol occasionally. Physical examination shows symmetric weakness of the proximal muscles while distal strength is normal. Laboratory tests are notable for an elevated creatine kinase level. Electromyography reveals a pattern consistent with muscle inflammation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosing her condition?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, nasal congestion, and episodes of blood-streaked sputum for the past 4 months. She has also experienced a persistent cough and occasional joint pain, primarily in her wrists and knees. Over the past few weeks, she has noticed dark-colored urine and swelling in her lower extremities. She denies any recent travel or new medications. Her past medical history includes chronic sinusitis, for which she occasionally takes over-the-counter decongestants. On examination, her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), blood pressure is 145/85 mm Hg, pulse is 90/min, and respirations are 16/min. Auscultation of the chest reveals bilateral crackles. There is mild edema in her legs. Laboratory studies show elevated creatinine and the presence of red blood cell casts in the urine. A chest X-ray reveals bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Check for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.G: Provide supportive care with oxygen therapy

question: A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain and progressively limited range of motion. She describes the pain as a constant dull ache that worsens with arm movement. She has no history of trauma to the area. Her past medical history includes hypertension, hypothyroidism, and osteoarthritis. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and significant restriction in both active and passive range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. Her vital signs are within normal limits. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to her rheumatologist with complaints of worsening joint deformities in her hands over the past two years. She describes stiffness in the morning lasting more than an hour and persistent pain in her wrists, elbows, and knees. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is currently taking methotrexate, which she started 6 months ago, and lisinopril. On physical examination, there is notable ulnar deviation of the fingers at the metacarpophalangeal joints and swan-neck deformities of several fingers. Laboratory results reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman comes to her primary care office due to persistent fatigue and unexplained weight gain over the last eight months. She reports experiencing cold intolerance and feels as though her skin has become unusually dry. Additionally, she mentions hair thinning and generalized muscle weakness. Her past medical history is non-contributory, and she denies any joint pain or swelling. On examination, she appears lethargic with dry skin and coarse hair. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated TSH level and low free T4. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has gradually worsened over the past six months. She reports stiffness in the morning lasting about 30 minutes and difficulty climbing stairs. She denies any fever, skin rashes, or recent weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the greater trochanteric area and pain with hip abduction. Her vital signs show temperature 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse 78/min, and blood pressure 132/84 mmHg. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count and chemistry panel, but her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. An X-ray of the pelvis shows mild degenerative changes in the hip joints. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure, joint pain in her hands and knees, and sores in her mouth that have developed over the past month. She reports feeling fatigued and has experienced a 3-kg unintentional weight loss. Her medical history is notable for frequent episodes of sinusitis and two previous episodes of pleuritis. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. Her vital signs show a temperature of 37.5°C, blood pressure 122/76 mm Hg, pulse 84/min, and respiratory rate 18/min. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash over her cheeks and nasal bridge, sparing the nasolabial folds, and tenderness with mild swelling in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, leukocyte count 3,800/mm3, and platelets 150,000/mm3. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein and 3-5 RBCs per high-power field. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that her symptoms are worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving throughout the day. She also notes swelling in her fingers and wrists. She denies any fevers, weight loss, or rashes. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressively worsening tightness of the skin on her fingers over the past 6 months. She also experiences intermittent episodes of finger discoloration, changing from white to blue to red, especially when exposed to cold. The patient notes joint stiffness in the morning lasting about 30 minutes and occasional heartburn. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Physical examination reveals skin thickening over the fingers and hands with reduced digital mobility. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of purple spots on her lower legs and intermittent blood in her urine. She reports mild joint pain and fatigue but denies fever or weight loss. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. On examination, she has palpable purpura on the lower extremities and no other significant findings. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria with mild proteinuria, normal white blood cell count, and elevated creatinine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of burning sensation and tingling in her feet and hands. She describes the symptoms as intermittent but progressively worsening, making it difficult to perform daily activities. She denies any weakness or muscle wasting. She has noticed occasional joint stiffness in her hands, especially in the morning, which resolves within a few hours. She has fatigue but no weight loss or fever. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 5 years ago, controlled with metformin. On examination, her vital signs are normal, and she appears well-nourished. Neurological examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in the distal extremities. Reflexes are normal, and no muscle weakness is noted. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, hemoglobin A1c of 8.2%, and ESR of 20 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past three months. She describes a burning sensation in her toes, which worsens at night, and occasional difficulty in gripping objects. She has noticed muscle weakness in her legs that affects her ability to walk long distances. She denies joint pain or swelling. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is managed with metformin. On physical examination, there is reduced pinprick sensation in a glove-and-stocking distribution and diminished ankle reflexes. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin A1c 7.8%
Vitamin B12 level 250 pg/mL (normal 200-900 pg/mL)
ESR 20 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old pregnant woman in her third trimester presents to the clinic with lower back and pelvic pain. She reports the pain started about a month ago and has gradually worsened, especially after long periods of standing or walking. She denies any fever, weight loss, or morning stiffness but notices the pain is relieved by lying down. She has a history of mild scoliosis but has otherwise been healthy. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the lower lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints. Neurological examination is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet, which she describes as a 'pins and needles' sensation. She also reports occasional burning pain in those areas and some difficulty with fine motor tasks, such as buttoning her clothes. She has a history of hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine, and takes no other medications. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution, and diminished ankle reflexes bilaterally. Muscle strength is preserved. Laboratory evaluation shows normal glucose levels, normal thyroid function, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management step for her symptoms?

options.G: Test for antibodies associated with Sjögren's syndrome

question: A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of right shoulder pain for the past 3 months. The pain started gradually and has become more severe, particularly when she tries to lift her arm overhead or reach behind her back. She denies any trauma or overuse of the joint. She reports mild morning stiffness in other joints that improves after an hour of activity. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, and she is a non-smoker. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the right shoulder with a reduced range of motion, especially in abduction and external rotation. There is no joint swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests show normal white blood cell count, ESR of 30 mm/hr, and CRP of 1.2 mg/dL. A shoulder X-ray is performed and is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits the primary care clinic due to several months of widespread muscle pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. She reports that the pain is persistent and affects her neck, shoulders, and lower back. The pain is often accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She sleeps poorly, often waking up feeling unrefreshed, despite sleeping for over 8 hours each night. She has a history of depression but is currently not on any medication. Her vital signs are normal. Physical examination reveals tenderness in multiple muscle groups but no joint swelling or redness. Muscle strength and range of motion are normal. Laboratory tests, including a complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and thyroid function tests, are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, a newly developed skin rash, and fatigue for the past two weeks. She mentions a recent camping trip to the northeastern United States, where she recalls being bitten by a tick. She describes the rash as initially looking like a small red bump that has now expanded to form a circular rash with central clearing. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness in her knees and elbows. Laboratory findings reveal elevated inflammatory markers. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in the management of this patient?

options.C: Order MRI of the affected joints

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of persistent fever, malaise, and joint pain. Her history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She denies recent travel, new medications, or any past similar episodes. On examination, her temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 124/78 mm Hg, heart rate is 86/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. She has mild swelling and tenderness in both wrists, but no significant erythema. Laboratory findings reveal elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), while antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is negative. Further imaging of the chest and abdomen shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins that developed over the past week. She also reports joint pain in her ankles and knees, as well as fatigue and a mild fever. She has a history of recurrent sore throats and was treated with antibiotics several times in the past year. Physical examination reveals erythematous, tender nodules on the anterior aspects of both lower legs, and mild swelling and tenderness of the ankle joints. Her vital signs are within normal limits, except for a low-grade fever of 100.4°F (38°C). Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and CRP. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth that have been worsening over the past six months. She also reports experiencing a persistent dull ache in her joints, particularly in her hands and wrists, along with general fatigue.
