Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling in her right arm and hand that has been worsening over the past six months. Her symptoms are particularly severe in the mornings, though they improve somewhat as the day progresses. She works as an accountant and notes that typing and desk work seem to exacerbate her symptoms. She denies any trauma or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, she has reduced range of motion in her neck due to pain and decreased sensation in the right C6 dermatome. Spurling's test is positive, and deep tendon reflexes are normal except for a diminished biceps reflex on the right. Cervical spine X-ray shows mild degenerative changes. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of neck pain and tingling sensations in her right arm and hand. She describes the pain as a dull ache that occasionally radiates to her shoulder and is associated with numbness and paresthesias in the right thumb and index finger. Her medical history includes mild hypertension managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss, but notes increased neck stiffness in the mornings. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the cervical spine and diminished sensation in the C6 dermatome on the right. Reflexes are normal. Laboratory tests show: Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, Leukocyte count 8,100/mm3, Platelet count 250,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 25 mm/h, C-reactive protein 1.5 mg/L. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive weakness in her shoulders and hips over the past 4 months. She reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She also mentions mild joint stiffness in the mornings, but it improves within thirty minutes. Her medical history includes mild eczema, and she takes over-the-counter antihistamines occasionally. Family history is non-contributory. On examination, she has 4/5 strength in the proximal muscles of her upper and lower extremities. No skin rashes are noted. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine kinase and aldolase levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with recurrent fevers and generalized lymphadenopathy over the past three weeks. She reports associated night sweats, a 5-pound unintentional weight loss, and fatigue. She denies any recent travel, exposure to sick contacts, or new medications. Her medical history is significant for occasional migraines, for which she takes sumatriptan. On physical examination, she is febrile with a temperature of 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, pulse of 88/min, and respirations of 16/min. There are palpable cervical and inguinal lymph nodes, but no hepatosplenomegaly or skin rashes are noted. Laboratory tests reveal a white blood cell count of 12,000/mm³, elevated ESR, and mild anemia. A chest X-ray shows no mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to establish the diagnosis?

options.B: CT scan of the chest and abdomen

question: A 58-year-old woman is evaluated for fatigue and generalized malaise that have progressively worsened over the past few months. She reports intermittent low-grade fevers and a loss of appetite. On examination, she has multiple firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules located on her forearms and elbows. Her past medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. She denies any joint pain or stiffness, and there is no erythema or swelling around her joints. Her temperature is 37.6°C (99.7°F), pulse is 88/min, and blood pressure is 130/84 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. A chest X-ray is performed and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of joint pain and skin changes. She reports pain and stiffness in her right knee and left ankle, which is worse in the morning and tends to improve throughout the day. She also notes red, scaly patches on her scalp and elbows that have been present for several years. Her mother had similar skin lesions and was diagnosed with a skin condition but the patient is unsure of the specifics. She denies any family history of rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals an erythematous, scaly rash on the extensor surfaces of the elbows and multiple swollen joints with a reduced range of motion. Laboratory studies show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash that appears on her cheeks after sun exposure. She notes that the joint pain, particularly in her hands and knees, is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. She reports sensitivity to sunlight and has developed a facial rash that spares the nasolabial folds. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, her temperature is 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 112/76 mm Hg, pulse is 80/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal examinations are normal. There is a malar rash present on her face. Which of the following is most likely to be elevated in this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-week history of difficulty walking and a sensation of weakness in her right foot. She reports that her toes seem to drag on the ground, and she has tripped several times. She denies any recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. On physical examination, there is a noticeable foot drop on the right side, and she demonstrates a steppage gait. Neurological examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the right lower leg. Tendon reflexes are normal. Laboratory studies show normal thyroid function and a mild elevation of inflammatory markers. Given this presentation, which of the following is most appropriate for evaluating her condition further?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain. She describes the pain as sharp and states that it worsens with movement and at night when lying on her right side. She reports no trauma but mentions that the pain started after an increase in her exercise routine. She has difficulty reaching overhead and has stopped her exercise regimen due to the pain. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and the range of motion is restricted, particularly in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or redness of the joint. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of purplish spots on her legs and joint pain in her knees and ankles. She reports that the rash started on her lower legs and has not spread above her waist. She also notes episodes of blood in her urine over the past week. She denies recent upper respiratory infections or new medications. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower extremities and mild edema of both knees. Laboratory studies show hematuria with red cell casts and normal complement levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl comes to the clinic with her mother, complaining of joint pain that moves from one joint to another over the past 2 weeks. She mentions the pain initially started in her knees and then moved to her wrists and elbows. Two weeks ago, she had a sore throat that resolved without treatment. Her mother reports that she has been more fatigued than usual. Her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 95/min, and respirations are 18/min. A physical examination shows swelling and redness in the affected joints but no tenderness in the throat or lymphadenopathy. There is no heart murmur or skin rash, and she denies any recent trauma or travel. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because of a 2-week history of sore throat, fever, and joint pain. She initially had a sore throat that resolved without treatment. However, she began experiencing migratory joint pain that started in her knees and then moved to her ankles and wrists. She also reports feeling fatigued and having occasional chest discomfort. She denies any skin rashes or photosensitivity. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), pulse is 92/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. On examination, there is mild swelling and tenderness of the wrists and knees. A cardiac examination reveals a soft systolic murmur best heard at the apex. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 11,200/mm3
Platelet count 280,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/h
Antistreptolysin O titer elevated
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of lower back pain and stiffness. The pain is worse in the morning and improves with exercise. She denies fever, recent trauma, or changes in bowel habits. She has been experiencing fatigue and occasional pain in her hips. On physical examination, her vital signs are stable. There is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and limited forward flexion of the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and CRP. HLA-B27 antigen is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of chronic fatigue and difficulty concentrating for the past 6 months. She reports weight gain despite no change in diet or physical activity, cold intolerance, and occasional muscle stiffness. Her menstrual cycles have become irregular over the past year. On examination, she appears tired with dry skin and brittle hair. Her vital signs include temperature of 36.5°C (97.7°F), pulse of 58/min, blood pressure of 118/76 mm Hg, and respirations of 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal a TSH of 8.2 μIU/mL (normal 0.4-4.0 μIU/mL) and a free T4 of 0.7 ng/dL (normal 0.8-1.8 ng/dL). Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past two weeks. She reports that the joint pain is particularly troublesome in her fingers and wrists, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. She denies any recent infections or travel history. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no medications regularly. On examination, she appears pale and has petechiae on her lower extremities. Joint examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory investigations show hemoglobin of 8.2 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,500/mm3, and platelet count of 55,000/mm3. Bone marrow biopsy reveals hypercellularity with increased numbers of immature cells. What is the most likely next step in management for this patient?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with swelling and intense pain in her right big toe over the past 24 hours. She reports having similar episodes affecting the same joint approximately twice a year for the last three years, often resolving spontaneously within days. She denies recent trauma or fever but admits to consuming alcohol regularly. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and chronic kidney disease managed with lisinopril and low-dose aspirin. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, warm, and tender to palpation, with visible nodules over the extensor surfaces of her fingers. Her temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 18/min. Serological studies report a white cell count of 8,000/mm³, elevated serum uric acid level, and a normal rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the foot reveals erosions with overhanging edges in the affected joint. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old woman presents to the clinic with lower back pain that has been gradually worsening over the past year. She describes the pain as a deep ache in the buttocks and lower back, which is worse in the morning and improves with physical activity throughout the day. She also reports occasional stiffness in the hips and difficulty getting out of bed in the morning. The patient denies any recent trauma or fever. Her father has a history of chronic back problems. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and decreased range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is related to this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of unexplained weight gain and periorbital edema. She also reports morning stiffness in her joints that improves throughout the day. She denies any fever, rash, or significant fatigue. Her medical history is notable for mild hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C). She has noticeable periorbital edema and mild swelling in her hands and feet. Laboratory studies are ordered and show:
Hemoglobin: 12.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 37%
Leukocyte count: 7,500/mm^3
Platelet count: 210,000/mm^3

Serum:
Na+: 138 mEq/L
Cl-: 100 mEq/L
K+: 4.6 mEq/L
HCO3-: 22 mEq/L
BUN: 18 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.2 mg/dL

Urinalysis shows proteinuria of 3+. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and a facial rash. She reports that the rash becomes more pronounced when she spends time in sunlight. Additionally, she experiences fatigue and morning stiffness in her wrists and fingers that lasts for over an hour. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg, and pulse is 78/min. Cutaneous examination reveals an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of her nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.G: Order complete blood count and urinalysis

question: A 35-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and occasional genital ulcers. She also reports intermittent joint pain and swelling, particularly in her knees and ankles, and feels fatigued. She denies any significant weight change or fevers. Her medical history is unremarkable except for a recent diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia. She takes a daily multivitamin and over-the-counter pain relief for joint discomfort. On examination, there are multiple aphthous ulcers on the oral mucosa and a shallow ulcer on the labia majora. The joints are warm and mildly swollen. Laboratory studies show normal white blood cell count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and a negative antinuclear antibody test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past several months. She describes experiencing stiffness in her fingers and wrists, particularly in the morning, lasting about an hour. She also notes difficulty in performing daily activities due to pain in her hands and knees. She denies any weight loss, fever, or skin rashes. Her family history is significant for her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr, and positive rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 22-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the pain started in her knees and has moved to her wrists and ankles. The symptoms began shortly after she recovered from a sore throat. She denies fever, rash, or recent travel. Her medical history is significant for mild seasonal allergies. She is not currently taking any medications and has a family history of autoimmune disease in her mother. On examination, she appears uncomfortable while walking, with tenderness and mild swelling in the involved joints. Laboratory studies show a leukocyte count of 10,000/mm³, elevated C-reactive protein, and an antistreptolysin O titer of 500 IU/mL (normal <200 IU/mL). Rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 16-year-old girl presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and fatigue. She mentions that the pain started in her knees and has since moved to her elbows and wrists. Two weeks ago, she had a sore throat that resolved on its own. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. She denies any skin rashes, fever, or headaches. Physical examination reveals a systolic murmur best heard at the apex and mild swelling in the wrists and knees without erythema or warmth. Her vital signs are: temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, pulse 88/min, and respirations 16/min. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) test is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain, periorbital edema, and morning joint stiffness over the past two months. She reports feeling fatigued and mentions that her rings have become tighter. She denies changes in diet or exercise and has no history of recent travel or infections. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals puffy eyelids and mild swelling of the hands. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, but serum creatinine is slightly elevated at 1.3 mg/dL and urinalysis reveals proteinuria. Thyroid function tests are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of painful, red nodules on her shins and generalized joint pain. She also reports feeling fatigued and has had a low-grade fever most evenings. She denies recent travel or changes in her diet. She has a history of irritable bowel syndrome but no other significant medical conditions. Her family history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals multiple tender, erythematous nodules on the anterior surfaces of both legs. Her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. A chest X-ray is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on her face and joint pains. She reports that the rash is exacerbated by sunlight exposure and is accompanied by generalized fatigue and low-grade fevers. She also has intermittent oral ulcers that are not painful. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash over her cheeks that spares the nasolabial folds, and swelling with tenderness in her bilateral knees and wrists. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia and mild leukopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, diffuse bone pain, and recent episodes of constipation. She has a 3-month history of worsening joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings, but is more concerned about her generalized weakness and bone discomfort. She denies any recent trauma or changes in physical activity. On examination, she appears fatigued with mild tenderness over the sternum and lumbar spine. Her blood pressure is 128/78 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C). Laboratory studies reveal:

Serum calcium: 11.8 mg/dL
Phosphate: 2.4 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase: 145 U/L
Parathyroid hormone: 150 pg/mL
25-hydroxyvitamin D: 18 ng/mL

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset pain and swelling in her right big toe that began last night. She describes the pain as severe and states that it was difficult to sleep because the weight of the bed sheets exacerbated the pain. She has had similar episodes in the past, which resolved spontaneously after a few days. She denies fever, chills, or recent trauma. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes amlodipine and atorvastatin. On examination, her temperature is 98.8°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 138/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 18/min. Her right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, warm, and extremely tender to touch, with overlying skin desquamation. Laboratory studies reveal a serum uric acid level of 8.9 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in this patient's management?

options.B: Aspiration and analysis of synovial fluid
options.F: Order an X-ray of the affected joint

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening fatigue, generalized bone pain, and occasional nausea. She describes the pain as dull and persistent, primarily affecting her lower back and hips. She denies any recent trauma or significant changes in physical activity. Her medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Family history is significant for osteoporosis in her mother. On examination, she has mild tenderness over the lumbar spine and hips. Laboratory studies reveal: calcium 12.1 mg/dL, phosphorus 2.3 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, and alkaline phosphatase 160 U/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive weakness in her arms and thighs over the past 3 months. Initially, she had difficulty climbing stairs and lifting heavy objects, but now even combing her hair is challenging. She denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. She reports occasional difficulty swallowing but denies any changes in her speech. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, which is well controlled with levothyroxine. Her family history is unremarkable. She works as a librarian and lives alone. On examination, she has symmetric proximal muscle weakness, but distal strength is preserved. There is no skin rash or joint swelling. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase levels. An electromyography study reveals myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue, bone pain, and generalized weakness for the past two months. She also reports an unintentional weight loss of 5 kg in this period. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for well-controlled hypertension. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated calcium level of 12.5 mg/dL, and renal function tests are within normal limits. She denies any recent changes in medication or diet. On physical examination, there is mild tenderness over the lumbar spine, but no joint swelling or erythema. The patient reports intermittent nausea but denies any episodes of vomiting. An X-ray of the lumbar spine shows osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 56-year-old woman visits the clinic with complaints of tingling and numbness in her hands and feet for the past 3 months. She describes a 'pins and needles' sensation that is more pronounced at night and has begun to interfere with her sleep. She also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue but denies any recent weight loss or fever. She has a history of type 2 diabetes, managed with metformin and diet control. Physical examination reveals decreased pinprick and vibration sensation in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory tests show normal glucose levels, hemoglobin A1c of 6.5%, and mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely cause of her neuropathy?

A 28-year-old woman is evaluated in the emergency department for a 2-week history of persistent fever and fatigue. She also reports generalized body aches and occasional joint pain. She denies any recent travel or known sick contacts. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), pulse is 102/min, and blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg. There is tenderness in the wrists and knees, but no joint swelling or redness. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Leukocyte count 12,000/mm3
Platelet count 320,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 55 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 60 mg/L
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.A: Start empirical antibiotics for possible infection
options.D: Order a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to your clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and weight gain over the past six months. She also reports feeling cold more often than usual, experiencing dry skin, and noticing some thinning hair. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and osteoarthritis. She is currently taking lisinopril and ibuprofen as needed for joint pain. She denies any recent illnesses or significant changes in lifestyle. On physical examination, her temperature is 36.5°C (97.7°F), blood pressure is 128/82 mm Hg, pulse rate is 58/min, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Her face appears puffy, and her skin is dry. Laboratory tests reveal a high TSH level and low free T4. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Referral to a rheumatologist for suspicion of systemic lupus erythematosus
options.D: Conduct rheumatoid factor testing for rheumatoid arthritis
options.E: Order a bone density scan to evaluate for osteoporosis
options.F: Begin a gluten-free diet for possible celiac disease

question: A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic reporting widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping over the past several months. She states that the pain is not localized to any particular joint and describes it as a deep, aching sensation. Her sleep is frequently disrupted despite getting an adequate number of hours, and she feels unrefreshed in the morning. She denies morning stiffness or swelling of the joints. Her medical history includes anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals diffuse tenderness upon palpation of soft tissues, particularly in areas such as the neck and shoulders, but no swelling or erythema in any joints. Laboratory studies reveal normal complete blood count and metabolic panel results. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.D: Advise physical therapy and stretching exercises
options.E: Order an MRI of the cervical spine

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness for the past six months. She describes the stiffness as being worse in the morning, lasting more than an hour before improving with activity. The pain primarily affects her wrists and proximal interphalangeal joints symmetrically. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On physical examination, the patient exhibits swelling, tenderness, and warmth over the affected joints, but there are no deformities or nodules present. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated C-reactive protein. A hand X-ray demonstrates periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive tightening of the skin on her fingers and hands over the past 8 months. She reports that her fingers turn white and then blue when exposed to cold, accompanied by mild discomfort. She also mentions occasional joint pain and fatigue, but denies any significant morning stiffness. Her medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is taut, shiny skin on her fingers, with some digital pitting. Capillary refill is delayed in all fingers. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

An 8-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic by her parents because of a 2-week history of a rash on her legs and episodes of dark urine. She recently recovered from a sore throat 3 weeks ago, which her parents attributed to the common cold. The rash is described as red spots and bruises that do not blanch with pressure, primarily located on her lower extremities. She also complains of mild abdominal pain and joint aches but denies any recent trauma or insect bites. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99.0°F), blood pressure is 105/65 mm Hg, pulse is 92/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Laboratory studies demonstrate hematuria, mild proteinuria, and a normal serum creatinine level. A urinalysis further confirms the presence of red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and a red rash that appeared after a recent hiking trip. She recalls that she was bitten by a tick approximately three weeks ago. Since then, she has developed flu-like symptoms including fatigue, low-grade fever, and myalgias. Her joint pain mainly involves the knees and is associated with swelling and stiffness, particularly in the morning. She denies any past medical history and is not on any medications. On examination, there is a large, erythematous, bullseye-shaped rash on her left thigh. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory studies show mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 34-year-old woman presents to her physician with a one-month history of painful, red nodules on her shins. She also reports joint pain in her knees and ankles, along with fatigue and a low-grade fever. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. She denies recent travel, new medications, or significant changes in diet. Her vital signs are stable, with a temperature of 99.5°F (37.5°C), blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination reveals multiple tender, erythematous nodules over the anterior tibial surfaces. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and a C-reactive protein level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Erythema nodosum secondary to streptococcal infection

question: A 45-year-old female comes to the clinic due to diffuse body aches and fatigue that began after recovering from a viral upper respiratory infection two weeks ago. She describes the pain as generalized and persistent, affecting her sleep. She denies any joint swelling but feels as though her muscles are constantly sore. She has a history of hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 76/min, and blood pressure is 122/78 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals no joint swelling or erythema, but there is tenderness to palpation over several muscle groups and fatigue on exertion. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, but her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of chronic fatigue and occasional joint discomfort. She reports having gained weight and feeling cold intolerant. She denies any fevers or significant joint swelling. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications. Physical examination reveals dry skin and mild puffiness around the eyes. Her vital signs are: temperature 98.1°F (36.7°C), blood pressure 120/78 mmHg, heart rate 60/min, and respiratory rate 16/min. Laboratory tests show the following:
Hemoglobin 12.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count 4,800/mm3
TSH 8.5 mIU/L (normal 0.4–4.0 mIU/L)
Free T4 0.7 ng/dL (normal 0.8–1.8 ng/dL)
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with pain and swelling of her hands and wrists for the past 6 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. She also mentions feeling more fatigued than usual. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and she is currently taking lisinopril and atorvastatin. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are elevated. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the urgent care clinic with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling after playing a game of tennis. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the anterior and lateral aspects of the knee, with difficulty bearing weight. No fever, chills, or systemic symptoms are reported. She has an unremarkable medical history, except for occasional migraines. On examination, her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 118/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. The right knee is visibly swollen with a moderate effusion and tenderness over the lateral joint line, but no erythema or warmth. Lachman test is positive, and there is limited range of motion due to pain. Radiographs of the knee show no fractures or dislocations. Laboratory studies reveal: 
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
C-reactive protein 0.4 mg/dL
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 12 mm/hr
Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in managing this patient?

options.B: Order magnetic resonance imaging of the knee
options.E: Apply a knee brace and instruct on rest and ice

question: A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic with severe lower back pain that began suddenly after she attempted to lift a heavy box. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her lower back without radiation. She reports a history of osteopenia and denies recent trauma other than the lifting incident. She feels more fatigued than usual and notes occasional stiffness in her hands and knees, especially in the morning. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals midline tenderness over the lumbar spine with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the lumbar spine shows a compression fracture at L3. Which of the following is the best initial step in management for this patient?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive bilateral hip pain over the past 6 months. She reports stiffness when getting out of bed in the morning, which diminishes as the day progresses. Recently, she has experienced difficulty climbing stairs and occasional fatigue. She has no significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her vital signs are normal. There is tenderness upon palpation of both hips, with limited range of motion in internal and external rotation. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 40 mm/h, and a C-reactive protein level of 8 mg/L. Hip X-rays show narrowing of the joint space bilaterally without erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising for the past two months. She notes that the joint pain is primarily in her hands and wrists and tends to worsen in the evening. Over the past few weeks, she has developed increased bleeding from small cuts and noticed petechiae on her legs. She denies any recent infections or significant weight loss. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Family history reveals that her mother had systemic lupus erythematosus. On examination, she appears pale, and there is tenderness in multiple small joints of the hands without significant swelling. Laboratory results show hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL, leukocytes of 2,500/mm³, platelets of 120,000/mm³, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive weakness in her shoulders and hips, making it difficult for her to climb stairs or carry groceries. She denies joint swelling or pain but mentions feeling more fatigued than usual. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-managed with medication. She denies fever, weight loss, or changes in her skin. On examination, there is notable weakness in the proximal muscles of the arms and thighs. Reflexes are intact, and no sensory deficits are found. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and mild elevation of inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute onset of right knee pain and swelling for the past 24 hours, which began after a vigorous hiking trip. She recalls no significant trauma to the knee during the activity. Her medical history includes asthma, well-controlled with inhaled corticosteroids, and seasonal allergies. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, the right knee is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation with a limited range of motion. Her left knee, elbows, and hands show no signs of swelling or tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal a mild leukocytosis with an elevated C-reactive protein level. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations but reveals mild effusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's knee condition?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, low-grade fever, and painful lumps under the skin on her arms and legs for the past 2 weeks. She describes the nodules as hard, non-itchy, and located on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and legs. She denies any joint pain or swelling. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. She has no family history of autoimmune diseases. On physical examination, multiple firm, subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of her forearms and shins. There is no erythema or warmth over the nodules, and joint examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP, with normal rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg (11 lbs). She reports progressive swelling and tenderness in her cervical and axillary lymph nodes, alongside intermittent joint pain in her wrists and knees. She denies fever or rash but mentions occasional shortness of breath. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. Physical examination reveals multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes in the neck and axilla. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of tingling and burning sensations in her feet and hands for the past month. She describes the symptoms as persistent and worsening at night, interfering with her sleep. She has had occasional episodes of joint pain and morning stiffness over the past year, which she attributes to aging. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago, currently managed with metformin. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive weight gain over the last 6 months, along with notable facial puffiness, particularly around her eyes in the morning. She also reports stiffness in her joints, which is most pronounced upon waking and gradually improves throughout the day. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes over-the-counter antihistamines as needed. She denies any recent changes in diet or exercise habits. On examination, she has periorbital edema and mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated TSH levels. Her vital signs are within normal limits. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. She reports frequent difficulty in swallowing dry foods and has noticed bilateral swelling in the areas beneath her ears, which increases in size during meals. She also mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing occasional joint pain in her hands. She denies having any recent infections or significant weight changes. Her vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, her oral mucosa appears dry, and the parotid glands are enlarged bilaterally. There is no lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fever, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. She reports occasional headaches but denies any recent travel or known sick contacts. She also mentions having mild joint pain in her wrists and knees. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for a tonsillectomy in childhood. On examination, she appears fatigued, with no focal neurological deficits. Her temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure 112/70 mmHg, pulse 88/min, and respirations 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Further rheumatologic evaluation for rheumatoid arthritis

question: A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and joint pain for the past two weeks. She recently recovered from a viral upper respiratory infection. She describes the pain as being more pronounced in the mornings and affecting her shoulders, wrists, and knees. She denies any significant weight loss or fever. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, she is afebrile with normal vital signs. There is no evidence of joint swelling or deformity, but the patient has generalized tenderness over her shoulders and knees. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with symptoms of persistent fatigue and joint pain for the past 3 months. She notes the pain is mostly in her wrists and knees and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. Additionally, she reports experiencing increased fatigue and occasional shortness of breath. Her past medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections, but she has no known allergies. Her maternal grandmother had a history of lupus. On examination, she appears pale and exhibits mild tenderness and swelling in the wrists and knees. Her vital signs include temperature 37.2°C, pulse 95/min, respirations 18/min, blood pressure 112/68 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin 8 g/dL, leukocyte count 3,200/mm³, and platelet count 120,000/mm³. The reticulocyte count is low, and a peripheral blood smear shows normocytic, normochromic anemia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to sudden onset of severe knee pain and swelling after participating in a local marathon. She describes the pain as sharp and notes that her knee has become swollen and difficult to move. She denies any history of arthritis or joint injuries. Her medical history is notable for asthma, managed with inhaled corticosteroids. On examination, her left knee is tender and erythematous, with an effusion and warmth to touch. Laboratory tests show a white blood cell count of 11,500/mm³. Renal and liver function tests are normal. An X-ray of the affected knee shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of joint pain and a rash on her legs. She reports that the joint pain began shortly after returning from a camping trip in the northeastern United States, where she recalls being bitten by several ticks. The pain affects her knees and ankles bilaterally and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting over an hour. She also describes a red, expanding rash with a clear center on her left calf, which developed a few days after the tick bites. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, the patient has tender and swollen knees and a circular rash on her calf. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and blood pressure is 118/75 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman, pregnant at 30 weeks, presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back and pelvic pain for the past 3 weeks. The pain is constant, worsens with standing, and occasionally radiates to her thighs. She denies fever, joint swelling, or skin rash. She reports difficulty sleeping due to the discomfort and has been trying to manage it with heat packs and acetaminophen, but with minimal relief. Her prenatal course has been uncomplicated so far, and she is up to date with her prenatal care. On physical examination, she is afebrile, and her vital signs are within normal limits. There is tenderness over the lumbar spine and sacroiliac joints, but no swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies show:
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
Hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL
Platelet count 210,000/mm3
C-reactive protein 2 mg/L
Urinalysis normal
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.F: Advise on maternity support belt use

question: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of diffuse body aches and joint stiffness that she noticed after recovering from a flu-like illness. She reports feeling fatigued and having a low-grade fever for the past few days. She describes the pain as most pronounced in her shoulders and knees, but not limited to these areas. She denies any skin rashes, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Her past medical history is notable for mild seasonal allergies. On physical exam, she appears tired, with slight swelling and tenderness in her knees and shoulders, but no significant warmth or erythema. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, but normal complete blood count and liver function tests. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits her primary care physician due to a rash on her face and joint pain for the past 3 weeks. She describes the rash as appearing on her cheeks and nose, worsening with sun exposure. Her joint pain involves her hands and knees, is symmetric, and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. She denies any fevers or recent infections. Her medical history includes migraines and mild seasonal allergies. She is not on any regular medication. On examination, the malar rash is noted, and there is tenderness and mild swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and knee joints. Her vital signs are stable, and the rest of her physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. What is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis of this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past three months. She also reports experiencing increased shortness of breath on exertion and occasional dizziness. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, well-controlled on levothyroxine. The patient is a non-smoker, does not consume alcohol, and has no family history of malignancy. On examination, she appears pale, and there are no signs of hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 8.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 72 fL, and low serum ferritin. Additional laboratory tests show a normal thyroid function panel. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.G: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis

question: A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of chronic lower back pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. She states that the pain is most severe in the morning and improves as the day progresses with physical activity. She also reports stiffness in her lower back that lasts about an hour each morning. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she denies any recent trauma or injury. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). X-rays of the lumbar spine show fusion of the sacroiliac joints. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 28-year-old woman visits her primary care physician complaining of tingling and burning sensations in her feet and hands for the past 3 months. She also reports occasional joint pain, but her primary concern is the persistent paresthesia which is affecting her daily activities. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she is on levothyroxine. She denies any alcohol or drug use. Her family history is notable for diabetes in her father. Physical examination reveals reduced pinprick sensation in a glove and stocking distribution. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 115/75 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies show normal blood glucose levels but elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.F: TSH and free T4 level assessment

question: A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of joint pain and skin changes. She reports that her knees and ankles are particularly affected, with swelling and pain that are worse in the morning and improve as the day progresses. She also mentions the development of red, scaly patches on her elbows and scalp. She denies any significant weight loss, fever, or prolonged fatigue. Family history reveals that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there is asymmetric swelling and tenderness of the joints of her fingers and toes. There are erythematous, scaly plaques on her elbows and scalp. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated ESR, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash. She reports that the symptoms started about two weeks ago, shortly after she returned from a camping trip where she was bitten by a tick. The pain is mainly in her knees and elbows, with some morning stiffness that lasts about 30 minutes. She also has noticed a circular rash on her left thigh. She denies any recent fever, fatigue, or weight loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On physical examination, there is a well-demarcated erythematous rash with central clearing, consistent with erythema migrans. Her knees are slightly swollen and tender to palpation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and dry skin over the past several months. She reports feeling cold more frequently than usual and has noticed some swelling in the front of her neck. She denies any significant change in her diet or exercise routine. She has no history of autoimmune diseases but mentions that her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, her heart rate is 60/min, blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg, and she has a slight swelling in the anterior neck region. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated TSH level with low free T4. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of progressive joint deformities in her hands and wrists over the past 6 years. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks. Her medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and ulnar deviation of the metacarpophalangeal joints and subluxation at the wrists. Laboratory tests show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands demonstrate joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following laboratory findings is most consistent with her diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances for the past 6 months. She describes her pain as a constant dull ache affecting her neck, shoulders, back, and hips. She often feels unrested after sleep and has difficulty concentrating during the day. Her medical history is unremarkable except for mild depression, for which she takes sertraline. On physical examination, there is tenderness at multiple soft tissue sites, but no joint swelling or deformity is noted. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and comprehensive metabolic panel. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein are within normal limits. Rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibody tests are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash that worsens with sun exposure. She notes that the joint pain, particularly in her wrists and knees, has been persistent over the past few weeks. She also reports experiencing painful oral ulcers that have been present intermittently. Past medical history is significant for occasional migraines, but she is otherwise healthy. Physical examination reveals a maculopapular rash on the face sparing the nasal folds, oral ulcers, and tenderness in the wrists and knees without swelling or warmth. Laboratory tests show a mild leukopenia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Urinalysis is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 6-month history of skin tightening on her fingers and intermittent episodes of color changes in her fingers upon exposure to cold. She describes her fingers turning white, then blue, and finally red with a burning sensation. She also notes some joint pain and stiffness in the morning, which resolves within 30 minutes. She feels tired but attributes it to her busy work schedule. Her medical history is significant for well-controlled hypothyroidism. Physical examination shows shiny, thickened skin over the fingers and decreased handgrip strength. Her blood pressure is 125/75 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL
Leukocyte count 6,000/mm3
Eosinophils 4%
Lymphocytes 32%
Monocytes 6%
Platelet count 250,000/mm3
ESR 22 mm/hr
ANA positive with speckled pattern
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe pain in her left wrist following a fall on an outstretched hand earlier today. She reports swelling and inability to move her wrist without significant pain. She denies hitting her head or losing consciousness. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril, and osteoarthritis. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or fatigue. On examination, there is noticeable swelling and tenderness over the distal radius, with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her current symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with a 6-month history of chronic fatigue and occasional joint pain. She reports feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and has difficulty concentrating at work. She also notes experiencing irregular menstrual cycles and weight gain despite a reduced appetite. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears fatigued but is afebrile. Her blood pressure is 120/75 mmHg, pulse is 70/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals mild swelling of the hands with tenderness in several proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal:

Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH): 8.5 mIU/L (elevated)
Free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (low)
Antithyroid peroxidase antibody: positive
Hemoglobin: 13.5 g/dL
White blood cell count: 6,200/mm^3
C-reactive protein (CRP): 1.2 mg/dL (borderline high)

What is the most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 35-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of a facial rash and joint pain that have persisted for the last 3 months. She notes that the rash worsens with sun exposure and describes the joint pain as particularly severe in the morning, lasting for about an hour before improving. She denies hair loss or mouth ulcers. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, along with tender and swollen wrists and finger joints. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following diagnostic tests is most appropriate to perform next?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin changes and intermittent color changes of her fingers, especially when exposed to cold. These episodes involve blanching followed by cyanosis and erythema. She also reports stiffness and tightening of the skin on her fingers, making it difficult to make a fist. These symptoms have gradually progressed over the past year. Her past medical history is significant for occasional heartburn and fatigue. Physical examination reveals taut, shiny skin on the fingers and hands, along with pitting at the fingertips. She denies any significant joint pain or swelling. Laboratory tests show a positive ANA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with a 3-month history of skin lesions and joint pain. She reports that the lesions are red, scaly, and located on her elbows and knees. She has also noticed stiffness and swelling in her right knee and left ankle, which worsens with prolonged sitting and improves with movement. She denies any recent fevers or weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for obesity and hypertension. On physical examination, there are well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales on her extensor surfaces. Her right knee and left ankle are warm and tender to palpation with reduced range of motion. Laboratory studies reveal a negative rheumatoid factor and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of skin changes and color changes in her fingers when exposed to cold. She reports that her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon rewarming. She also describes increasing tightness and thickening of the skin on her fingers, which has resulted in difficulty performing tasks such as buttoning her shirt. She occasionally experiences joint pain in her hands and wrists but has no significant morning stiffness. The patient has a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease managed with omeprazole. On physical examination, there is sclerodactyly of the fingers and telangiectasias on the face. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody titer with a speckled pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of right shoulder pain that has progressively worsened over the past 3 months. She reports difficulty performing overhead activities and has limited range of motion in the affected shoulder. The patient denies any trauma or recent upper respiratory infections. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder with pain on abduction and external rotation. The Neer and Hawkins tests are positive. Laboratory studies reveal: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 18 mm/h, C-reactive protein 0.8 mg/dL, Rheumatoid factor negative. X-ray of the shoulder shows no acute bony abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 65-year-old woman presents to the clinic with sudden onset of severe back pain following a minor fall while gardening. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her mid-back, with no radiation. She has a history of postmenopausal osteoporosis but has been non-compliant with her calcium and vitamin D supplements. She denies any recent weight loss, fever, or night sweats. On examination, she has tenderness over the thoracic spine but no neurological deficits. Laboratory studies show normal inflammatory markers and no signs of infection. Given her presentation, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 2-month history of right shoulder pain and stiffness. She reports that the pain is constant, worsens with movement, and is accompanied by a decreased range of motion. She denies any trauma or recent infections. Her past medical history includes obesity, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes, for which she takes metformin and lisinopril. She denies smoking and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is a marked limitation in both active and passive range of motion of the right shoulder, especially in abduction and external rotation. There is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder, but no swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests show normal levels of C-reactive protein and a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. X-rays of the shoulder reveal mild degenerative joint changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and a dragging sensation in her right foot for the past 2 weeks. She reports tripping over her toes frequently and notes that her right foot slaps the ground when she walks. She denies any pain but mentions intermittent numbness and tingling over the dorsum of her right foot. The patient has a history of well-controlled type 1 diabetes since childhood and was recently diagnosed with hypertension. Her medications include insulin and lisinopril. On examination, there is weakness of ankle dorsiflexion in the right foot, and sensory examination reveals decreased sensation in the first web space. Reflexes are normal. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and a rash. She reports that the pain affects her knees and ankles, and she has noticed increased fatigue. A week ago, she developed a round, red rash on her right thigh, which she believes began after a camping trip in the northeastern United States. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 92/min, and blood pressure is 120/76 mm Hg. Physical examination reveals mildly swollen and tender knees and ankles. A circular erythematous rash with central clearing, measuring approximately 10 cm in diameter, is noted on her right thigh. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and mildly decreased hemoglobin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of progressively worsening difficulty walking. She describes weakness in her legs and frequent tripping over the last few weeks. She also reports intermittent joint pain and morning stiffness that lasts for about 30 minutes. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled with medications. On examination, there is notable weakness in the lower extremities, more pronounced proximally, and diminished deep tendon reflexes in the knees and ankles. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact. Laboratory studies reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. MRI of the lumbar spine shows mild degenerative changes without significant nerve root compression. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Perform electromyography and nerve conduction studies
options.B: Initiate physical therapy for muscle strengthening

question: A 65-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with severe back pain that started suddenly after she lifted a heavy box. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to her lower thoracic spine. She denies any preceding trauma or falls. Her medical history is significant for osteoporosis, for which she takes calcium and vitamin D supplements. She also reports fatigue and occasional joint stiffness in the mornings, but no swelling or redness in her joints. On examination, there is tenderness over the T11 and T12 vertebrae, but no neurological deficits. Her vital signs are within normal limits. A lateral spine X-ray shows a wedge-shaped deformity of the T11 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 3-week history of fatigue, weight loss, and painful nodules on her elbows. She describes the nodules as firm and non-tender on the extensor surfaces, which have gradually increased in number. She also reports occasional low-grade fevers and joint stiffness in the morning, especially in her wrists and knees, which improves throughout the day. She denies any recent infections or new medications. Her medical history is notable for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, there are multiple subcutaneous nodules on her elbows and forearms, and mild swelling in her wrists. Her temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg. Laboratory tests reveal a white blood cell count of 8,500/mm3, hemoglobin of 11 g/dL, and an elevated C-reactive protein level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, occasional nosebleeds, and a chronic cough. Recently, she has experienced increasing fatigue and joint pain, especially in her wrists and knees. She reports unintentional weight loss of 4 kg (9 lbs) over the last 3 months. Her past medical history is notable for recurrent sinusitis. She denies any skin rashes or photosensitivity. On examination, her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), blood pressure is 145/90 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. There is bilateral tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and auscultation of the lungs reveals crackles in the right lower lobe. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and an increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness. She reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning and lasts for over an hour before improving as the day progresses. She has noticed swelling in the small joints of her hands and feet, and the symptoms are symmetric in nature. Her past medical history is significant for mild hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She has no known allergies and her family history is unremarkable. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor (RF). Anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) is also positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old female software engineer presents to her primary care physician with joint pain and a rash. She reports that the symptoms started about a week ago and have been progressively worsening. She recalls being bitten by a tick during a camping trip in the northeastern United States three weeks ago. The joint pain is primarily in her knees and ankles, and she notes that they are slightly swollen. She also has a distinctive rash on her thigh, which she describes as a red, expanding patch with some central clearing. She denies any significant fever, fatigue, or recent illnesses. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications regularly. On examination, her joints are mildly tender with reduced range of motion. The rash on her thigh is approximately 10 cm in diameter with a classic bull's-eye appearance. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial step in management?

A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a complaint of left shoulder pain that has been progressively worsening over the past few months. She reports that the pain is constant, even at rest, and that she has difficulty lifting her arm above her head. She denies any recent trauma or injury to the area. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, controlled with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She describes the pain as aching and sometimes sharp, with limited range of motion. On examination, the shoulder is tender to palpation, and there is a reduced active and passive range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. X-rays of the shoulder show no fractures or dislocations. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but her rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past two months. She reports feeling extremely tired, even after a full night's sleep, and has experienced intermittent pain in her wrists and knees. Over the past month, she has developed several unexplained bruises on her arms and legs. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. On physical examination, she appears pale and has petechiae on her lower extremities. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 9.4 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,500/mm3, and platelet count of 75,000/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of lower back pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. She also reports stiffness lasting for over an hour after waking up. She has no significant past medical history and is not on any medications. She denies recent trauma or injury. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. HLA-B27 testing is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive bilateral hip pain over the past 8 months. The pain is described as a deep ache that worsens with prolonged standing or walking and is partially relieved by rest. She has also noticed stiffness in the morning lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. She works as a teacher and has difficulty with her usual activities due to the pain. Physical examination reveals tenderness in the hip joints with a slightly reduced range of motion. Laboratory tests show a mildly elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.B: Hip joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis

question:
A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and swelling. She reports that the symptoms began after returning from a trip to the Caribbean. The joint pain started in her wrists and fingers and has since spread to her knees and ankles. She also experiences intermittent fevers, fatigue, and a skin rash on her torso. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, she has swelling and tenderness in her wrists, knees, and ankles. There is also a maculopapular rash noted on her back and abdomen. Laboratory studies show:
Leukocyte count 8,500/mm3
Platelet count 280,000/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 45 mm/h
C-reactive protein 4.2 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressive deformity in her hands over the past year. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day but returns with more activity. She also notes fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. Her medical history includes hypertension and hypothyroidism, for which she takes lisinopril and levothyroxine, respectively. On physical examination, there are ulnar deviations at the metacarpophalangeal joints and subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her elbows. Her laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of right shoulder pain and limited range of motion for the past 2 months. She states that the pain is dull and aching and worsens with movement. She denies any recent trauma, but mentions that she had a similar issue with her left shoulder last year, which resolved spontaneously. She reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes and occasional fatigue, but no fevers or weight loss. On physical examination, there is a reduced active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, especially in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or erythema, and neurovascular examination is normal. Laboratory tests reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 40 mm/hr, C-reactive protein of 8 mg/L, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic with worsening bone pain that has persisted for several months. She notes that the pain is particularly severe in her hips and legs and is accompanied by a sense of fatigue. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled with medications. She is postmenopausal and does not take any hormone replacement therapy. Physical examination reveals tenderness upon palpation of the long bones, and laboratory testing shows elevated levels of alkaline phosphatase with normal calcium and phosphate levels. Her vitamin D levels are also within normal limits. A skeletal survey is ordered and shows mixed lytic and sclerotic lesions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive tingling and numbness in her hands and feet. She also reports intermittent joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. She denies any significant weight loss, fever, or night sweats. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, there is mild weakness in her distal upper and lower extremities, and sensory testing reveals decreased sensation to light touch and vibration. Reflexes are 2+ throughout. Laboratory studies reveal mild microcytic anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old woman presents to the clinic with chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in both arms for the past 6 months.
