She also reports recurrent fevers, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 4 kg (8.8 lbs) over the past month. She mentions occasional joint pain in her wrists and knees, but says it is not severe. On examination, there is palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated ESR and CRP levels. A biopsy of one of the axillary lymph nodes shows a mix of small and large atypical lymphocytes with some areas of necrosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis based on these findings?

A 27-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure, joint pain, and mouth sores over the past three months. She has a family history of autoimmune disease, with her mother having rheumatoid arthritis. She reports fatigue and occasional fever but denies any recent infections. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 118/72 mm Hg, and heart rate is 92/min. On physical examination, there is an erythematous rash across her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Oral examination reveals ulcers on her hard palate. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and mild anemia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman visits her physician due to a 6-month history of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth. She also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. She states that her symptoms are getting progressively worse, and recently, she noticed swelling and discomfort in the area below her ears. She denies any difficulty swallowing or symptoms of gastroesophageal reflux. Her medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, she is afebrile with a blood pressure of 118/76 mm Hg and a pulse of 72/min. There is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Schirmer's test shows decreased tear production. Laboratory tests reveal a leukocyte count of 6,500/mm3 and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 36 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of worsening left shoulder pain and stiffness. She reports difficulty lifting her arm above her head and reaching behind her back. The pain is dull and constant, and she has noticed it is particularly severe in the mornings, but improves slightly as the day progresses. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or significant weight loss. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the left shoulder with pain on both active and passive movement, particularly with abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or warmth over the joint. Laboratory studies show a normal white blood cell count, C-reactive protein level of 10 mg/L, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 30 mm/h. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of difficulty lifting her right foot and tripping over her toes while walking. She reports associated numbness and tingling in the top of her right foot and the outer part of her leg. She denies any recent injuries or new physical activities. Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, treated with levothyroxine. On examination, there is weakness of right ankle dorsiflexion, decreased sensation over the right lateral shin and dorsum of the foot, and a high-stepping gait. Reflexes are normal and symmetric in both legs. The remainder of her neurologic examination is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the last 3 months. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. The patient denies any recent infections, skin rashes, or gastrointestinal symptoms. She works as a graphic designer and her symptoms have started affecting her ability to work. On examination, there is synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with mild swelling but no deformity. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor, elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies, and an ESR of 48 mm/h. Hand X-rays show mild joint space narrowing without erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with joint pain and swelling that began 6 months ago. She reports that her symptoms initially started in her fingers and wrists, and have progressively worsened. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves throughout the day with activity. She denies any recent illnesses, fevers, weight loss, or skin changes. She has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain relief. Her family history reveals that her mother has a history of an autoimmune disease. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr (normal: <20 mm/hr) and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of her hands shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 78-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of worsening back pain. She describes the pain as a constant dull ache that occasionally becomes sharp with sudden movements. The pain began after she bent over to lift a heavy pot, and she denies any history of trauma or fall. Her past medical history includes osteoporosis and hypertension. She takes alendronate for her osteoporosis and lisinopril for her hypertension. She denies any numbness, tingling, or weakness in her legs. On physical examination, she has tenderness over the thoracic and lumbar vertebrae, particularly at T8 and L1. There is no spinal deformity, and her neurologic examination is normal. Her laboratory results are as follows:
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Leukocytes 6,400/mm3
Platelets 210,000/mm3
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL
ESR 25 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 1.0 mg/dL
Rheumatoid factor Negative
Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with acute left knee pain and swelling that began after she completed a short-distance run two days ago. She describes the pain as throbbing and notes that it worsens when she tries to bend the knee. She has no significant past medical history and reports being generally healthy, although she occasionally feels fatigued after exercise. Her weight is stable, and she denies any recent fever or trauma. Examination reveals an erythematous, swollen left knee with an effusion and limited range of motion. There is no warmth or tenderness in other joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein, while a knee aspiration reveals clear fluid with moderate leukocytes, predominantly neutrophils, and no crystals. Rheumatoid factor is negative, and antinuclear antibodies are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and unintended weight loss of approximately 10 pounds. She also reports occasional dizziness and shortness of breath on exertion. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. She denies any significant changes in her diet or exercise patterns. She works as a graphic designer and denies alcohol, tobacco, or drug use. Her family history is notable for hypothyroidism in her mother. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild tachycardia. Laboratory results show a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL (reference range: 12.0–16.0 g/dL), mean corpuscular volume of 72 fL (reference range: 80–100 fL), and low serum ferritin. The peripheral blood smear shows microcytic, hypochromic red blood cells. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of purpura on her lower extremities and joint pain. She also reports episodes of hematuria. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies and eczema. She denies recent infections or changes in medication. Her vital signs are normal, and physical examination reveals palpable purpura distributed over the legs and buttocks. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and urinalysis positive for red blood cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects over her head for the past two months. She reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in her fingers and wrists. She denies any recent viral illnesses or travel. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she does not take any medications regularly. On examination, she has symmetric proximal muscle weakness, particularly in the shoulders and hips, but full strength distally. Her skin appears normal without rashes or discoloration. She has tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints, but no swelling or deformities. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatine kinase level and normal complete blood count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of painful, erythematous nodules on her shins and joint pain, particularly in the knees and ankles. She works as a preschool teacher and reports feeling more fatigued than usual. She denies any recent infections or travel. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), blood pressure is 120/75 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. There are several 2- to 3-cm tender, erythematous nodules on both shins. Her knee and ankle joints are tender without swelling, erythema, or deformity. Laboratory studies reveal a leukocyte count of 11,200/mm3, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 60 mm/h, and a C-reactive protein level of 5.2 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Erythema nodosum secondary to a streptococcal infection

question: A 59-year-old woman comes to the clinic with recurrent episodes of painful swelling in her right great toe for the past year. The episodes typically resolve spontaneously within a few days. She describes the pain as severe, and it often wakes her from sleep. She denies any recent trauma to the affected area. On examination, her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 132/76 mm Hg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals a swollen, erythematous, and tender right first metatarsophalangeal joint. There are firm nodules overlying the joint. Laboratory tests show an elevated serum uric acid level. She has no history of skin rashes, oral ulcers, or photosensitivity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past 3 weeks. She reports that the symptoms began shortly after she recovered from a viral illness characterized by fever, sore throat, and nasal congestion. She denies joint swelling but notes some stiffness in the morning lasting about 15 minutes. She does not recall any recent trauma or unusual physical activity. On examination, she is afebrile and has tenderness over multiple muscle groups, but no significant joint abnormalities. Her vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Prescribe a short course of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
options.E: Schedule a follow-up appointment in 4 weeks

question:
A 55-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. She reports morning stiffness lasting about an hour, and the pain is most prominent in her hands and wrists bilaterally. She denies any recent trauma or new medications. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. There are no skin rashes or oral ulcers. Laboratory studies show:

| Laboratory Test | Result |
| --- | --- |
| Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) | 45 mm/h |
| C-reactive protein (CRP) | 12 mg/L |
| Rheumatoid factor (RF) | Positive |
| Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies | Positive |

X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old woman visits her primary care physician with complaints of weight gain despite a decreased appetite over the past 3 months. She also reports morning stiffness in her knees and wrists lasting more than an hour, as well as increased fatigue and facial puffiness, particularly around the eyes. She denies fever, night sweats, or recent travel. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and seasonal allergies, and she is currently taking lisinopril. On physical examination, her blood pressure is 132/78 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and BMI is 30 kg/m2. Notably, there is periorbital edema and mild bilateral knee swelling without warmth or erythema. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin at 12.0 g/dL, leukocyte count at 7,500/mm3, and ESR at 58 mm/hr. Metabolic panel reveals normal glucose but low sodium at 130 mEq/L. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling that began after a tennis match earlier today. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the anterior part of the knee. She denies any recent trauma or falls. Her medical history is significant for mild asthma, controlled with an inhaler, and she is otherwise healthy. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness over the right knee, with warmth and limited range of motion due to pain. No erythema is observed. Vital signs are normal. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of foot drop and difficulty walking over the past few weeks. She reports tripping frequently and has noticed a loss of sensation over the top of her right foot. She denies any recent trauma to her leg. Her medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with metformin. Her vital signs are stable. Neurological examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and reduced sensation over the dorsum of the foot. Tendon reflexes are normal except for an absent ankle jerk on the right side. The patient also reports mild joint pain in her hands, but she attributes this to aging. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened, especially after prolonged periods of sitting or standing. She reports no significant morning stiffness, but notes that the pain tends to improve slightly with activity. She denies any trauma, weight loss, or significant fatigue. Her past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Physical examination reveals pain on internal rotation of both hips. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and ESR of 25 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of skin tightening of her fingers and episodes of fingers turning white and then blue in cold weather for the past 6 months. She also reports joint pain, fatigue, and occasional difficulty swallowing. On examination, her skin appears thickened over her fingers and hands. Capillary nailfold microscopy reveals dilated loops. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain in her hips and lower back, which she has noticed over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as dull and aching, and it worsens in the evening. She also mentions feeling more fatigued than usual and has had to cut back on her gardening activities due to discomfort. She is postmenopausal and does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril, and she had a hip fracture 2 years ago following a minor fall. Her physical examination is notable for tenderness over the lumbar spine and hips, but her range of motion is preserved. Laboratory results reveal elevated levels of alkaline phosphatase, with normal calcium and phosphate levels. Her erythrocyte sedimentation rate is mildly elevated. An X-ray of the pelvis shows cortical thickening of the ilium and femur. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with severe back pain that began suddenly after lifting a heavy box. She describes the pain as sharp and localized to the lower thoracic region. She has a history of osteoporosis, for which she takes alendronate, and she has been on chronic glucocorticoid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed 15 years ago. She denies any recent trauma, fever, or neurological deficits. On examination, she has tenderness over the lower thoracic vertebrae but no swelling or erythema. Neurological examination is normal, and deep tendon reflexes are intact. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, but her erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated at 45 mm/hr. A lateral spine X-ray reveals a compression fracture at T10. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Order an MRI of the spine
options.E: Increase calcium and vitamin D supplementation

question:
A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 9-month history of bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened. She describes the pain as aching and states it is exacerbated by physical activity and relieved by rest. She denies any significant morning stiffness but mentions some discomfort lasting about 15 minutes after sitting for extended periods. Her medical history is significant for obesity, hypertension, and hypothyroidism. She takes levothyroxine and lisinopril regularly. On examination, there is tenderness upon palpation of the hips and reduced range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal a normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A hip radiograph shows mild joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation.

Which of the following is most consistent with this patient's presentation?

options.B: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with acute right wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while running in the park. She immediately felt a sharp pain in her wrist and has since been unable to move it without significant discomfort. Her medical history is notable for hypertension and mild osteoarthritis. She denies any recent fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the distal radius, with limited range of motion due to pain. X-rays show a distal radius fracture with minimal displacement. Blood tests reveal a normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers are only slightly elevated. What is the next best step in managing this patient?

options.A: Immediate open reduction and internal fixation
options.B: Application of a short arm splint and referral to orthopedics
options.D: Evaluate for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.E: Consult with a rheumatologist for possible autoimmune disease
options.F: Conduct MRI of wrist to assess for ligamentous injury
options.G: Initiate physical therapy as first-line treatment

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past six months. She also reports feeling cold more often, experiencing dry skin, and noticing some hair thinning. She denies any recent fever, night sweats, or significant joint pain. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she does not take any long-term medications. Physical examination reveals a diffusely enlarged thyroid gland without nodules and a heart rate of 60/min. Laboratory tests show a low serum T4 level and elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels. Anti-thyroid peroxidase antibodies are present. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.F: Refer to an endocrinologist for further evaluation
options.G: Perform fine-needle aspiration of the thyroid

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of numbness and tingling in her hands and feet. She describes a burning sensation that worsens at night and occasionally disrupts her sleep. She has also noticed some occasional joint pain and stiffness in her fingers, which she attributes to long hours at her desk job. She denies any recent injuries or new medications but admits to a 10-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, poorly controlled due to inconsistent medication use. She also reports fatigue and occasional headaches. Her mother had rheumatoid arthritis, and her father had type 1 diabetes. On examination, she has decreased pinprick and vibration sensation in a stocking-glove distribution. Her hemoglobin A1c is 9.5%. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?

A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of persistent swelling in her neck and axillary regions. She reports experiencing night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and generalized fatigue during this time. She has no known allergies and is not on any medications. Her medical history includes occasional migraines, for which she takes ibuprofen as needed. She works as a graphic designer and denies tobacco or illicit drug use, but she drinks alcohol socially. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis. On physical examination, there are multiple palpable lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary areas, which are nontender and firm. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.6°C (99.7°F), blood pressure of 122/76 mm Hg, pulse of 82/min, and respirations of 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal a mild normocytic anemia and elevated lactate dehydrogenase levels. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?

options.B: Schedule a CT scan of the chest
options.D: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.E: Conduct a purified protein derivative (PPD) skin test
options.G: Refer to hematology for further evaluation

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful nodules on the front of her legs and joint pain for the last week. She describes the nodules as red and tender, and they have gradually increased in size. Additionally, she reports morning stiffness in her knees and ankles that improves throughout the day. She denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for seasonal allergies, and she takes loratadine as needed. She works as a nurse and is up to date with her vaccinations. On physical examination, there are multiple erythematous, raised, tender nodules on the anterior shins bilaterally. There is mild swelling of the right ankle. Laboratory studies show a normal white blood cell count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal renal function. A chest X-ray is ordered and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. She also reports occasional joint pain in her hands but denies any swelling or morning stiffness. Additionally, she has noticed a persistent swelling in the area around her jaw. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. She is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol socially. On examination, there is bilateral parotid gland enlargement, and her oral mucosa appears dry. Schirmer's test is positive. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 25 mm/h. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while playing soccer. She reports immediate pain and swelling in her right wrist, which has been difficult to move since the injury. She denies any previous trauma to the wrist. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the distal radius and limited range of motion due to pain. X-ray of the wrist shows a fracture of the distal radius with possible dorsal angulation. Her vital signs are stable. Which of the following is the next best step in management?

options.B: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) for pain control
options.C: Surgical referral for potential open reduction and internal fixation

question: A 37-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past 2 months, periorbital puffiness, and morning joint stiffness. She describes the joint stiffness as most prominent in her hands and knees, lasting for about 30 minutes after waking up. She denies any skin rashes, fevers, or significant changes in appetite. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies, and she takes no regular medications aside from occasional antihistamines. On examination, her blood pressure is 118/76 mm Hg, pulse is 78/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. She has noticeable periorbital edema and mild pitting edema in her lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old woman presents to the clinic with worsening pain and stiffness in her hands and wrists over the past two years. She reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, and her symptoms have been progressively getting worse. She has a family history of autoimmune diseases, including a sister with lupus. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Her laboratory findings are significant for a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. A hand X-ray shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with these findings?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent nasal congestion, facial pressure, and occasional nosebleeds. She also reports a dry cough and fatigue for the past month. Additionally, she mentions experiencing joint stiffness in the morning lasting about an hour, but this symptom has improved recently. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, and she has no known allergies. Physical examination reveals bilateral nasal crusting and tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. Auscultation of the lungs reveals rales in the right lower lobe. Laboratory results show:

Hemoglobin: 11.5 g/dL
Hematocrit: 35%
Leukocyte count: 10,000/mm^3

Serum:
Na+: 138 mEq/L
K+: 4.2 mEq/L
Cl-: 102 mEq/L
BUN: 28 mg/dL
Creatinine: 1.5 mg/dL

Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. A chest CT shows bilateral ground-glass opacities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of fatigue, occasional shortness of breath, and joint pain in her wrists and knees. She describes the joint pain as persistent and worsening in the morning. She also notes easy bruising and frequent nosebleeds over the past few weeks. She denies any recent infections, fever, weight loss, or night sweats. Past medical history is significant for a resolved Epstein-Barr virus infection as a teenager. She is a non-smoker and does not consume alcohol. Family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals pallor, mild hepatosplenomegaly, and tenderness of the affected joints without significant swelling. Laboratory results show hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, leukocytes 2,900/mm3, platelets 90,000/mm3, and elevated ESR. A peripheral blood smear exhibits pancytopenia without significant abnormalities in red blood cell morphology. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old female presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, low-grade fevers, and swelling over the knuckles and elbows. She reports that the nodules on her elbows have been present for several months and are increasingly tender. She also notes occasional mild joint pain but denies any significant morning stiffness. Her medical history is unremarkable except for a recent episode of bronchitis. Vital signs show: temperature 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure 125/82 mmHg, pulse 78/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination reveals firm, non-mobile nodules on the extensor surfaces of her elbows and knuckles. Laboratory tests show a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal complete blood count (CBC). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.C: Measurement of serum uric acid levels
options.G: Skin biopsy for suspected dermal pathology
options.H: Referral to a dermatologist for further evaluation

question: A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a history of skin changes and recurrent episodes of leg swelling over the past year. She describes a purple, lace-like pattern on her thighs and forearms that becomes more pronounced in cold weather. She has experienced two episodes of deep vein thrombosis in the past six months, for which she is on warfarin. She denies joint pain or morning stiffness. Her family history is significant for autoimmune conditions. On examination, she has livedo reticularis on her lower extremities and no synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count and liver function tests, with elevated D-dimer levels. Anticardiolipin antibodies and lupus anticoagulant are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth for the past few months. She also notes swelling and discomfort in her cheeks, which has been particularly noticeable in the mornings. She reports occasional joint stiffness and fatigue, but no significant joint pain or swelling. Her medical history is notable for seasonal allergies, and she takes over-the-counter antihistamines as needed. On examination, her vitals are stable, but there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Her oral mucosa is dry, and Schirmer's test is positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis of this patient's condition?

options.E: Conduct CT scan of the head

question: A 29-year-old woman presents to the neurology clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the last 6 months. She reports bilateral leg weakness and frequent stumbling. Additionally, she has noticed some numbness in her feet and lower legs. She denies any back pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or recent illnesses. She has fatigue that has been worsening over the past year. Her past medical history is notable for frequent joint pains and morning stiffness, especially in her hands and wrists, which she attributes to her busy work schedule as a writer. On examination, she has a spastic gait and decreased strength in both lower extremities. Her hand joints show mild swelling but full range of motion. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk in her legs, and Babinski sign is present bilaterally. MRI of the brain shows periventricular white matter lesions. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count, electrolytes, and liver function tests, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 3 months. She reports feeling increasingly tired even after minor activities and has lost about 8 pounds without trying. She also mentions occasional joint pain in her hands and wrists, but denies any morning stiffness. Her medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, she appears pale and her conjunctiva are noticeably anemic. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations are unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal a microcytic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 9.8 g/dL and elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 61-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of worsening bone pain and fatigue over the past several months. She mentions that the pain is mostly in her hips and lower back. She is postmenopausal and has not experienced any recent trauma. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin respectively. On examination, there is tenderness over the pelvic area but no joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests reveal elevated alkaline phosphatase, normal calcium, and normal phosphate levels. A bone scan shows increased uptake in the pelvis and lumbar spine. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her right big toe over the past 6 months. The episodes last a few days and resolve spontaneously, but have gradually become more frequent and intense. She noticed a firm, nodular swelling near the joint. She denies fever, chills, or significant weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension and chronic kidney disease. She takes lisinopril and low-dose aspirin daily. She occasionally uses ibuprofen for pain relief. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, her temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), pulse is 78/min, and blood pressure is 138/84 mm Hg. The right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, swollen, and tender with a palpable tophus. Serum uric acid level is elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 34-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain. She mentions that the pain is present in her neck, back, and shoulders, often described as a dull ache. Additionally, she reports significant fatigue and non-restorative sleep despite sleeping for 8 hours per night. She denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness lasting longer than 30 minutes. Her medical history is significant for depression, for which she takes sertraline. Physical examination reveals multiple tender points across her body, but no synovitis or joint deformities are evident. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers including erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). She asks about potential diagnoses. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's condition?

options.A: Measurement of serum creatine kinase levels

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of headaches, fatigue, and swelling of the ankles. She reports that her blood pressure has been elevated at home despite adherence to a low-sodium diet. She has no significant past medical history but mentions some morning joint stiffness that improves by midday. Family history is significant for hypertension in her mother and systemic lupus erythematosus in her sister. On examination, her blood pressure is 160/100 mm Hg, and there is bilateral ankle edema. Laboratory tests reveal creatinine of 2.0 mg/dL, elevated blood urea nitrogen, and a urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio of 300 mg/g. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that appeared after she returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States two weeks ago. She reports a fever, fatigue, and describes the rash as expanding in a circular pattern with a clear center, which appeared after a tick was removed from her thigh. Her joint pain is mainly in her knees and wrists. She denies any history of similar symptoms, and her medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, she appears tired, with tender and swollen wrists and knees, and a circular rash with central clearing on her thigh. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR but normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate step in management?

options.B: Prescribe methotrexate for presumed rheumatoid arthritis
options.D: Order ANA testing to evaluate for autoimmune disease
options.E: Refer to rheumatology for further evaluation

question: A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to joint pain and swelling in her hands and wrists for the past year. She reports stiffness in her joints lasting over an hour each morning. She has noticed lumps on her elbows that are not painful but have grown slowly. She denies any recent trauma or fever. Her medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which she takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. She does not smoke or consume alcohol. Her mother has a history of rheumatoid arthritis. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 125/80 mm Hg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate is 65 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of recurrent episodes of joint pain in her right big toe over the past year. She describes the pain as severe, lasting for several days each time, and it often wakes her from sleep. Her past medical history is significant for hypertension and obesity. On examination, she has noticeable swelling and redness over the first metatarsophalangeal joint with a firm nodule that is painful to touch. Her temperature is 37.3°C (99.1°F), blood pressure is 148/92 mm Hg, and heart rate is 88/min. Laboratory findings reveal an elevated white blood cell count and increased serum uric acid level. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Aspiration of the joint for synovial fluid analysis

question: A 38-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and joint pain over the past few months. She describes the joint pain as symmetric and affecting her wrists and knees, particularly worse in the morning. She also reports feeling increasingly tired and has noticed occasional bruising and paleness. She denies any recent infections or changes in weight or appetite. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild swelling of the wrists without any deformities. Laboratory studies show pancytopenia with a white blood cell count of 2,900/mm^3, hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, and platelet count of 90,000/mm^3. Additional tests reveal normal renal and liver function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 42-year-old woman presents to your clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain over the past 3 months. She also notes swelling around her eyes, particularly noticeable in the mornings, and stiffness in her joints that improves as the day progresses. She denies any changes in diet or exercise routine. Medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. She reports feeling more tired than usual and has difficulty concentrating at work. Her blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 74/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Joint examination shows minimal tenderness without significant synovitis. Laboratory results show a normal complete blood count, but an elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.F: Order an MRI of the brain

question: A 56-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of fatigue and joint pain. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and pain in her wrists, knees, and fingers. Her past medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she is taking levothyroxine. She denies any recent travel or changes in medication. Physical examination reveals bilateral swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman visits the clinic due to generalized body aches and fatigue that began two weeks ago after a short-lived upper respiratory tract infection. The patient reports that her symptoms have persisted despite over-the-counter analgesics. She denies any significant morning stiffness or joint swelling. Medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She denies fever, weight loss, or skin changes. On examination, she appears fatigued but in no acute distress. There is no synovitis or joint deformity on examination, but tenderness is noted diffusely over the muscles of the upper and lower extremities. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, ESR of 15 mm/hr, and C-reactive protein of 1.0 mg/L. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past week. She mentions recovering from a viral illness two weeks ago that involved a sore throat and low-grade fever. Since then, she has been experiencing generalized muscle and joint discomfort, particularly in her shoulders and hips. She denies any joint swelling, redness, or warmth. Her medical history is unremarkable, and she is not taking any medications. On examination, her vital signs are within normal limits, and there is no evidence of joint inflammation. Laboratory studies show a mildly elevated ESR and CRP. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of persistent fatigue, fever, and night sweats. She reports swollen lymph nodes in her neck and underarms that have not subsided since they appeared 2 months ago. She also has experienced unintentional weight loss of approximately 10 pounds over the past 3 months. Her medical history includes seasonal allergies. She denies any recent travel, exposure to tuberculosis, or use of illicit drugs. On physical examination, there are multiple enlarged, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Chest X-ray shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent nasal congestion, recurrent nosebleeds, and a chronic cough that has lasted for several months. She also reports increasing fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 3 kg over the past 2 months. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent travel or exposure to sick contacts. On physical examination, she appears pale and has tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. There are no nasal polyps, but mild bilateral nasal crusting is noted. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory investigations show hemoglobin 10.5 g/dL, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A chest x-ray reveals bilateral patchy infiltrates. Urinalysis demonstrates hematuria and red cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back pain and stiffness that have been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. She notes that the pain is particularly severe in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. She denies any recent trauma or injury to the back. Her medical history is significant for occasional episodes of anterior uveitis. She reports no significant weight loss, but mentions feeling fatigued lately. Her temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 118/74 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, tenderness is noted over the sacroiliac joints with limited lumbar flexion. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein levels, but rheumatoid factor is negative. A pelvic X-ray shows sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of joint pain and stiffness that has persisted for the past 6 months. She reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the mornings, lasting over an hour, and primarily affects her hands and wrists. Her past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with limited range of motion. Laboratory studies show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A hand X-ray reveals joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of diffuse musculoskeletal pain for the past 6 months. She notes that her pain is persistent and widespread, affecting her neck, shoulders, back, and hips. She also reports severe fatigue and non-restorative sleep, which have significantly impacted her daily activities. She denies any joint swelling or erythema. Her medical history is notable for anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. She has tried over-the-counter pain relievers with minimal relief. On physical examination, multiple tender points are noted, but there is no synovitis or swelling. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Order a sleep study for obstructive sleep apnea

question: A 55-year-old woman presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of severe pain and swelling in her right big toe over the past two years. The pain usually resolves within days but has been increasing in frequency. She reports that the episodes are often triggered by red wine and meat consumption. She has noticed firm, yellowish nodules on her ear and along her fingers. Her vital signs are stable, and she denies any fever, rash, or systemic symptoms. On examination, she has a swollen, erythematous, and tender right first metatarsophalangeal joint with visible tophi. Laboratory studies reveal elevated serum uric acid levels. An X-ray shows erosions in the affected joint. Considering her presentation, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Perform arthrocentesis of the affected joint
options.E: Consider rheumatoid arthritis as a differential diagnosis

question: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 1-week history of fever, fatigue, and joint pain. She reports that her knees and wrists are painful and swollen, and she has noticed a red rash on her arms and legs. She mentions a recent hiking trip in a wooded area 3 weeks ago, where she recalls a tick bite on her leg. She denies any significant past medical history and is not currently taking any medications. On examination, her temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F), blood pressure is 120/75 mmHg, pulse is 90/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical exam reveals erythematous macular rash on the extremities and swelling of the knees and wrists. Laboratory findings show mild leukocytosis, elevated ESR, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to her primary care clinic with complaints of a facial rash and joint pain. She notes that the rash is most prominent after sun exposure and is accompanied by swelling and pain in multiple joints, especially in the hands and knees. She reports fatigue and occasional fevers but denies any recent infections or travel. Her blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg, heart rate is 85/min, and temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F). Physical examination reveals a butterfly-shaped rash across her cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory workup shows mild anemia, leukopenia, and a positive antinuclear antibody test. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 58-year-old woman presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past 6 months. She describes the pain as a dull ache that intensifies with movement and is relieved by rest. She denies any recent trauma or significant improvement with over-the-counter pain medication. Additionally, she reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes, as well as occasional fatigue. She has no history of psoriasis, inflammatory bowel disease, or uveitis. Her medical history is significant for hypertension and hypothyroidism, managed with amlodipine and levothyroxine, respectively. Physical examination reveals tenderness in the greater trochanteric region bilaterally, with a full range of motion in both hips. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and erythrocyte sedimentation rate within normal limits. X-rays of the hips reveal minimal joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 32-year-old woman who is 28 weeks pregnant presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back and pelvic pain that has progressively worsened over the past few weeks. She describes the pain as a dull ache that is exacerbated by prolonged standing or walking, and it occasionally radiates to her thighs. Her obstetric history includes two prior pregnancies with similar symptoms, but they were not as severe. She denies any swelling, morning stiffness, or joint deformities. Her medical history includes mild asthma, for which she uses an albuterol inhaler as needed. On examination, her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), pulse is 90/min, and blood pressure is 115/70 mm Hg. The abdomen is gravid and nontender, and there is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Lumbar spine palpation elicits mild tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated ESR. Urinalysis is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Advise use of a maternity support belt

question: A 36-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in her legs. She notes that climbing stairs has become increasingly challenging, and she occasionally stumbles. She reports some fatigue but denies any significant joint pain or swelling. She has a history of hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. On examination, her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 82/min, and blood pressure is 122/76 mm Hg. Neurological examination reveals 4/5 strength in the proximal muscles of the lower extremities, with intact sensation and reflexes. Laboratory tests show normal thyroid function and elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of progressive weakness in her shoulders and hips, making it difficult for her to climb stairs and lift objects overhead. She denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. Her past medical history is significant for seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals decreased muscle strength in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs. Her reflexes and sensation are intact. Laboratory tests show an elevated creatine kinase level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent swelling in her neck and armpit regions for the past two months. She also reports experiencing night sweats and unintended weight loss over the same period. On further questioning, she admits to feeling fatigued and occasionally having mild joint pain but denies fever or sore throat. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for seasonal allergies. Vital signs show a temperature of 37.1°C (98.8°F), blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, pulse of 78/min, and respirations of 14/min. On physical examination, multiple non-tender, rubbery lymph nodes are palpated in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. A chest radiograph is performed and shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old woman presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of chronic pain and swelling in her hands and wrists over the past 18 months. She describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and often feels fatigued. On examination, there is notable synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands, along with subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of her forearms. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of generalized bone pain and fatigue. She has difficulty walking long distances due to increasing discomfort in her legs. She reports no recent fever, weight loss, or changes in appetite. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, for which she takes lisinopril. Family history is unremarkable. Her physical examination reveals tenderness over the sternum and lower ribs, with no joint swelling or erythema observed. Laboratory studies show: Calcium 12.2 mg/dL, Phosphate 2.4 mg/dL, Parathyroid hormone 150 pg/mL, and Alk Phos 130 IU/L. Serum protein electrophoresis shows no monoclonal spike. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive weakness in her upper arms and thighs over the past 3 months. She has difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above her head. She also reports mild joint pain in her knees and wrists. Her medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals symmetrical proximal muscle weakness in both the upper and lower extremities. The patient has no skin rashes. Her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 75/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. Laboratory studies show elevated serum creatine kinase levels. Electromyography demonstrates myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with numbness and tingling in her feet that has progressively worsened over the past year. She describes a burning sensation and occasional sharp pains in her toes. She has a history of hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine and hypertension controlled with amlodipine. She denies any recent infections or new medications. On examination, she has decreased sensation to pinprick and vibration in her feet. Reflexes are reduced at the ankles but normal at the knees. Her hemoglobin A1c is 5.8%. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient?

options.E: Magnetic resonance imaging of the lumbar spine

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past two months. She reports feeling increasingly tired and experiencing pain in her wrists and knees, making daily activities challenging. Her medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. She denies any recent travel, fever, or weight loss. On examination, she appears pale and has petechiae on her lower legs. Her temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 102/min, blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.0 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 2,500/mm3, and a platelet count of 90,000/mm3. A bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellularity and a decreased number of hematopoietic stem cells. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 32-year-old woman in her third trimester of pregnancy presents to her obstetrician with complaints of persistent lower back and pelvic pain over the past month. She reports that the pain is worse at night and disrupts her sleep. Walking and standing for long periods exacerbate the discomfort, but sitting provides some relief. She denies any previous history of similar symptoms, joint swelling, or morning stiffness. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable except for mild anemia managed with iron supplements. On physical examination, her vital signs are within normal limits. There is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and lower lumbar region, but no swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests reveal a mild elevation in the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Refer to physical therapy for pregnancy-safe exercises
options.D: Initiate opioid therapy for pain relief
options.G: Consult a rheumatologist for further evaluation
options.H: Prescribe acetaminophen as needed for pain

question: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a complaint of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that has progressively worsened over the past year. She experiences significant discomfort in the morning, which gradually improves with movement as the day progresses. She reports feeling fatigued and has had intermittent episodes of mild fever. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. Her medical history is notable for hypothyroidism, for which she takes levothyroxine. Her family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Physical examination reveals decreased lumbar spine flexibility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints bilaterally. Laboratory investigations show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.A: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the lumbar spine

question: A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on her shins and joint pain that has been persistent for the past two weeks. She also describes feeling fatigued and experiencing mild fever. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. She reports no recent travel or known sick contacts. On examination, her temperature is 37.8°C (100.0°F), blood pressure is 112/72 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Physical examination reveals tender erythematous nodules on her lower legs and mild swelling of the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but other routine blood tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness, primarily affecting her wrists, metacarpophalangeal, and proximal interphalangeal joints. She reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and lasts for at least an hour before easing with movement. She also notes fatigue and unintentional weight loss. She has no history of recent infections or travel. Her mother has a history of an autoimmune disease affecting the joints. Physical examination reveals synovial thickening and tenderness in the affected joints, with decreased grip strength. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels, but her rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman comes to the physician because of progressive tightness and swelling of the skin on her fingers for the last 6 months. She also reports that her fingers turn white and blue in cold weather or stress, which is often painful. She feels generally fatigued and has occasional difficulty swallowing solid foods. She denies any significant joint pain. Her medical history is significant for well-controlled hypothyroidism. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is thickening and tightening of the skin on the fingers, with some shiny areas. Capillary refill is delayed in the fingers. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.G: Doppler ultrasound of the digital arteries

question: A 42-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening and discoloration of her fingers over the past 6 months. She describes episodes where her fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon rewarming. She also reports mild joint pain, fatigue, and occasional difficulty swallowing. Her medical history is significant for hypothyroidism. Physical examination reveals thickened skin over the fingers and hands, and capillary dilation at the fingertips. Laboratory tests show a positive ANA titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old woman visits her doctor due to persistent dryness of the eyes and mouth for the past six months. She has also experienced mild swelling below her ears and occasional joint discomfort. She reports an increase in dental cavities despite regular dental hygiene and mentions using artificial tears multiple times a day. Her medical history includes hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine. Her temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), pulse is 75/min, and blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg. On examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Schirmer's test shows decreased tear production. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive bone pain, primarily in her hips and lower back, over the past few months. She describes the pain as dull and constant, with occasional exacerbations that affect her mobility. She also reports fatigue and mild joint stiffness, especially in the mornings. She has a history of hypertension, which is well-managed with lisinopril, and no other significant medical conditions. Her family history includes a sister with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, she appears slightly pale, and there is tenderness upon palpation over the lumbar spine and pelvis. Her laboratory results reveal a serum calcium level of 11.3 mg/dL, phosphate 2.5 mg/dL, and parathyroid hormone (PTH) level of 85 pg/mL. A bone density scan shows generalized osteopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Repeat serum calcium and phosphate levels
options.C: Perform a 24-hour urinary calcium excretion test
options.D: Refer for surgical evaluation of hyperparathyroidism
options.G: Order a bone scan to rule out metastatic disease

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of joint pain in her hands and wrists. She describes the pain as a persistent ache that is worse in the morning and improves slightly with movement as the day progresses. She also reports stiffness lasting more than an hour upon waking. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, as well as the wrists. There are subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies are positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening over her fingers and hands over the past 6 months, along with episodes of fingers turning blue and white when exposed to cold. She also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue but has no morning stiffness. She denies fever, weight loss, or oral ulcers. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals thickened, shiny skin over the fingers and telangiectasia on the face. Her nailfold capillaries show abnormally dilated loops. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody with a titer of 1:320. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old woman comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and joint pain over the past 8 months. She reports stiffness in her hands and wrists, which lasts for more than an hour every morning. She also mentions intermittent swelling of her joints and pain that disrupts her daily activities. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normocytic, normochromic anemia with a hemoglobin level of 11.0 g/dL and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 50 mm/hr. A serological test reveals positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman visits the clinic due to a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure, joint pain, and fatigue lasting for the past month. She reports that the joint pain is particularly severe in the morning and improves slightly throughout the day. She denies any recent illnesses, weight changes, or new medications. Her medical history is notable only for occasional migraines. On examination, she has a well-demarcated, erythematous rash over her cheeks and nasal bridge, sparing the nasolabial folds, along with tenderness and mild swelling of the wrists and knees. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the diagnosis of this patient's condition?

A 28-year-old woman comes to the clinic complaining of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash on her face that worsens with sun exposure. She reports that her symptoms have been present for several weeks, and the joint pain affects her wrists and fingers bilaterally. She also mentions experiencing occasional low-grade fevers and unintentional weight loss of 5 pounds over the past month. She denies any recent infections. On examination, there is an erythematous rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Joint examination shows tenderness and mild swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, but no deformities are present. Laboratory studies show anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a two-week history of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and low-grade fevers. Two weeks ago, she had a self-limited upper respiratory tract infection. Since then, she reports generalized muscle and joint pain predominantly affecting her shoulders, hips, and knees, alongside a feeling of considerable fatigue. She denies any significant swelling or morning stiffness in her joints. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. Her temperature is 37.8°C (100°F), pulse is 92/min, blood pressure is 118/72 mm Hg, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Physical examination is notable for mild tenderness to palpation over the bilateral shoulders and hips but no visible joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.0 g/dL
Leukocyte count 6,200/mm3
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 38 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 12 mg/L
Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient's current condition?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a two-month history of painful joints, primarily affecting her fingers and knees. She also reports red, scaly rashes on her face that worsen with sun exposure and have been present for about three weeks. She denies any fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Her medical history is significant for migraines, for which she takes sumatriptan as needed. On examination, she has several oral ulcers and a notable malar rash. Her joints are warm and tender, but there is no visible swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) at 1:320, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal complete blood count and liver function tests.

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in her evaluation?

options.A: Skin biopsy of the facial rash
options.C: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibody testing
options.G: Synovial fluid analysis from affected joints

question:
A 38-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to persistent fatigue and weight gain over the past 6 months. She reports feeling cold more often than usual and has noticed her skin is dry and her hair is thinning. She also mentions occasional joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning. She has no significant past medical history and takes a daily multivitamin. Her mother has rheumatoid arthritis, and her father has hypertension. On examination, her temperature is 36.4°C (97.5°F), pulse is 58/min, blood pressure is 120/78 mm Hg, and BMI is 27 kg/m2. Physical examination reveals dry skin and brittle hair. Laboratory studies show:
TSH 8.4 mIU/L (normal: 0.4-4.5 mIU/L)
Free T4 0.6 ng/dL (normal: 0.7-1.9 ng/dL)
Anti-TPO antibodies positive
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 52-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg. She also reports experiencing fatigue and generalized joint pain. She has noticed swelling under her jaw and in her armpits. Her past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional headaches. On physical examination, she has fever at 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure 118/76 mmHg, pulse 92/min, and notable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal anemia with hemoglobin at 10.5 g/dL, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 72 mm/hr, and normal liver function tests. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain that has persisted for over 6 months. She states that the pain is worse in the morning, accompanied by stiffness that improves with activity throughout the day. She denies any recent injuries but reports occasional joint swelling and fatigue. She has no significant medical history and takes ibuprofen as needed for pain. She works as an office manager and is a non-smoker. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory findings are significant for an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). HLA-B27 antigen testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

options.F: Sacroiliitis due to inflammatory bowel disease

question: A 52-year-old woman presents to the clinic with pain and swelling in her right big toe that began 2 days ago. She reports having similar episodes over the past year, each lasting about a week and resolving spontaneously. She notes that this pain is excruciating and often wakes her from sleep. She denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. Her medical history includes hypertension, for which she takes hydrochlorothiazide. She consumes alcohol occasionally and her diet is rich in red meat and seafood. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, warm, and tender with decreased range of motion. There are several small, firm nodules over the helix of her ear. Laboratory studies show a serum uric acid level of 9.5 mg/dL. Which of the following conditions is most indicated by the presence of these symptoms?

A 35-year-old woman presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fevers for the past month. She reports temperatures occasionally reaching 102°F (38.9°C) and feels fatigued. She describes occasional night sweats and has had some unintended weight loss. She denies any recent travel, sick contacts, or new medications. On review of systems, she mentions occasional joint stiffness in the morning affecting her hands and knees, which subsides by midday. Her vital signs include temperature 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure 115/75 mmHg, pulse 88/min, and respirations 16/min. Physical examination shows mild pallor, but she is otherwise in no acute distress. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are negative. A chest X-ray is performed and is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition?

A 28-year-old female presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and recent genital ulcers. She has experienced these symptoms intermittently for the past year. She also reports joint pain in her knees and ankles, particularly in the morning, and occasional fatigue. She denies any significant weight loss or fever. Her medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. On examination, multiple shallow ulcers are noted on the oral mucosa, and there is evidence of erythematous papules in the genital area. No synovitis is observed, and her skin shows no rashes. Laboratory tests reveal elevated ESR and CRP levels, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Given these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the past three months. She also describes generalized weakness and occasional dizziness. Her past medical history is unremarkable. On further questioning, she mentions experiencing mild joint stiffness in the mornings that improves throughout the day. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory investigations show a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 75 fL, and low serum ferritin levels. A peripheral blood smear shows microcytic, hypochromic red blood cells. Which of the following is the most likely next step in management?
